Now classify them by what the research actually IS:

Categories:
- basic_research: Understanding mechanisms, pathways, disease processes (knowledge output)
- biotools: Developing research tools, assays, methods, platforms (tool output)
- therapeutics: Developing drugs, treatments, therapies (treatment output)
- diagnostics: Developing clinical tests, biomarker panels (diagnostic test output)
- medical_device: Developing physical devices for patients (device output)
- digital_health: Patient-facing software, apps, clinical decision support
- other: Health services, behavioral interventions, epidemiology

org_name: CHILDREN'S HOSP OF PHILADELPHIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Despite intensive therapy, patients with high-risk neuroblastoma frequently suffer from relapses that prove fatal. To prevent and treat relapse, we need a better understanding of how to target the populations of cancer cells that survive our current therapies. This proposal aims to target drug-resistant cancer cells by exploiting differences in how they sense inflammatory stimuli in order to enhance their susceptibility to immunotherapy.

application_id: 11380468
activity_code: F31
title: Investigating the potential of wastewater surveillance data to improve SARS-CoV-2 dynamical modeling and forecasting
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Mathematical models that help predict cases and hospitalizations of COVID-19 are essential for policy makers
and the public to plan public health interventions and prepare for outbreaks and typically rely on data about
cases. As data on cases has become less reliable and sparser, wastewater surveillance has emerged as a new
source of data for tracking infections; however, it is not clear yet how wastewater surveillance can be used to
improve modeling of transmission. This project will take advantage of a unique collaboration with the New York
State Wastewater Surveillance Network to find out how wastewater surveillance data may be used to improve
SARS-CoV-2 forecasting and protect the public health.

title: Understanding HIV testing, HIV care engagement, and the implementation of HIV prevention strategies for priority populations in the Southern United States
org_name: MEDICAL COLLEGE OF WISCONSIN
phr: This study directly addresses the goals of the Ending the HIV Epidemic (EHE) initiative by focusing on critical gaps in HIV prevention and care within rural communities of the Southern United States. application_id: 11129050
activity_code: K25
title: Super-resolved multiphoton microscopy with dual output ultrafast laser
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative Gene delivery using adeno-associated virus vectors has tremendous potential as treatment for congenital and acquired diseases of the nervous system. Understanding viral vector dynamics is key to optimizing drug design and has been hampered by a lack of means to resolve nanoscale viral particles in living tissues. This proposal seeks to develop efficient super-resolution imaging tools to improve imaging of adeno-associated viral vector trafficking in live animal models to advance understanding of viral vector bgene delivery.

phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Adolescent binge drinking has long-term consequences on brain function which are only beginning to be understood. These proposed experiments will characterize the impact of adolescent alcohol exposure on the ability of newborn neurons to integrate into brain circuitry and contribute to cognitive impairments in adulthood. application_id: 10929390
activity_code: F31
title: Targeted Death of Collagen1a1-Expressing Fibroblasts Reduces Silica-Induced Pulmonary Fibrosis
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: Project Narrative Pathogenic collagen-producing fibroblasts accumulate and persist at sites of fibrosis in progressive silica- associated pulmonary fibrosis. We hypothesize that targeted removal of collagen1a1-expressing fibroblasts through genetic ablation or sensitization to apoptosis after Bcl-2 knockdown will reduce fibrosis in a silica-induced progressive fibrosis mouse model. The results of our studies should provide insight into the dependence of fibrosis progression on Col1a1-expressing fibroblasts and development of novel therapeutic strategies to stop fibrosis progression that will improve the outcomes for patients with silica-induced fibrotic lung disease; as there are currently no FDA approved therapies.

application_id: 10985654
activity_code: K23
title: Investigation of within Household Transmission of Influenza in Low-Resource Settings
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Seasonal influenza causes 290,000-645,000 deaths annually and many of these deaths are in low-middle income countries especially in particularly vulnerable groups such as infants, pregnant women, and the elderly. However, how influenza transmits between members of a household is not well understood in low-middle income countries. This project hopes to fill the gap in knowledge of how influenza spreads within-households in low-middle income countries and has the potential to influence vaccination and mitigation measures recommended in these countries.

application_id: 11014334
activity_code: K99
title: The role of AHR in modulatingimmunotherapy response and adverse events in bladder cancer
org_name: VAN ANDEL RESEARCH INSTITUTE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE A subgroup of bladder cancer patients who are treated with immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICI) develop and succumb to hyperproliferative immune-related adverse events (HP-irAEs), for reasons that are unknown. I discovered a genetic and metabolic signature for HP-irAE that seems to pre-condition the tumor environment for HP-irAE before ICI treatment. By understanding how the genetic and metabolic pathway is differentially activated in tumor and immune cells within the tumor microenvironment, we will be able to better stratify bladder cancer patients before ICI treatment, and identify new opportunities for future drug discovery and precision medicine efforts to minimize HP-irAE in ICI-treated patients.

application_id: 11014343
activity_code: K01
title: Cortical mechanisms and modulation of somatosensation in older adults with foot sole somatosensory impairments
org_name: HEBREW REHABILITATION CENTER FOR AGED
phr: Project Narrative In older adults, diminished sensation of the legs and feet is highly prevalent and causes poor balance and reduced mobility. This type of sensation is not only dependent upon the receptors and nerves in the legs and feet, but also upon a complex central nervous system pathway that includes the cerebral cortex of the brain. This project will use a form of noninvasive brain stimulation called transcranial direct current stimulation (tDCS) to test whether increasing the excitability of the brain networks that process sensory feedback can augment foot sole sensation, balance, and mobility in older adults suffering from mild-to-moderate foot sole sensory impairments.

title: Structure and Function of an ADAM10-Tspan5 Complex
org_name: HARVARD MEDICAL SCHOOL
phr: Project Narrative A Disintegrin and Metalloprotease 10 (ADAM10) is an enzyme that catalyzes the ectodomain shedding of critical signaling and adhesion proteins including Notch, amyloid precursor protein, and cadherins, and its dysregulation is implicated in the development of varied pathologies including neurodegenerative diseases and several cancers. ADAM10 is tightly regulated by the C8 subclass of tetraspanin proteins (TspanC8s) according to mechanisms that are incompletely understood. title: CSHL Computational Genomics Course
org_name: COLD SPRING HARBOR LABORATORY
phr: NARRATIVE
The Computational Genomics, an 8 day course, is designed to meet the continuing need for

phr: Project Narrative
The Pathway-RRT seeks to recruit promising physician scientists into a longitudinal research program within
the University of Colorado School of Medicine Psychiatry residency. The proposed program will create
pathways for strong research productivity while still in residency and seeks to prepare our physician scientists
to compete for post-doctoral research positions and future grant funding. We anticipate preparing program
graduates to launch into academic careers thus increasing the workforce of physician scientists conducting

application_id: 10974203
activity_code: K08
title: Lung resident Treg suppression of Th2 resident memory T cells in allergicasthma
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MINNESOTA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE T helper type 2 tissue-resident memory cells (Th2-Trm) are key drivers of allergic asthma due to their persistence in the airways and amnestic responses upon local allergen re-exposure. FoxP3+ regulatory T cells (Tregs) are potent suppressors of allergic inflammation. This proposal explores the possibility that allergen- specific tissue resident (r) Tregs in the lung are particularly effective at suppressing allergic inflammation due to their positioning in a unique niche alongside Th2-Trm through shared chemokine receptor systems.

application_id: 10907712
activity_code: F30
title: Metabolomic signatures of inflammation and metabolic health in relation to colorectal cancer risk
org_name: HARVARD MEDICAL SCHOOL
phr: Project Narrative Despite advances in screening and identification of modifiable risk factors, the incidence and mortality of colorectal cancer (CRC) remains high, as does the incidence of obesity, which is a well-established risk factor for CRC. Lack of understanding of the biological pathways linking obesity and associated risk factors to CRC is a major limiting factor in the development of new strategies for CRC prevention. If successful, our project will identify novel pathways for CRC prevention and will establish methods for integrating metabolomic and other biomarker data and dimension-reduction approaches to identify pathways underlying risk factors for cancer and other chronic diseases.

application_id: 11244197
activity_code: F32
title: Investigating the impact of sickness on the maternal brain and behavior
org_name: NEW YORK UNIVERSITY SCHOOL OF MEDICINE
phr: Project Narrative
How mothers allocate caregiving efforts in the face of stress, including sickness, is critical for offspring survival
and ecological fitness. The hypothalamic neuropeptides oxytocin, corticotropin-releasing hormone, and
vasopressin facilitate caregiving behavior, stress response, and sickness behaviors, but their interplay during
inflammatory challenges remains unclear. This proposed research will determine how local hypothalamic
neuropeptide circuits in the maternal brain respond to sickness and shape caregiving behavior, advancing
knowledge of neuroimmune interactions and maternal health under stress.

title: Targeting spinal orexins to treat chronic pain
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Pain perception can be modulated via descending projections from the brain to the spinal cord, which includes orexin releasing neurons of the hypothalamus. title: Unraveling the role of molecular capacitors that obscure cryptic genetic variation in Astyanax mexicanus during the evolution of eye loss
org_name: HARVARD MEDICAL SCHOOL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The accumulation of hidden genetic variants over generations potentially represents a mechanism by which
complex multigenic traits can be suddenly revealed in response to environmental challenge. This proposal
aims to use the natural case of eye variation in the blind cavefish, Astyanax mexicanus, as a model for
vertebrate cryptic variation. By leveraging the strength of systematic analysis in the zebrafish, we will dissect
out the specific molecular mechanism by which the chaperone molecule, Hsp90, buffers cryptic variants that
contributed to the evolution of eye-loss in the Astyanax cavefish.

application_id: 11184163
activity_code: F31
title: Input specific adaptations to glutamatergic strength and plasticity on PFC PV-INs following ethanol drinking
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: Project Narrative
Although both men and women are affected by alcohol use disorder (AUD), women often progress through
AUD disease milestones faster and experience more severe health consequences than men; however, little is
known about the synaptic and molecular mechanism driving these sex-differences. The sexually dimorphic
prefrontal cortex (PFC) integrates excitatory glutamatergic inputs from subcortical structures to modulate
cortical excitation, yet the mechanism by which ethanol alters these inputs remains unclear. This proposal
seeks to investigate how ethanol alters glutamatergic strength and plasticity at discrete excitatory inputs to the
PFC in female and male mice.

application_id: 11244708
activity_code: F31
title: Defining a Role for Atypical B Cells in RV-Induced Exacerbations of Allergic Asthma
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF VIRGINIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Exacerbations of asthma caused by rhinovirus present an enormous health and economic burden to society;
however, little is known about how the underlying immune events that contribute to chronic lung inflammation,
and the role of B cells in particular is understudied. This project will leverage novel single-cell analytical methods
and a human experimental infection model to resolve how pro-inflammatory functions of virus-specific B cells
contribute to the severity of asthma. The knowledge gained will add a new dimension to understanding virus-
induced asthma and identify new cellular and molecular targets for treatment.

application_id: 10764331
activity_code: K00
title: Evaluating the role of protein self-assembly in the innate immune system
org_name: NORTHWESTERN UNIVERSITY
phr: Aging in humans leads to a chronic inflammatory state, known as inflammaging, which increases the risk for age-related diseases such as type 2 diabetes, atherosclerosis, and Alzheimer's disease. This proposal applies novel approaches to identify a potential molecular mechanism of inflammaging and the associated impairment in blood cell differentiation, while illuminating the physiological roles of protein self-assemblies in innate immune signaling. The insights that emerge from this research will advance our understanding of aging from a fundamental molecular perspective and potentially illuminate new opportunities to combat age- and disease-associated autoinflammation.

application_id: 11077341
activity_code: R25
title: EvolvingSTEM: a classroom research curriculum in microbiology that helps students see themselves as scientists
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: The EvolvingSTEM program will use a research-education partnership to educate high school introductory biology students about evolutionary biology, microbiology, and their impact on human health using authentic experimentation and 21st century bioinformatic skills. Our program increases understanding of these topics and their connection to biomedical advances and public health. Our program will improve design and implementation of research experiences that involve high school students in research methods and concepts that are rarely available in conventional classroom activities, and will determine which interventions (e.g., near-peer mentorship, student-driven inquiry) lead to sustained engagement in STEM and pursuit of STEM-related careers.

application_id: 11051872
activity_code: F31
title: Hypertensive Disorders of Pregnancy – The Black/White Disparity
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MIAMI SCHOOL OF MEDICINE
phr: Hypertensive disorders of pregnancy (HDP) are a leading contributor to maternal mortality in the US, and Black women are three to four times more likely than White women to die from HDP. This study addresses an urgent public health issue by investigating individual, interpersonal, and neighborhood factors that may contribute to differences in HDP outcomes. Results from this research will help guide the development of targeted interventions to improve maternal health, particularly among Black women.

application_id: 10995575
activity_code: F31
title: The Role of Platelet-Derived Transforming Growth Factor Beta in Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CINCINNATI
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA) is characterized by progressive deterioration of the aortic wall and catastrophic aortic failure resulting in sudden death in >80% of acute ruptures leading to the death of at least 15,000 Americans every year. Unfortunately, there are no pharmacological treatments available for the prevention of AAA progression and the only option for these patients is surgical intervention. Our proposal seeks to evaluate platelets as the source of protective transforming growth factor β (TGFβ) and adventitial fibroblasts as the target cell type mediating this protection.

application_id: 10791927
activity_code: K01
title: Microstructure and connectivity modeling from the cortex to the spinal cord in Multiple Sclerosis
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY MEDICAL CENTER
phr: NARRATIVE The proposed research aims to unify and improve simultaneous diffusion-based magnetic resonance imaging of the brain and spinal cord in order to study the complete motor pathways in vivo and to study their involvement in patients with multiple sclerosis. Previously, the brain and spinal cord have been studied in isolation due to challenges associated with long image acquisitions, different demands on resolution and acquisition paradigms, tissue modeling, and connectivity analysis. The proposed studies will provide a basis for understanding the role of the motor pathways in normal and damaged white matter microstructure in multiple sclerosis, and provide a foundation for further applications in diseases that involve both brain and spinal cord tissues.

application_id: 10929541
activity_code: R90
title: Interdisciplinary Training in Computational Neuroscience
org_name: CARNEGIE-MELLON UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Summary Rationale: To understand the many disorders of the brain it is necessary to grapple with its complexity. Increasingly large and complicated data sets are being collected, but the tools for analyzing and modeling the data are not yet available. More researchers trained in computational neuroscience are desperately needed. This project supports interdisciplinary graduate training programs in computational neuroscience (TPCN) at both Carnegie Mellon University (CMU) and the University of Pittsburgh (Pitt), and a summer school in computational neuroscience for undergraduates, which are available to students coming from colleges and universities throughout the United States. org_name: NORTHWESTERN UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Engineered cell therapies have successfully treated many blood cancers but have experienced challenges treating solid tumors due to issues involving specificity of the cells for the tumor and how the tumor suppresses these therapeutic cells. This project aims to address these challenges to improve the safety and potency of solid tumor cell therapy by engineering cells with a synthetic genetic circuit that allows them to sense modestly hypoxic tumors and express molecules to kill tumor cells and mitigate immunosuppression. application_id: 11082970
activity_code: F32
title: De novo design of photoacid-binding proteins to study proton dynamics in biological systems
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: Project narrative
Biological energy transduction is crucial to life and is primarily orchestrated through the coupling of proton and
electron movement. While electron transfer has been extensively studied, proton transfer has been more difficult
to study experimentally. Through protein design, we will design and express proteins that bind abiological
photoacid cofactors that will enable us to observe, for the first time, the generation and management of proton
currents in proteins, giving fundamental insight into proton conduction in biological systems and take the first
step toward the design of complex protein machinery.

title: Hematopoietic stem cell dysfunction in traumatic brain injury and Alzheimer's disease
org_name: CASE WESTERN RESERVE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Acute traumatic brain injury (TBI) is causally linked to chronic neurodegeneration and is the leading environmental risk factor for Alzheimer’s disease (AD), yet the reasons why are not understood. In this study, I will investigate the role of hematopoietic stem cell (HSC) dysfunction in mediating the transition from acute TBI to chronic neurodegeneration, and will examine HSC dysfunction in AD and TBI-accelerated AD. Through this research, I aim to contribute to our understanding of the pathophysiology of these neurodegenerative diseases and guide future treatments by unveiling novel therapeutic targets to counteract progressive neurodegeneration.

title: Enhancing motivation to quit smoking via self-help intervention targeted to cancer type: A pilot RCT
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF UTAH
phr: Project Narrative Smoking cessation is critically important for cancer patients, however smoking rates are very high in this population and many are unmotivated to quit, especially patients with cancers that are not typically associated with smoking. This study will pilot test a recently developed brief self-help intervention targeted by cancer type to increase smoking cessation motivation and will gather data on contextual factors to inform its future implementation in routine cancer care. The goal is to increase motivation to quit smoking, and ultimately smoking cessation itself among cancer patients.

application_id: 11014370
activity_code: K23
title: Addressing Weight Bias Internalization to Improve Adolescent Weight Management Outcomes
org_name: MIRIAM HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Weight bias internalization (WBI) has been associated with a number of negative physical and mental health consequences for adolescents with obesity, including negative weight-related outcomes. This study will develop, adapt, and pilot test a combined treatment for WBI and behavioral weight control for adolescents, designed to improve WBI and mechanisms of weight stigma induced weight gain including biological markers of stress and inflammation, and dysregulated eating behaviors. Addressing WBI as a particularly salient and impactful psychosocial stressor could improve behavioral weight control outcomes and improve physical and psychological stress for adolescents with obesity.

application_id: 10792866
activity_code: F31
title: Role and regulation of inflammasome in the intraocular pressure-induced injury of retinal ganglion cells
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MIAMI SCHOOL OF MEDICINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Current gaps in understanding the molecular mechanisms linking intraocular pressure to glaucoma pathology can be filled by utilizing a novel spiking ocular hypertension model. I will use this model to determine the role that endogenous neuroinflammation plays in dysfunction and death of retinal ganglion cells after exposure to repetitive mild spikes in intraocular pressure. The results of this project will improve our knowledge of early cellular responses triggered by IOP fluctuations associated with common lifestyle activities, and likely identify new targets for clinical treatment and slowing of glaucoma progression.

application_id: 10947071
activity_code: F31
title: Novel bioinformatics methods for integrative detection of structural variants from long-read sequencing
org_name: DREXEL UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Structural variants (SVs) are frequently associated with disease phenotypes and represent the largest and most complex source of human genomic variation. Limitations in sequencing technologies lead to challenges in the accurate discovery and characterization of SVs, but which can be overcome by taking an integrative approach leveraging the advantages of multiple platforms, as well as by utilizing more comprehensive reference genome representations such as the pangenome graph. In this proposal, I will be constructing an SV calling pipeline which is versatile to technological developments across sequencing platforms, and which can leverage the latest developments in graph genome reference representations to achieve high-performance results.

application_id: 11135917
activity_code: K99
title: Mechanism of TFEB-mediated proviral and antiviral autophagy during EV-D68 infection
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MARYLAND BALTIMORE
phr: Project Narrative
EV-D68 is a reemerging non-polio enterovirus and a significant course of acute respiratory
infection in children, with the potential to cause severe neurological conditions known as AFM.
For years, autophagy has been shown to promote EV-D68 infection. However, the same cellular
process can be antiviral depending on the timing of induction. This project will clarify how TFEB
regulates proviral and antiviral autophagy and inform the design of therapeutics against EV-D68.

application_id: 10995741
activity_code: F31
title: The Role of Kidney-derived APOM in S1P-mediated glomerular injury
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MIAMI SCHOOL OF MEDICINE
phr: Project Narrative Glomerular disease is responsible for a large part of the chronic kidney diseases cases around the world but have very few specific and effective treatments due to poorly understood pathogenic mechanisms. There is a large body of evidence that points to sphingolipid metabolism as a driving force in the progression of glomerular disease. This study aims to define the mechanisms by which alterations in sphingolipid balance and signaling lead to renal injury, thereby allowing us to target these pathways in search of better therapeutic options for patients.

org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA BERKELEY
phr: Project Narrative Sjögren’s Disease (SjD, previously known as Sjögren’s syndrome) is a chronic autoimmune disease that causes significant morbidity and predominantly affects women. While previous research has demonstrated that vitamin D deficiency is a known risk factor for several autoimmune conditions, the role of vitamin D in SjD is understudied. The objective of this F31 fellowship application is to identify and characterize vitamin D binding sites that are associated with SjD susceptibility, onset, and severity.

application_id: 11126798
activity_code: F31
title: Lineage and transcriptional analysis of sacral neural crest contribution to the enteric nervous system
org_name: CALIFORNIA INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY
phr: Project Narrative
 The enteric nervous system (ENS) is responsible for critical functions of the gut such as peristalsis, waste
elimination, and nutrient absorption. The sacral neural crest contributes to the post-umbilical ENS and disruption
of this innervation can result in life threatening developmental diseases. Further study of the sacral neural crest’s
development will provide better insight into this vital developmental population and will build a foundation for
better therapeutic treatment of diseases of the ENS.

org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: Despite chimeric antigen receptor (CAR) T cell immunotherapy mediating impressive response rates in B cell malignancies, fewer than 50% of patients experience long-term disease control. the sympathetic nervous system that may lead to cardiovascular problems. This proposal investigates arginine-
vasopressin modulation of the ventral midbrain and responses to psychosocial stressors that are risk factors
for developing cardiovascular disease. The proposed study is relevant to public health because it explores the
therapeutic potential of arginine-vasopressin, acting via the V1A receptor, in treating psychosocial stress-
associated cardiovascular conditions.

application_id: 11211873
activity_code: F32
title: Post-Translational Modification of Annexin A2 Mediates Surfactant Dysfunction During Injurious Mechanical Ventilation
org_name: OHIO STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS) causes respiratory failure, and patients frequently require life-
support with mechanical ventilation when affected with ARDS. Mechanical ventilation can be lifesaving but can
also cause an injury known as ventilator induced lung injury (VILI), which is at least partly due to surfactant
dysfunction. This proposal will deepen our understanding of the mechanisms that drive surfactant dysfunction
in VILI and identify novel therapeutic targets to improve the outcomes of patients with ARDS who require
mechanical ventilation.

application_id: 11115720
activity_code: T32
title: Infectious Diseases and Microbial Immunology Post-doctoral Training Program
org_name: WASHINGTON STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
The landmark Institute of Medicine report, “Emerging Infections: Microbial Threats to Health in the United
States," noted that roughly 60% of human diseases are caused by zoonotic pathogens. The importance
of this observation is exemplified by the current SARS-CoV2 pandemic, underscoring the urgent need for
understanding the biology of zoonotic pathogens, including mechanisms of persistence, evolution of
virulence, and epidemiology, to predict, prevent and control emerging infections This Infectious Diseases
and Microbial Immunology Post-doctoral Training Program specifically prepares a scientific workforce
prepared to and capable of addressing critical knowledge gaps in zoonotic infectious diseases and
antimicrobial resistance emergence.

application_id: 10996104
activity_code: F32
title: Optimizing Vasopressor Delivery in Early Sepsis
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE In its most severe form, sepsis causes hypotension that can lead to organ failure and death, a process that is often treated with vasopressor medications that raise blood pressure and increase blood flow to vital organs. This proposal will assess current practices for delivering vasopressors in sepsis and the association of these practices with patient outcomes and complications, and will identify factors that influence provider decisions about how to administer vasopressors. The ultimate goal of this proposal is to inform future clinical trials to optimize vasopressor delivery and improve sepsis outcomes.

application_id: 11295738
activity_code: DP2
title: Deciphering the crosstalk between bacteria and their mammalian hosts
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Patient responsiveness to cancer immunotherapy varies widely between individuals and is associated with the
bacterial inhabitants of the gut microbiome, but the mechanism is not understood. Recent evidence suggests
the cGAS-STING immune pathway also plays a crucial role in activating anticancer signaling and we have
found that gut-associated bacteria produce signaling molecules that activate with this pathway. This project
investigates how bacteria in the microbiome manipulate cGAS-STING signaling, thereby enabling future
development of better anticancer therapeutic agents.

application_id: 11216510
activity_code: F32
title: Defining the molecular mediators of cell morphogenesis in Trypanosoma cruzi
org_name: BROWN UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Chagas' disease is a severely neglected tropical disease caused by the protozoan parasite Trypanosoma cruzi,
for which treatments are inadequate and no vaccine exists. During its lifecycle, the parasite undergoes dramatic
morphological changes between the insect and mammalian hosts which likely contribute to its success as a
pathogen. In this proposal, I aim to describe the molecular drivers of one of these morphological transitions
known as metacyclogenesis by combining [transcriptomics and high resolution imaging techniques, which will
allow for the identification of targets for transmission blocking].

application_id: 11224076
activity_code: R25
title: Marine Neurotoxins:  Examining the impacts of a pedagogically focused RET model that explores the interplay of marine biotoxins, commercial shellfish aquaculture, and public health safety
org_name: COASTAL MARINE BIOLABS
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE/PUBLIC HEALTH RELEVANCE
This research education project will create novel opportunities for high school teachers and students to explore
the interplay of naturally occurring marine neurotoxins, environmental health science, and public health safety.
Although the research experience is interdisciplinary in scope, conceptual emphasis will be placed upon the
clinical syndromes associated with consumption of shellfish containing marine neurotoxins (e.g., amnesic and
paralytic shellfish poisoning) and effects of phytoplankton-derived marine biotoxins on neurotransmission. The
experience will also enable students to explore the efforts of public health agencies to mitigate the health risks
of marine neurotoxins.

Autistic adults experience adverse aging outcomes that manifest early and confer significant personal and public
health costs, including higher rates of chronic health deterioration, cognitive decline, and mortality. The proposed
research will leverage epigenetic markers that can detect deviations in cellular aging years before the onset of
overt declines, thus helping to identify autistic persons most at risk of poor outcomes and the factors contributing

autism coalesce to influence biological aging processes may ultimately inform preventive strategies to mitigate
subsequent health and longevity disparities by minimizing risk exposures and promoting identified ...

application_id: 11123088
activity_code: F31
title: Norepinephrine and second messenger encoding of temporal threat proximity
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 Survival requires the accurate prediction of threats at diverse proximity, and aberrant threat
computation processes underlie psychiatric disorders such as post-traumatic stress disorders. However, the
neural substrates of determining temporal proximity of incoming threats have not been well determined. The
proposed work seems to elucidate how the neuromodulator norepinephrine, the second messenger cyclic
AMP, and cellular activity in the prefrontal cortex encode temporal threat proximity and underlie adaptive
defensive behaviors.

title: PiNBAC: The Program in Neuroscience Post-Baccalaureate Training Program
org_name: HARVARD MEDICAL SCHOOL
phr: There are a dearth of opportunities for students from a wide range of educational and experiential backgrounds to obtain specialized post-baccalaureate training in neuroscience, even as such training is becoming increasingly important for admission to top neuroscience PhD programs. Here, we propose further development of PiNBAC, a post-baccalaureate program launched in 2021 with significant mentorship, research experience, and educational programming housed in the Harvard Medical School Department of Neurobiology, in affiliation with the T32-funded Harvard PhD Program in Neuroscience. application_id: 11201761
activity_code: K99
title: Quantitative dissection of size memory during zebrafish appendage regeneration
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Unlike mammals, zebrafish possess a remarkable ability to regenerate entire appendages, and these
regenerated appendages faithfully reproduce the original size and shape of the appendage. Fundamental
questions remain about how size memory is encoded during regeneration, in part because we lack a
quantitative understanding of the molecular events and cellular decisions that underlie regeneration. Using a
combination of live imaging, computational analysis, and theoretical modeling, this proposal will define the
dynamic molecular and cellular events encoding size memory during zebrafish caudal fin regeneration and
reveal molecular events that are therapeutic targets for promoting functional appendage regeneration in
humans.

application_id: 11115396
activity_code: K99
title: Towards a functional understanding of genotypic and phenotypic diversity across the Salmonella species
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
The genetic landscape of the enteric pathogen Salmonella is vast but remains underexplored. Using a panel of
genetically diverse clinical Salmonella isolates from both generalist and human-restricted lineages, this study
applies cutting-edge functional genomic approaches to systematically dissect how host-associated and phage-
associated stress have shaped Salmonella genomic evolution at the isolate level. Ultimately, this work aims to
identify novel therapeutic targets for the treatment of Salmonella-related diseases.

on laboratory experiences, participants contribute to our fundamental understanding of neural mechanisms that

motivating the next generation of neuroscience researchers.

title: Bioinspired Synthetic High-Density Lipoprotein (sHDL) nanotherapeutics with immunomodulatory and regenerative potential in endodontics
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Dental pulp necrosis and apical periodontitis (AP) are widespread, often compromising root development in
young patients and affecting long-term tooth resilience. This project aims to develop a synthetic high-density
lipoprotein (sHDL) nanoparticle loaded with antibiotics, offering a biocompatible solution with antimicrobial,
immunomodulatory, and regenerative properties to improve endodontic treatment outcomes.

application_id: 11222282
activity_code: F32
title: Impact of Titanium-mediated Oxidative Stress on LPS/TLR4 Signaling
org_name: RUTGERS BIOMEDICAL AND HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Dysregulation of oral mucosal homeostasis is a causative factor underlying peri-implant inflammatory disease.
Titanium particles are associated with peri-implantitis, yet the interactions between titanium particles, oxidative
stress, and mechanisms maintaining homeostasis such as regulation of lipopolysaccharide/Toll-like receptor
signaling have yet to be investigated in the context of peri-implant disease. Understanding the role of implant-
derived titanium particles in modulating oral homeostatic mechanisms will lead to identification of future
therapeutic targets for peri-implantitis treatment.

title: UC Santa Cruz PREP
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA SANTA CRUZ
phr: Narrative
The biomedical research community at UC Santa Cruz has played a leading role in
broadening participation in pre-graduate and graduate research training programs.
Through extensive programming and various support services based on best practices,
we create an inclusive and equitable environment for underrepresented students to
succeed in attaining higher degrees in the biomedical field. With this PREP proposal,

gain admission to and complete a top-ranked Ph.D. program.

title: Interrogating sub-epithelial cell heterogeneity in the developing human intestinal stem cell niche
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: Project Narrative
Single cell mRNA sequencing (scRNAseq) data previously generated from the human fetal intestine indicates
that one of the potential intestinal epithelial niche-supporting populations of the intestinal mesenchyme is the
sub-epithelial cells (SECs). This mesenchymal sub-population is located immediately adjacent to the intestinal
epithelium, can be defined by PDGFRahi/F3+ gene expression, and was recently determined to not be a
homogenous population; but rather there are distinct, mutually exclusive identities localized to the crypt and
villus SECs (Holloway, 2021). This proposal aims to use iPSC-derived HIO cultures to 1) interrogate the role of
crypt-based SECs as a potential source of niche signaling and 2) to investigate the role of HGF signaling in
maintenance of the i...

application_id: 11143270
activity_code: K01
title: Development of abuse liability-based tobacco tax proposals: Experimental impact on tobacco-related socioeconomic cancer health disparities
org_name: VIRGINIA POLYTECHNIC INST AND ST UNIV
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Tobacco taxes are widely used and demonstrably effective in reducing mortality and morbidity including cancer
related outcomes, but they may exacerbate tobacco-related socioeconomic disparities. This project will develop
and test the health equity of an abuse liability dependent tax proposal in a sample of cigarette smokers from
diverse socioeconomic background. The findings of this project will elucidate a novel tax proposal designed to
reduce tobacco dependence and tobacco-related disparities.

application_id: 10828924
activity_code: F32
title: TREM2 senses both pathogen- and damage-associated molecular patterns to promote S. aureus craniotomy infection
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF NEBRASKA MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) is responsible for the majority of craniotomy-associated infections, where it forms a biofilm on the bone flap that is recalcitrant to antibiotics and evades immune clearance. Combined infection and injury from the surgical procedure leads to a mixture of pathogen- and damage-associated molecular patterns (PAMPs and DAMPs) in the local tissue environment, which are both sensed by triggering receptor expressed on myeloid cells-2 (TREM2). This proposed research will investigate the molecular and metabolic mechanisms by which TREM2 mediates an anti-inflammatory immune cell response and S. aureus immune evasion to promote biofilm growth.

application_id: 11199007
activity_code: K99
title: Deciphering the underlying mechanisms of craniofacial spliceosomopathies
org_name: NEW YORK UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Nager syndrome (OMIM#154400) is a developmental disorder characterized by hypoplasia of the facial bones
and a variety of limb defects. Mutations in the SF3B4 gene, which encodes a protein involved in the maturation
of pre-mRNA, is a major cause for Nager syndrome. In this application I propose to gain novel insights into the
mechanisms underlying Nager syndrome craniofacial defects through in vivo and in vitro modeling of the
disease, and carry that knowledge over to the modeling of other craniofacial disorders to determine if there is a
common root cause.

title: HCMV stimulation of interferon signaling to promote viral gene expression and latency
org_name: DARTMOUTH COLLEGE
phr: Project Narrative
Human cytomegalovirus (HCMV) is a ubiquitous and medically significant pathogen which masterfully evades
and manipulates host immune responses. As part of its life cycle, HCMV enters a quiescent state known as
latency, which it accomplishes using a combination of viral and host factors. This project seeks to determine
the ways HCMV manipulates the human immune system to its own advantage, to enhance viral latency and
persistence.

activity_code: DP2
title: Turning back biological clock: mechanisms of age reversal in a whole organism
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Age reversal in an entire organism can postpone age-related diseases and enhance the well-being during the
later stages of life. Our research has revealed that sexual planarians of Schmidtea mediterranea have evolved
a natural solution to age reversal at the whole-body level, and here we propose to investigate the underlying
molecular and cellular mechanisms that drive age reversal. Successful completion of the project holds the
promise of providing valuable insights to advance anti-aging medicine for humans.

application_id: 10913516
activity_code: F31
title: Single-molecule Investigation of the Interaction Between MeCP2 and Chromatin
org_name: ROCKEFELLER UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The methyl-CpG binding protein 2 (MeCP2) is a highly abundant chromatin-binding protein, which if mutated causes Rett syndrome, a severe neurological disorder with no approved therapies that directly address the underlying defects of MeCP2 loss of function. One primary contributor to the lack of MeCP2-targeted interventions is an inadequate understanding of how MeCP2 reads methylated DNA within hierarchically organized chromosomes. This proposal will use single-molecule visualization and structural biology technologies to investigate how MeCP2 biophysically navigates the chromatin landscape to reach its canonical methyl-DNA substrate at unprecedented spatial and temporal resolution.

application_id: 11231650
activity_code: F32
title: Highly parallelized characterization of histone reader dysfunction
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Proper interactions between chromatin and its regulatory factors are fundamental for essential biological
processes such as DNA replication, repair, and transcription. Protein domains known as “readers” facilitate
recruitment of regulators to specific regions of chromatin marked by histone posttranslational modifications,
however the characterization of “histone reader” domains is far from complete and hinders our comprehension
of their role in diseases. The goal of this project is to develop a technology platform that will enable rapid and
systematic characterization of protein variant interaction with post-translational modifications and apply it to
studying the fundamental properties and impact of disease mutations on histone reader function.

title: 3D genome-based transcriptional program for male germline differentiation

The gene expression program in male germ cells ensures unidirectional spermatogenic differentiation. This

title: Training Grant in Vision Research
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY

application_id: 11243243
activity_code: F31
title: Structural and Functional Roles of Membrane Lipids in the pH gating of Connexin-46/50 Channels.
org_name: OREGON HEALTH & SCIENCE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Gap junctions facilitate the flow of electrolytes, nutrients, and waste in the human lens, and the age-related
loss of this function is a major contributor to cataract disease. While the regulation of gap junction channels by
acidification (i.e., pH) has been extensively studied and linked to diseases such as stroke, heart attack, and
cataract, the precise mechanism of pH-induced gap junction closure remains unknown. This study aims to
elucidate the molecular details, including the putative role of lipids, describing the response of lens gap
junctions to pH changes associated with aging.

application_id: 11234238
activity_code: F32
title: Structure-Property-Function Relationship in mRNA/CARTs for Targeted mRNA Delivery
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
The recently developed charge-altering releasable transporter (CART) gene delivery vectors exhibit
remarkable efficiency for mRNA transfection and expression with site specific selectivity and low
cytotoxicity. We propose to study the structure-property-function relationship of mRNA/CARTs
complexes with advanced microscopy and scattering techniques to elucidate how physicochemical
properties affect nanostructure. Distinct formulations will be further screened to relate biological
function to structure allowing for the establishment of a set of design rules to aid the development of
CARTs for efficient and selective immunotherapies.

title: Multifunctional roles of an Orientia tsutsugamushi nucleomodulin
org_name: VIRGINIA COMMONWEALTH UNIVERSITY
phr: NARRATIVE
Orientia tsutsugamushi is a bacterium that is the causative agent of scrub typhus, a potentially fatal disease that
afflicts one million people annually in the endemic Asia-Pacific region but also South America, Europe, and
Africa. This proposal will examine the mechanisms by which O. tsutsugamushi modulates host gene expression
and immune signaling. This will expand our understanding of pathogen-host manipulation and provide potential
targets for treatment of this global disease.

title: Elucidating the roles of the beta-2 adrenergic receptor and epidermal growth factor receptor in flavivirus NS1-mediated endothelial dysfunction
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO

application_id: 10996617
activity_code: F32
title: Defining the role of stress granules in cancer initiation
org_name: BOSTON CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative. Stress granules are cytoprotective biological condensates that are induced by cell stress and are hijacked by cancer cells to withstand the harsh environment of tumorigenesis. This proposal leverages a humanized zebrafish melanoma model to test the essentiality and epitranscriptomic influence of stress granule formation in melanoma precursor cells on melanoma initiation and progression in vivo. As stress granules are observed in many cancer systems, the findings of this proposal will uncover foundational knowledge that can be used to exploit a conserved molecular pathway of cancer initiating cells, aiding the effort to control cancer in the long-term by stopping it before it can start.

activity_code: K01
title: Intersectional discrimination and linkage to HIV prevention and care in transgender women in Ecuador
org_name: WHITMAN-WALKER INSTITUTE, INC.
phr: 8. Project Narrative In Ecuador, the HIV epidemic is concentrated primarily among transgender women—those who identify as women or on the transfeminine spectrum and who were assigned a male sex at birth. There is an urgent need for status-neutral HIV interventions tailored to this high-risk group. activity_code: K23
title: Mapping Genetic Pathways Associated With Neurodevelopmental Variability after Fetal Anti-Seizure Medication Exposure
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Over one million women with epilepsy (WWE) of childbearing age in the USA are considered at higher risk for
adverse pregnancy outcomes, but our ability to predict individual outcomes is limited due to a lack of
understanding of how genetic factors interact with antiseizure medications. This proposal aims to investigate
the impact of genetic factors in WWE and their children on the children’s long-term cognitive status. The
findings from this study will advance our knowledge of the mechanisms that impact long-term
neurodevelopmental outcomes in children of WWE, and pave the way for future innovative, personalized
therapeutic and preventative approaches to improve care for WWE.

activity_code: F32
title: Regulation of IGF-1R in acute and chronic thyroid eye disease
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO
phr: Project Narrative Thyroid eye disease is a debilitating and disfiguring condition featuring deposition of hyaluronan and massive tissue fibrosis leading to proptosis, eye muscle restriction, eyelid retraction, dry eye, and optic nerve compression. The molecular basis of the disease is not fully understood, and neither is the regulation of the main signaling axis upon which clinical trials and the new gold standard of treatment is based. Studying the cellular and molecular basis of thyroid eye disease and its key cell surface proteins will identify novel treatment paradigms and targets to improve the treatment of acute and chronic thyroid eye disease.

title: Developing a decision aid for reproductive health for women with kidney disease
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Chronic kidney disease affects more than 2 million American women of childbearing age, and is associated
with decreased fertility and increased risk of adverse pregnancy outcomes. Planning for pregnancy prior to
conception is advocated for all women but is especially important for women with kidney disease because of
their potential complications. This research will develop and test a decision aid that will help women and their
kidney doctors share information to support informed decision making related to reproductive health care and
pregnancy in women with kidney disease.

title: Histone crotonylation as a novel regulator of learning and memory
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA

title: EngageTEXT: Using phenotypes to enhance engagement with a diabetes text messaging intervention
org_name: NEW YORK UNIVERSITY SCHOOL OF MEDICINE
phr: Project Narrative
The epidemic rise of type 2 diabetes (T2D) in the US highlights the need for effective interventions. While digital
health behavioral interventions (DHBI) that use text messaging show promise in improving T2D health outcomes,
their efficacy is limited by insufficient user engagement. This proposal addresses this gap by leveraging machine
learning for phenotyping user behavior from existing text messaging DHBIs to create robust engagement
phenotypes and evaluate their efficacy in a pilot trial among individuals with T2D, advancing a more personalized
approach to diabetes management.

title: Dissecting the role of the Fanconi anemia pathway in chromothripsis
org_name: UT SOUTHWESTERN MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Cancer genomes commonly harbor complex rearrangements that arise through chromothripsis, the catastrophic fragmentation and reassembly of individual chromosomes. Chromothripsis originates from mis-segregated chromosomes in micronuclei, yet our understanding of the causes and consequences of chromothripsis is limited. This proposal seeks to define the mechanisms by which the Fanconi anemia pathway promotes chromosome fragmentation and the generation of highly rearranged chromosomes.

title: Angiotensin-(1-7) engages hypothalamic arcuate-paraventricular nucleus inhibitory pathways to lower blood pressure
org_name: PENNSYLVANIA STATE UNIV HERSHEY MED CTR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Obesity is a worldwide health problem that increases the risk for hypertension and cardiovascular disease; however, the molecular mechanisms connecting obesity with hypertension and optimal therapies to lower blood pressure in this disease remain unclear. We propose to determine the role of tuft cells, a type of cell that has been found to be important for proper immune functioning in other organs, for the proper immune functioning of salivary glands in healthy patients and those with Sjögren’s syndrome. These studies are expected to introduce a new area of investigation to salivary gland research and potentially lead to novel treatment options.

application_id: 10996882
activity_code: F31
title: Cellular and Molecular Mechanisms of Inhibitory Synapse Development
org_name: BRANDEIS UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Synapses are the primary sites of cell-cell contact in the nervous system which rely on protein signaling to determine when and where synapses will develop and to specify their identity as excitatory or inhibitory. Perturbations to this process are implicated in a variety of neurological and neurodevelopmental disorders including autism, epilepsy, and schizophrenia. We propose to utilize the demonstrated ability of Sema4D (signaling via the PlexinB1 receptor) to rapidly promote inhibitory synapse formation to study the mechanisms by which new inhibitory synapses are formed in the brain.

application_id: 10794910
activity_code: K00
title: Basic neural processing mechanisms of live human face viewing
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Despite the importance of social functioning to both health and psychiatric illness, relatively little is known about the neural processing mechanisms of live human face viewing. While activity in the right temporoparietal junction has been shown to distinguish live face viewing from humanoid robot face viewing, the neural features of that activity and their correlates in stimulus acquisition remain to be seen. This proposal will use simultaneous functional Near Infrared Spectroscopy, electroencephalography, eye-tracking, and pupillometry during live face and robot face viewing to test the hypothesis that visual sensing strategy is an active contributor to the neural processing of live human faces.

application_id: 10996884
activity_code: F31
title: Advancing a biopsychosocial model of bedtime procrastination
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF UTAH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Although emerging research suggests that circadian disruption serves as a risk for cardiometabolic disease, there is a lack of research investigating behavioral contributors to circadian disruption. Bedtime procrastination, or delays in bedtime in the absence of external obligations, may disrupt circadian rhythms through alterations in sleep timing. This project seeks to evaluate the impact of bedtime procrastination on circadian disruption and cardiometabolic health, while also investigating the roles of anxiety, reward, and self-regulation in the development of daily bedtime procrastination in order to inform novel treatments for bedtime procrastination and circadian-sleep disturbances.

title: Dissecting the role of vascular endothelial BMP9 signaling in lung transplantation
org_name: NORTHWESTERN UNIVERSITY AT CHICAGO
phr: Narrative The goal of this project is to determine the role of Bone Morphogenetic Protein (BMP) signaling in primary graft dysfunction (PGD). BMP signaling is essential for microvascular homeostasis, and as such, could play an important role in modulating IRI post lung transplantation. We will use novel rodent and in vitro modeling systems together with therapeutic inhibitors to dissect the role of BMP signaling in PGD with the overall goal to develop BMP specific therapeutic strategies that improve lung transplant outcomes.

phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  In the current proposal, I devised a mixed-methods study to expedite the development of interventions addressing the negative consequences of opioid or cannabis use among patients with chronic pain. title: Enhancing Prostate Cancer Treatment Outcomes with Combined Radioimmunotherapy and Antibody Drug Conjugates
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: Project narrative:
To effectively manage metastatic and therapy-resistant tumors, high doses of a single drug are typically required,

application_id: 10997009
activity_code: F31
title: Identifying the Drivers and Consequences ofHost-Microbiome Composition and Function
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The microbiome plays a large role in host health and physiological functioning but the mechanisms by which hosts recruit microbes is largely unknown. This study leverages the microbiomes of unicellular algae as a model system to assess the main drivers of microbiome assembly and the resulting physiological effect that these microbiomes have on host organisms. By approaching this question from an evolutionary perspective, the results of this study will have implications for all eukaryotic organisms, ultimately leading to new insights as to why microbiomes might fail in disease and how we might generate new microbiome-based therapeutics.

application_id: 11136455
activity_code: F31
title: Regulation of hematopoiesis during tumor progression
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Altered hematopoiesis and myeloid biased skewing promotes the expansion of immune suppressive myeloid
populations, a phenomenon that is observed in cancer patients regardless of the tumor type and/or dissemination
to bone. Currently, the impact of a primary tumor on bone marrow hematopoietic stem and progenitor cells
(HSPCs), as well as the factors involved in changing their frequency and/or functionality, remain unclear. The
aims proposed here will broaden our understanding of the effects of tumor progression on hematopoiesis and,
importantly, the role of the bone in generating the immune suppressive environment.

application_id: 11215091
activity_code: K99
title: Pulse-oximetry method for un-biased assessment of arterial saturation
org_name: TUFTS UNIVERSITY MEDFORD
phr: Project Narrative
Conventional pulse-oximetry does not fully account for individual-specific anatomy or
hemodynamics, leading to possible inequalities in care due to skin-tone biases. This research
project aims to pioneer a novel noninvasive pulse-oximetry method, designed around an auto-
calibrated arrangement of optical sensors, that more accurately and unbiasedly determines a
person's arterial oxygen saturation than conventional pulse-oximetry. The positive impact of
this technique will be a non-biased and equitable noninvasive assessment of one's arterial
oxygen saturation, a ubiquitous and clinically relevant measure of oxygen perfusion.

application_id: 10829463
activity_code: K08
title: Genetic and Cellular Mechanisms of Temporal Lobe Epilepsy
org_name: BRIGHAM AND WOMEN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  Epilepsy is a serious chronic neurologic condition that affects over 50 million people worldwide, and the most common form of focal epilepsy, temporal lobe epilepsy (TLE), is frequently resistant to medical treatment resulting in significant neurologic and psychiatric morbidity. Recent research has shown that genetic mutations that arise after conception (somatic variants) may disrupt normal cellular function in the brain and cause neuropsychiatric disease. This proposal will examine the hypothesis that somatic mutations cause TLE and it will aim to identify the cell-types and gene expression changes that contribute to the development of this disease.

activity_code: F31
title: Hyperphosphatemia Contributes to Cardiac Injury in Chronic Kidney Disease
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ALABAMA AT BIRMINGHAM
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Patients with advanced chronic kidney disease (CKD) suffer from widespread tissue injuries and develop markedly increased blood levels of phosphate and fibroblast growth factor 23 (FGF23). We have experimental evidence suggesting that phosphate and FGF23 can directly target the heart and cause pathologic changes in cardiac structure and function. In this research proposal, we will determine how phosphate and FGF23 act on heart tissue and whether FGF23 made by heart muscle cells can cause CKD-associated heart injury, which would suggest that CKD patients might benefit from therapies tackling phosphate and FGF23.

title: Adapting an evidence-based intervention to improve HIV testing and PrEP uptake among young men who have sex with men in Vietnam
org_name: HANOI MEDICAL UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Even though young men who have sex with men (YMSM) in Vietnam are disproportionately affected by the HIV epidemic, HIV testing and pre-exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) uptake among this group are low. By proposing to adapt an mobile health (mHealth) intervention developed in the United States for YMSM in Vietnam and explore barriers to implementing the app, the proposed study will provide critical guidance for the implementation of mHealth interventions for HIV prevention, not only in Vietnam but also in other low-resource settings. This research holds great potential to improve HIV testing and PrEP use among YMSM and significantly contribute to the goal of ending the AIDS epidemic globally.

title: JAK2V617F transactivation of JAK1 mediates constitutive activation of IL-10R signaling
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA-IRVINE
phr: Project narrative The central purpose of our project is to identify and understand the mechanism by which JAK2V617F creates a competitive advantage for outgrowth in haematopoietic stem cells (HSC) in MPN patients that exhibit dampened IL-10R signalling response. In using diverse cell signalling analysis techniques in western blot, co- immunoprecipitation, and phosphoflowcytometry we will be able to effectively interrogate the transformative effect of the mutant JAK2V617F on IL-10R signalling in a non-canon context. title: Uncovering the impact of oxidative stress on centromere integrity
org_name: THOMAS JEFFERSON UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Defects in centromere integrity cause chromosomal mis-segregation and chromosomal instability, which are commonly observed in cancers. Under conditions of chronic oxidative stress (OS) cells exhibit increased centromeric aberrations; however, the effects of OS specifically at the centromere are unclear. In my proposal, I employ a specialized chemoptogenetic tool to target OS specifically at the centromere in order to investigate the DNA damage response and factors involved in maintaining centromere integrity.

title: Defining the molecular landscape of hyperfiltration-mediated glomerular injury using kidney allografts as a model system
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: LAY SUMMARY: The proposed research has broad relevance to public health as it focuses on improving
our understanding of the molecular basis of a shared mechanism of kidney disease progression, i.e., renal
hyperfiltration injury. Kidney transplants will be used as a model system to test how hyperfiltration causes kidney
damage in humans. We expect the findings to be broadly applicable to common kidney disease states such as
diabetes and obesity that are also accompanied by hyperfiltration. Insights from this analysis we be applied to
prolong the lifespan of kidney transplants and reduce morbidity and mortality associated with chronic kidney
disease.

application_id: 10794954
activity_code: F30
title: Tracking and regulation of nicotine dependence in the insular cortex
org_name: HARVARD MEDICAL SCHOOL
phr: Project Narrative Over half of current tobacco smokers report a desire to quit, yet due the highly addictive nature of nicotine, it often takes ~10 attempts before individuals successfully quit smoking. This project will use a mouse model of nicotine dependence to characterize how nicotine dependence/withdrawal states and nicotine-predicting cues are represented in the insular cortex – a region of the brain implicated in addiction. By characterizing how the actions of nicotine are encoded in the insular cortex and defining the causal role of this region in nicotine- seeking behaviors, this proposal will give new insight into behaviors relevant to nicotine addiction.

phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Alzheimer’s disease (AD) is a debilitating and irreversible neurodegenerative disease associated with major disruptions to memory. activity_code: K23
title: Vending machines: a low-barrier method to deliver harm reduction services in the community
org_name: PENNSYLVANIA STATE UNIV HERSHEY MED CTR
phr: NARRATIVE
In response to the growing number of deadly drug overdoses in the US, vending machines (VMs), which
dispense naloxone, fentanyl testing strips, and/or other harm reduction supplies, are becoming popular.
However, there is limited research on using VMs as a harm reduction delivery strategy and evaluating its
impact in diverse communities. This study addresses the existing research gap by proposing ways to
effectively implement VMs in different communities and evaluate their impact, thereby laying the foundation for
future, larger-scale research on VMs’ effects on individual and community health, particularly in reducing
overdose deaths and other SUD-related harms.

activity_code: K23
title: MObile health interVention to INcrease activity in Heart Failure (MOVIN-HF)
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: Project Narrative/Relevance to Public Health Statement
Heart failure is projected to affect more than 8-million American adults by 2030 and is a leading cause of
morbidity and mortality. Physical activity improves outcomes for patients with heart failure, although most heart
failure patients rarely participate in enough physical activity. This research will use mobile technology to
characterize real-time factors such as symptoms, mood, and motivation that impact physical activity for heart
failure patients, and then use that information to adapt and pilot a mobile health intervention to support heart

application_id: 11061756
activity_code: K23
title: Climate Variability and Respiratory Health in Asthma and COPD
org_name: BETH ISRAEL DEACONESS MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
In order to protect public health from a rapidly changing climate through preventive patient care and public
health policies, it is essential for the medical community to understand the impact of temperature, precipitation,
and its extremes on respiratory health, particularly in those disproportionately impacted by climate change,
such as individuals with asthma and COPD, and Black and Latinx communities. The results of this study will
provide valuable data to understand relationships of temperature and precipitation with asthma and COPD.
This data will support future investigation of interventions, such as air purification and community cooling
centers, to build resiliency and adaptation to a rapidly warming world.

application_id: 11061199
activity_code: F30
title: Inhibitory neuron dysfunction in intellectual disability and epilepsy
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The proposed research is relevant to public health because it seeks to understand how synaptic dysfunction of
inhibitory interneurons contribute to disease phenotypes of human disorders due to pathogenic loss-of-function
variants in the X-linked gene CASK. Lack of knowledge about how loss of the protein CASK contributes to
neuronal dysfunction and how this dysfunction connects to disease phenotypes has limited the ability to develop
therapeutics to alleviate symptoms or prevent disease. Furthermore, this project is applicable to the NIH’s
mission because it will provide fundamental knowledge about the cellular mechanisms of intellectual disability
and epilepsy, common symptoms across numerous neurodevelopmental disorders.
.

activity_code: F31
title: A Multiscale Computational Model of the Heart for Patient-specific Diagnosis of HFpEF
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE This project seeks to non-invasively estimate patient-specific myocardial stiffness via computational means in patients with heart failure with preserved ejection fraction (HFpEF). As a result, this work will help elucidate deeper insights into how myocardial stiffness changes over the course of HFpEF. Such knowledge could help physicians stratify patients into risk groups and devise management plans that are optimal for each patient as well as predicting HFpEF in the long-term, ultimately improving clinical outcomes and enhancing their quality of life.

application_id: 11215916
activity_code: K99
title: Mapping inflammatory bowel disease variants to genes and functional phenotypes in human macrophages
org_name: ICAHN SCHOOL OF MEDICINE AT MOUNT SINAI
phr: Project Narrative
Inflammatory Bowel Disease (IBD) is a complex genetic disorder driven by dysregulated immune responses to
environmental triggers, influenced by multiple genetic variants. Despite genome-wide association studies
(GWAS) identifying hundreds of IBD-associated loci, the functional roles of many variants remain unclear. This
project aims to prioritize genes linked to IBD risk variants and functionally investigate their roles in human
macrophages, advancing our understanding of genetic regulation in IBD and guiding the development of
targeted therapies.

application_id: 10998462
activity_code: F32
title: Investigating the role of the Unfolded Protein Response in Genetic Dilated Cardiomyopathy
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Adding to the growing evidence of impaired proteostasis as a cellular hallmark of cardiac disease, Endoplasmic Reticulum stress, protein aggregation, and activation of the Unfolded Protein Response (UPR) have been identified in a genetically inherited form of Dilated Cardiomyopathy (DCM). Pharmacological activation of the UPR alleviates ER stress and rescues impaired contractility in DCM cardiomyocytes. Understanding the mechanism of how UPR bolstering improves DCM phenotypes will identify new therapeutic targets to treat the cell stress in the cardiomyocyte that underlies DCM.

application_id: 11021013
activity_code: F30
title: Dissecting the Role of Desmoplakin in Inflammation in Cardiomyopathy
org_name: NORTHWESTERN UNIVERSITY AT CHICAGO
phr: Project Narrative Desmosomes are specialized complexes, necessary for normal cell interactions in the heart. Desmoplakin links the desmosomal components to the cytoskeleton through intermediate filaments, and genetic mutations that disrupt desmoplakin lead to an inflammatory form of cardiomyopathy with irregular heart rhythms. This study will assess the role of innate immune activation using human induced pluripotent stem cells (hiPSCs) containing truncating variants in DSP to demonstrate the potential for immune modulation in preventing disease progression of DSP-associated arrhythmogenic cardiomyopathy.

application_id: 11230027
activity_code: K99
title: Investigating the impact of abnormal lipid metabolism on mouse intervertebral disc
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MARYLAND BALTIMORE
phr: Project Narrative
Abnormal lipid metabolism in cells can cause energy imbalance, abnormal lipid accumulation, and insulin
resistance, leading to cellular dysfunction and disease. Based on our preliminary findings that mouse
intervertebral disc cells undergo active lipid metabolism and that abnormal lipid metabolism causes lipid
accumulation in these cells, we plan to further investigate how abnormal lipid metabolism affects IVD functions.
We believe that the findings from this project will lay the foundation for future studies exploring a novel
mechanistic link between metabolic diseases and disc disease.

application_id: 11071633
activity_code: F30
title: Individualized Prediction of Progression to Heart Failure Using Digital Twins, an Explainable Reinforcement Learning Approach
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ILLINOIS AT URBANA-CHAMPAIGN
phr: Project Narrative
Heart failure (HF) is an urgent public health problem where specific individuals of the population demonstrate
markedly elevated risk of developing HF. This project will develop a personalized precision medicine approach
to identify HF before symptoms begin and predict individualized HF risk using a digital twin, an advanced
explainable machine learning method, leveraging information on blood biomarkers, organ function measures,
and ultrasound images of the heart. The results of this novel study are vital to inform the future interventions to
prevent HF, and thereby decrease HF-associated morbidity and mortality.

application_id: 11079433
activity_code: F30
title: Coronavirus Infection Dynamics and Cross-species Transmission in Bats
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA AT DAVIS
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Coronavirus Infection Dynamics and Cross-species Transmission in Bats
Newly emerged coronaviruses are a major threat to public health and understanding infection dynamics in
reservoir bat hosts has the potential to inform on disease emergence and mitigation of epidemic and pandemic
risk. This proposal will investigate host and environmental conditions facilitating cross-species transmission in
wildlife sampled across Asia, Africa, and the Middle East. I will also prospectively collect data on bat-borne
coronaviruses in diverse ecosystems locally in California (USA) and at an international field site (Myanmar) to
assess seasonality, model disease dynamics, and investigate drivers of cross-species disease transmission.

activity_code: K01
title: Community-Engaged Structural Approaches to Prevent Violence and Improve HIV Prevention and Care Outcomes Among Trans Women of Color
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Trans women of color are at elevated risk of HIV, yet interventions to address upstream structural determinants of HIV in this population, including stigma-driven economic vulnerability and violence, are limited. The proposed work will evaluate how an innovative, gender-affirming cash transfer and economic empowerment intervention with trans women of color in Detroit can reduce violence and promote HIV prevention and care. Findings from this study and from a community needs assessment will be used to design a new intervention addressing structural determinants of health in collaboration with a community-based participatory research partnership.

application_id: 11076280
activity_code: K23
title: Preparing older adults for major abdominal surgery: a systems engineering approach to implement preoperative comprehensive geriatric assessments
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WISCONSIN-MADISON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The growing aging population increasingly requires major surgery, often resulting in serious complications,
functional impairment, cognitive decline, increases in healthcare utilization and death, which are all major public
health burdens. Improving perioperative practices to care for this vulnerable, complex population can reduce this
burden. The proposed work will utilize a novel approach to surgical care of older adults, applying systems
engineering and implementation science principles to develop an implementation package for preoperative
geriatric assessments that can be adapted and applied to a wide range of hospitals, serving a diverse population
of older adults.

title: Identifying neuronal targets and mechanisms of manganese-induced neuromotor disease
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF TEXAS AT AUSTIN
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Over-exposure to manganese (Mn) is neurotoxic and can lead to incurable motor deficits. application_id: 11071062
activity_code: F30
title: A Novel Strategy to Inhibit Candida glabrata Virulence by Targeting an Essential Fungal Lipid Transfer Protein
org_name: TEXAS A&M UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCE CTR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Multidrug resistant Candida, including Candida glabrata, present a rising public health crisis due to the high
mortality of invasive candidiasis – a systemic consequence of inadequately treated Candida infection. The goal
of this work is to address the prospect of developing new antimycotic compounds that inhibit fungal
phosphoinositide signaling pathways with very high specificity. In agreement with the National Institute of Allergy
and Infectious Diseases mission statement to “conduct and support basic and applied research to better
understand, treat, and ultimately prevent infectious, immunologic, and allergic diseases,” this work aims to set
the preclinical foundation to prevent a life-threatening infectious disease.

application_id: 11071349
activity_code: F31
title: Studies on the SARS-CoV-2 Main Protease Dimerization Mechanism
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF TEXAS HLTH SCIENCE CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The main protease enzyme (Mpro) of SARS-CoV-2 is required for virus polyprotein cleavage, replication, and pathogenesis, and it is a validated drug target as evidenced by the efficacy of Paxlovid. Mpro functions as a homodimer, and studies here investigate the molecular mechanism of dimerization. A detailed understanding of this molecular mechanism will advance fundamental knowledge and provide starting points for the development of a new class of drugs that works differently from Paxlovid by blocking dimerization.

application_id: 11078837
activity_code: K23
title: Using Artificial Intelligence to Predict Cognitive Training Response in Amnestic Mild Cognitive Impairment
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF FLORIDA
phr: Project Narrative/Relevance to Public Health:
Patients in the early stages of Alzheimer’s disease each display a unique pattern of altered brain structure, function,
and thinking and memory skills. Knowing that their diagnosis of amnestic mild cognitive impairment (aMCI) places
them at risk for developing Alzheimer’s disease, patients often seek out intervention to delay the progression of the
disease without knowing whether it will truly provide benefit. This project will use advanced machine learning tools to
provide detailed predictions of aMCI patients’ likelihood of improving from a widely supported cognitive training
intervention, and clarify how an individual patient’s brain and cognitive features contribute to their response-to-
intervention.
