title: Investigating the Interaction Between Nicotine and GPR3: Gene expression, cell activity and withdrawal
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA-IRVINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Despite public health measures aimed at reducing tobacco use, nicotine products remain a leading global
cause of death, with limited success in long term cessation despite available agents. This proposal seeks to
investigate the regulation and function of G-Protein Coupled Receptor 3 (GPR3) as a novel target for nicotine
cessation and withdrawal. Through this research, I will advance my expertise in translational research,
neurobiology, and pharmacology, and foster my ability to translate scientific discoveries into clinical
interventions.

activity_code: K01
title: Investigating housing, education, and employment conditions and maternal and child health outcomes: a geospatial and data science approach
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CHICAGO
phr: In the US, maternal and child health (MCH) outcomes remain poor, with little evidence of sustained improvements despite significant investments. Improving these outcomes requires characterizing the variation in place-based conditions- particularly, housing, education, and employment- that shape MCH to inform the design of more effective interventions and policies. The goal of this project is to apply novel and rigorous data science and analytic tools to address significant gaps in our understanding of how housing, education, and employment conditions are geographically patterned and influence MCH outcomes from preconception to birth.

application_id: 11086804
activity_code: K23
title: Clinical Impact and Epidemiology of Nonsteroidal Anti-inflammatory Drug Allergies
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) are recommended for first-line therapy of many conditions, but
allergy over-reporting and lack of reaction clarification may lead to unnecessary avoidance of NSAIDs and
increased use of second line, more costly, and/or less effective treatments. Our study aims to determine how
NSAID allergy labeling influences alternative medication prescribing and patient clinical outcomes, and to
identify predictors for true NSAID-induced allergic hypersensitivity. This work will inform the development of
new evidence-based approaches to risk stratify and target previously overlooked populations for allergy
intervention, allowing delabeling and providing safer management and improving the care of drug-allergic
patients.

title: Improving transitions of care for hospitalized patients with opioid use disorder
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Hospital visits are important opportunities to start treatment for opioid use disorder (OUD) with evidence-based
medications, but this only improves outcomes if treatment is continued in the community. The
present
study
proposes to develop and test strategies that improve transitions of care following hospitalizations for patients
with OUD, which has the potential to impact public health by increasing the connection of patients to lifesaving
care. The
implementation
mentorship, training, and research activities will inform future studies to improve both efficacy and
of care for patients with OUD and set the stage for a larger-scale trial.

org_name: NEW YORK UNIVERSITY SCHOOL OF MEDICINE

The efficient assessment of Major Depressive Disorder (MDD) and Generalized Anxiety Disorder (GAD) is a

application_id: 11082476
activity_code: K23
title: Establishing Mechanisms Between Traumatic Brain Injury and Dementia Using Epidemiology, Clinical Studies, Blood-Based Biomarkers, and Neuroimaging Biomarkers
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
TBI is associated with significant morbidity, including cognitive impairment and dementia, but the mechanisms
linking TBI to dementia remain poorly understood. The central hypothesis of this proposal is that TBI is
associated with cognitive decline and dementia risk in part via vascular pathways which accelerate
neurodegeneration for years post-TBI. The overall scientific objective of this proposal is to use blood-based and
neuroimaging biomarkers of vascular injury and dysfunction to investigate mechanisms linking TBI and
neurocognitive outcomes and to identify time-periods during which future interventions may be effective at
preventing TBI-related dementia.

application_id: 11091624
activity_code: K23
title: Detecting Adolescent Suicidality Biometric Signals and Dynamic Variability with Wearable Technology
org_name: OREGON HEALTH & SCIENCE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The proposed project is relevant to public health because it focuses on developing new, objective, physiologic-
based monitoring methods for adolescent suicidality, an exponentially rising disease in the United States, and
overwhelming emergency departments. These methods lead to the potential for significant advances in early
detection and utilization of therapeutic interventions when dynamic suicidality changes occur in the outpatient
setting. This competitive application aligns with three of the NIMH's strategic goals, including increased
preventative measures for early detection, research to study mental illness trajectory over time, and increased
public health impact through the ED, where preventive efforts may be introduced/implemented.

application_id: 10999296
activity_code: F32
title: Text-messaging Communication Micro-Intervention for Couples Coping with Advanced GI Cancer
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Many couples have difficulty communicating about cancer-related concerns, which can impede their ability to cope with the disease, lead to increased psychological distress, and negatively impact the quality of their relationships. While efficacious, couple-based communication interventions have limited uptake due to their time-intensive nature; the proposed research project aims to address this problem by developing and pilot testing a text-messaging communication micro-intervention to increase reach and participation in couples coping with advanced cancer. If successful, we will have developed a brief, targeted, and scalable intervention that could be integrated into routine oncology care, resulting in better outcomes for both patients and their family caregivers.

title: Precision Monitoring of Multiple Sclerosis with Mobile Devices: A Transformative Approach
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Multiple Sclerosis is a neurodegenerative disease that is tracked and assessed using infrequent and expensive clinical assessments and imaging studies. Capturing extra-clinical disease activity at higher frequencies using new sensors and capabilities on mobile devices can improve clinical decision making, and improve clinical researchers monitor disease activity. This work aims to develop the models and practices necessary to remotely track MS disease activity using mobile device data, and unlock our understanding of what happens outside of the clinic, thus having the potential to improve the care of patients and quality of MS research worldwide.

application_id: 11082406
activity_code: K23
title: Addressing social adversity to improve outcomes among children undergoing liver transplant: the role for a health advocate on the transplant team
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE .
The proposed research serves public health with its novel focus to improve pediatric liver transplant outcomes
by assessing and intervening on children’s underlying health-related social risks, e.g., material economic
hardship, social isolation, caregiver mental health, health literacy, and neighborhood characteristics.
Integrating a Health Advocate as a member of the post-transplant care team will support the delivery of
interventions to overcome barriers interposed by these social risks, with clear potential to close disparities
and significantly improve long-term outcomes for these children. The proposed research is directly relevant to
NIDDK’s vision to seek insights and answers to bridge health disparities.

application_id: 11089517
activity_code: K23
title: Accelerating PrEP provision in primary care settings: Development and Testing of A Practice-Level Multifaceted Implementation Strategy
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
African Americans comprise approximately 10% of in HIV pre-exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) users in the United States, underscoring the urgent need for innovative research to optimize PrEP provision for this population. The proposed research will identify key determinants of PrEP provision (Aim 1); develop a multifaceted implementation strategy to improve PrEP provision for African American primary care patients (Aim 2); and conduct a 12-month two-arm pilot at four primary care practices in Philadelphia to evaluate strategy feasibility and acceptability (Aim 3). Given the critical need to improve PrEP care continuum outcomes, this research will have significant public health implications.

application_id: 11130980
activity_code: K99
title: Genetic and single-cell multi-omic characterization of Alzheimer's disease risk and resilience pathways
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Alzheimer’s disease is a complex neurodegenerative disorder with overwhelming personal, societal, and
economic burdens for which there is currently no means of prevention or disease-modifying treatments. In this
proposal, I will use multi-omic analyses in observational and interventional studies of human and mouse
models of AD to systematically characterize molecular pathways associated with Alzheimer’s genetic risk and
resilience. Completing this proposal can identify causal genetic variants of Alzheimer’s disease that have not
been previously resolved using traditional genetic approaches and prioritize genes and pathways for drug
development.

application_id: 11136653
activity_code: K01
title: Pubertal development, stress system functioning, and addiction vulnerability
org_name: BAYLOR UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Dysregulation in stress system functioning increases vulnerability to addiction (e.g., impulsivity, emotion
dysregulation, early initiation of drug use). The proposed study will investigate the potential for environmental
factors to affect stress system functioning during adolescence, a developmental period characterized by
heightened neuroplasticity and sensitivity to contextual environments. This study will be part of a training
program for a promising early-career scientist, aimed at advancing research on the interplay among pubertal
development, stress system functioning, and drug use vulnerability.

application_id: 10795650
activity_code: F31
title: Structural and Biochemical Effects of Capsid-targeting Molecules on HIV-1 Capsid Assembly
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative For the tens of millions of individuals living with human immunodeficiency virus 1 (HIV-1), antiretroviral therapies are life-saving medicines that prevent the onset of acquired immunodeficiency syndrome (AIDS). Our lab previously reported compounds with anti-HIV-1 activity that belong to a unique class of antiretrovirals not currently used in clinical practice. Here, I aim to determine how these compounds modify the assembled structures of the HIV-1 virion and to establish the biochemical parameters of compound binding to lay the groundwork for the next generation of HIV-1 therapeutics in order to help outpace antiviral drug resistance.

application_id: 11098502
activity_code: K23
title: Trichomonas vaginalis through the lifespan
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Trichomonas vaginalis (TV) is the most prevalent non-viral sexually transmitted infection globally, is associated
with a number of adverse health outcomes, and has received comparatively little public health attention. We
propose to examine the natural history of TV across the lifespan by characterizing the epidemiology, assessing
risk factors associated with TV susceptibility, and profiling the local and systemic (immune/microbiota) effects
of TV infection by life stage and HIV status. This work will shed light on the reasons for the curious bimodal
age distribution in TV prevalence, the mechanisms by which the parasite induces disease and, ultimately,
contribute to understanding the disproportionate adverse health outcomes in women of African descent and in
the Southern...

application_id: 11129684
activity_code: F30
title: Chromatin modifier Polycomb Repressive Complex 2 as a regulator of dental epithelial progenitor cells
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: Project Narrative
Dental decay and tooth loss constitute an important public health issue. Stem cells in continuously growing dental
tissues provide an excellent model for deciphering mechanisms that enable repair and regeneration of teeth, as
well as other organs. This proposal will use state-of-the-art approaches to study how dental stem cells in the
mouse incisor differentiate into enamel-producing ameloblasts, which will lay the long-term groundwork for
clinical applications of stem cell biology in human dentition.

application_id: 11131042
activity_code: K08
title: Investigation of enterovirus D68 pathogenesis in the human spinal cord
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Enterovirus D68 (EV-D68) has been implicated in severe respiratory illness and acute flaccid myelitis (AFM), a
polio-like illness causing paralysis in previously healthy children. There are no specific treatments for AFM,
most children are left with residual deficits, and it is currently unknown how EV-D68 targets the central nervous
system. The goal of this proposal is to identify how EV-D68 mediates cellular damage in the human spinal cord
by using an innovative human spinal cord organoid model.

title: Pathways Relating Amnestic MCI to a Mild Traumatic Brain Injury History (PATH)
org_name: UT SOUTHWESTERN MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PUBLIC HEALTH RELEVANCE:
Amnestic mild cognitive impairment (aMCI) is an early stage in the Alzheimer’s clinical syndrome, and it will be
important to determine whether biological processes in aMCI might differ due to a remote history of mild
traumatic brain injury (mTBI). To this end, the proposed study will combine state-of-the-art high definition
transcranial direct current stimulation and blood-derived biomarker tools with comprehensive clinical and
cognitive information to inform whether the biological changes in aMCI relate to a history of mTBI. This new
information will provide the necessary groundwork to begin identifying the biological processes for how ACS is
related to TBI, which could eventually lead to the development of new interventions to mitigate risk for ACS in
older adul...

application_id: 11136844
activity_code: F31
title: Hp1bp3 oversees chromatin remodeling to slow tumor growth in high grade glioma
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Epigenetic dysregulation is a known driver of high-grade glioma (HGG) but pinpointing the precise epigenetic
disturbances that accelerate tumor growth is still a challenge. We have found that Hp1bp3 is involved in
chromatin remodeling, and patient sequencing data suggests that decreased expression is associated with
enhanced susceptibility to glioma. Here I aim to evaluate the function of Hp1bp3 in HGG disease progression
and its role in epigenetic dysregulation.

title: Physical Activity in Transition-Age Youth on the Autism Spectrum
org_name: KESSLER FOUNDATION, INC.
phr: RELEVANCE TO PUBLIC HEALTH
Four in five youth on the autism spectrum do not engage in sufficient physical activity (PA), which is
linked to increased risks of major chronic diseases such as type 2 diabetes and cardiovascular
diseases in later adulthood. This study will help advance intervention development to promote PA in
transition-age youth on the autism spectrum, by validating an accelerometer-based outcome measure,
quantifying health-enhancing physical activity behaviors, and identifying potential intervention targets.
The findings and data of this study will directly inform a targeted intervention addressing the PA
barriers unique to this population and enable accurate evaluation of intervention outcomes,
contributing to improving PA and health outcomes in people on the autism spectr...

title: Understanding the effects of mitochondrial fission disruption during early cortical development
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative There are a growing number of children being identified with mutations in the mitochondrial fission protein Dynamin-related Protein 1 (DRP1), leading to severe neurodevelopmental symptoms including severe epilepsies. This study aims to address gaps in knowledge about interactions between mutant DRP1 and mitochondria-associated organelles such as actin and the endoplasmic reticulum during neurodevelopment. Successful completion of this project could improve our understanding of the role of mitochondrial fission during early brain development and how unique DRP1 mutations result in varied pathological mechanisms.

extent to which the trajectory of emotion regulation dysfunction-associated neurobiology and behavior

sample of nearly 12,000 youth. By elucidating how and to what extent emotion regulation dysfunction

title: Regulation of Atherosclerosis by Matrikine Endotrophin
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: Narrative
The proposed research is relevant to public health because it focuses on establishing peptide endotro-
phin as a therapeutic target for coronary artery disease (CAD) and establish the mechanism by which it may
alter disease progression. We propose to utilize antibody-mediated neutralization and transgenic overexpres-
sion strategies in mice to investigate how endotrophin may signal to promote atherosclerosis. Thus, the pro-

activity_code: T32
title: Vision Sciences Training Grant
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: Project Narrative The goal of the proposed program is to support predoctoral students in vision research - to expand their understanding of current basic and clinical investigations on the eye and visual pathways, and to provide professional development in academic and science-allied careers. Areas of program strength include studies of higher-level influences on visual perception, of development and plasticity of visual circuitry, and of genetics and cell biology of retinal degeneration. This training is critical to the next generation of scientists who will tackle perturbations of vision at these levels.

application_id: 11241830
activity_code: F30
title: Dissecting the molecular mechanism of the cardiac fight-or-flight response via real-time light-controlled phosphorylation of Rad
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF IOWA
phr: Project Narrative:
Medications targeting upstream factors in the cardiac fight-or-flight response are vital for treating heart failure,
arrhythmias, and ischemic heart disease, but their lack of cardiac specificity often leads to undesirable side
effects. This research aims to uncover how phosphorylation of Rad, a key downstream regulator of this
response in the heart, controls calcium channel function. By identifying novel pathways for cardiac-specific
drug development, this study has the potential to improve treatments for cardiovascular disease and enhance
public health outcomes.

title: Population genomics and relapse epidemiology of Plasmodium ovale in sub-Saharan Africa
org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
While malaria control efforts have focused on other species, infections by Plasmodium ovale spp. are more
common than previously thought and rising in prevalence in sub-Saharan Africa. This proposal aims to employ
genomic and epidemiological methods to characterize the parasite population and determine relapse patterns
of this neglected malaria parasite. The foundational knowledge gained will inform relapse treatment guidelines
and provide essential genomic details for future research and surveillance of ovale malaria.

application_id: 11128805
activity_code: K23
title: Optimizing Telehealth to Improve Secondary Stroke Prevention
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Blood pressure control and physical activity in the first months after a stroke are known to reduce the risk of a
second stroke and improve recovery, but effective interventions and strategies to promote these factors are
lacking, particularly for ethno-racial minorities. We hypothesize that repetitive learning through self-monitoring
and motivational feedback with patient-tailored infographics can improve these preventive health behaviors
through enhanced self-regulation. Therefore, in alignment with the NINDS mission to reduce the burden of
neurological disease for all people, we will develop and test a scalable patient-centered telehealth-delivered
behavioral intervention for secondary stroke prevention in our underserved urban population.

application_id: 11136912
activity_code: K23
title: Opioid Dose Reduction in Comprehensive Inpatient Rehabilitation: A pharmaco-behavioral approach
org_name: SPAULDING REHABILITATION HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Pain is a common, highly distressing symptom for patients hospitalized in comprehensive rehabilitation that may
disrupt recovery and rehabilitative efforts (e.g., avoidance of physical therapy). Narcotic treatment is an option
for severe pain, but side effects and risk of addiction prevent its long-term use. Using quantitative and qualitative
methods, this project will assess the feasibility and acceptability of a pharmaco-behavioral intervention for pain
by applying principles of classical conditioning and open-label placebo among hospitalized patients and will
inform the science of managing pain and their clinical consequences amongst medical populations more broadly.

application_id: 11131108
activity_code: K00
title: What Mechanisms Underlie Top-Down Modulation of Human Visual Cortex?
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE What we perceive and hold in working memory is dependent on selective attention — a top-down cognitive ability that regulates the limited cortical resources available in the human brain from moment to moment. The proposed work will use a combination of cutting-edge non-invasive human brain imaging (fMRI and EEG), combined with computational modeling, to reveal the neural computational mechanisms through which attention regulates limited visuocortical resources. Understanding the neural substrates underlying attention in healthy adults is paramount for developing targeted interventions for brain disorders that disrupt attention, perception, and working memory — crucial cognitive functions in our everyday lives.
terms: Academic Training;Adult;Attention;attentional modulation;Award;Behavioral;Benchmarking;Brain;Brain Diseases;Brain imaging;BRAIN initiative;Bypass;cognitive ability;cognitive function;cognitive process;Communication;Computer Models;Data;Dependence;Detection;Development;Diagnosis;Disease;Down-Regulation;Electroencephalography;Ensure;Environment;Equity;experience;experimental study;Fostering;Functional Magnetic Resonance Imaging;functional magnetic resonance imaging/electroencephalography;Goals;Human;Impaired cognition;improved;information processing;innovation;International;Intervention;Intuition;Machine Learning;Maintenance;Manuscripts;Measures;Mentors;Mentorship;Methodology;Methods;Mission;Modeling;multimodal neuroimaging;National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke;neural;neural correlate;neuroimaging;Neurons;Noise;Oral;patient population;Pattern;Perception;Phase;Play;posters;Printing;Process;process improvement;Psychophysics;Regulation;Reporting;Research;Research Personnel;Resolution;Resources;Scientist;selective attention;Short-Term Memory;Signal Detection Analysis;Signal Transduction;spatiotemporal;Stimulus;symposium;Techniques;temporal measurement;Testing;Time;Training;United States National Institutes of Health;Vision;Visual;Visual Cortex;Visual evoked cortical potential;visual information;visual memory;visual processing;Work

application_id: 11131115
activity_code: K08
title: Developing Nanoparticle Drug Delivery Systems for Venous Malformations
org_name: BOSTON CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
An effective therapy with reduced risks and side effects for treating venous malformations is
greatly needed. We propose to develop an intravenous drug delivery system comprised of very
small (“nano”) particles that will convert to a tissue-targeting state when irradiated within venous
malformations. By our strategy, the accumulation of drugs at the disease site will be enhanced
while off-target drug delivery will be minimized.

application_id: 11141122
activity_code: K08
title: Optimizing Smoking Cessation Treatment for Patients with Bladder Cancer
org_name: SLOAN-KETTERING INST CAN RESEARCH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Bladder cancer is caused by smoking and continued smoking after diagnosis leads to significant
and preventable morbidity and mortality. Bladder cancer patients rely principally on their
urologist for medical guidance but urology providers do not frequently use evidence-based
practices (EBPs) that screen patients for smoking and provide them with the necessary
counseling and medications to quit. In this study, we will characterize use of smoking cessation
EBPs in the urology clinic then adapt and pilot a strategy to facilitate use of these EBPs in the
urology clinic for patients with bladder cancer.

application_id: 11141052
activity_code: K23
title: Sleep Disturbances and Circadian Misalignment as Potential Markers of Early Childhood Depression
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Early childhood depression symptoms are a significant public health concern as they are associated with
functional impairments and heightened risk for depression across the lifespan. This project will comprehensively
examine sleep in an ascertained sample of 3-year-olds oversampled for familial risk for depression, examining
whether objectively measured sleep/circadian rhythms are markers of early childhood depression and whether
sleep disturbances in infancy/toddlerhood are developmental precursors of early childhood depression at age 3.
This project represents an unexplored, but vitally important area of research that may have substantial impact
on our understanding of risk for early childhood depression, as well as our ability to identify children who may be
suffering ...

application_id: 11165985
activity_code: F30
title: The Effects of Evolution and Associated Differences in Dietary Preferences on Pancreas Architecture and Islet Function
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA AT DAVIS
phr: The proposed research is relevant to public health because there is currently no consensus on the relative
importance of differences in islet architecture between rodent and human islets to physiology. A comparative
approach bridging differences in islet architecture with functional changes in macronutrient sensitivity will address
this gap. Thus, the proposed research is relevant to the NIDDK’s mission because it directly tackles how
variations in islet architecture correlate with the macronutrient sensitivity of α and β cells within the islet, which
can be leveraged to inform therapies to improve the quality of life for millions of Americans afflicted with diabetes.

application_id: 11171627
activity_code: F30
title: Implementation and Effectiveness of Patient Reported Outcome Measures for Peripheral Arterial Disease
org_name: UTAH STATE HIGHER EDUCATION SYSTEM--UNIVERSITY OF UTAH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The application of patient reported outcome measures (PROMs) for peripheral arterial disease (PAD) patients
will allow for improved detection of health changes and incorporate a patient voice into PAD clinical care. This
study fills contextual gaps for using PROMs in the healthcare delivery for PAD, provides a rigorous and replicable
process for the identification of patient perceptions, and develops a strategy for meaningfully engaging
stakeholders in the deployment of implementation strategies. The achievement of the proposed aims will produce
a generalizable strategy to promote PROM clinical utilization and patient-centered care for PAD patients.

title: Bridge to the Doctorate at University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ILLINOIS AT URBANA-CHAMPAIGN
phr: Quantitative approaches, including data-driven artificial intelligence, can significantly help improve human health by providing innovative diagnostic tools and effective therapeutic strategies. Amblyopia is the leading cause of preventable vision loss among children in the United States. This project aims to identify vision screening policies that increase screening uptake, examine how factors influence access to eye specialty care, and develop predictive models to identify children with preventable vision loss. title: The Role of the Bifidobacterium Gut Microbiome Guild in Pathophysiology of Chronic Heart Failure with Reduced Ejection Fraction
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The disease course of chronic heart failure, a prevalent disease with high morbidity and mortality, is highly variable and our ability to predict progression on an individual level and prevent it is limited. Data from animals suggests that the gut microbiome, a community of microbes living in our gastrointestinal tract, may play a role in heart failure pathophysiology and disease progression. The goal of this project is to better understand disturbances in the gut microbiome, its interactions with the host and their joint impact on heart failure pathophysiology and disease progression in patients with chronic heart failure, which will identify novel preventative and therapeutic targets and improve risk stratification in this disease.

application_id: 11180165
activity_code: F30
title: Multimodal, multiclass prediction of disease status in Alzheimer’s
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA-IRVINE
phr: Project Narrative
Alzheimer’s disease poses a large public health challenge not only for the nearly 6 million Americans affected
but also for their families, caregivers, and communities. To enable better disease detection, this proposal aims
to develop a reliable, multimodal, and multiclass classifier for Alzheimer’s prediction of clinical diagnosis and
biomarker status. In the long term, achieving the proposed aims will establish a framework for earlier detection
of disease risk, improve stratification of patients for more effective clinical trials, and expand our ability to
differentially diagnose Alzheimer’s disease and related dementias.

application_id: 10914877
activity_code: K99
title: Mechanisms of corneal stromal activation during regeneration and fibrosis by exosomes
org_name: SCHEPENS EYE RESEARCH INSTITUTE
phr: NARRATIVE Currently, there are no effective tools for preventing or treating corneal scarring, which is a complication that potentially occurs with ocular injury, trauma, and/or surgery, and may result in decreased quality of life and possibly blindness. Extracellular vesicles (EVs), which contain cargo (e.g., RNA, protein, and lipids), are released by the corneal cells, and those released by the corneal epithelium can trigger myofibroblast differentiation, which will cause scarring and diminish vision if they persist. In this proposal, we will identify the EV-subset that is vital for corneal stromal activation and scarring, so that we may move forward with the development of novel therapies for the prevention and treatment of corneal scarring.

application_id: 11218658
activity_code: F30
title: Loaded Fibrin-Specific Nanogels for Sepsis-Induced DIC
org_name: NORTH CAROLINA STATE UNIVERSITY RALEIGH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The proposed research is relevant to public health because the current management protocols for disseminated
intravascular coagulation (DIC) focus on the underlying disease, rather than addressing coagulopathy, and result
in staggeringly high mortality rates for both humans and animals. We have developed fibrin-specific nanogels
(FSNs) that site-specifically deliver anti-clotting drugs while also enhancing fibrin cross-linking and formation,
providing a solution for both the prothrombotic and hemorrhagic DIC phenotypes. Loading FSNs with both a
fibrinolytic and an anticoagulant has the potential to provide an enhanced effect on improving DIC outcomes,
with the potential of overcoming the limitations of current therapies and saving lives.

application_id: 11223324
activity_code: F30
title: Investigation of neural ensembles underlying chronic pain
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA-IRVINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Opioid overdose has claimed the lives of over 450,000 adults in the United States since the turn of the century,
an epidemic fueled in large part by physicians’ reliance upon opioids for chronic pain management. Successful
mitigation of the opioid crisis is therefore dependent upon the identification of novel, non-opioid analgesics. By
identifying key neural circuits involved in pain chronification, this project will pave the way for targeted, circuit-
based therapies that effectively mitigate pain without the deleterious effects of long-term opioid use.

org_name: HARVARD UNIVERSITY D/B/A HARVARD SCHOOL OF PUBLIC HEALTH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 Due to a high prevalence of obesity and increasing maternal ages, a substantial number of women are
entering pregnancy in poor cardiometabolic health, leading to adverse maternal and infant outcomes. While
medications are available for obesity and related cardiometabolic conditions, the risk of congenital
malformations with many of these medications is uncertain. By rigorously quantifying the relative risk of
malformation after early pregnancy use of statins, angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitors, and
glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) receptor agonists this project will provide valuable information for clinicians
and women of reproductive age, enabling them to balance possible teratogenic risk with the known benefits of
these important medications.

application_id: 10807562
activity_code: K99
title: Prediction of dementia in older adults using nonlinear EEG features
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: NARRATIVE The goal of this project is to construct biomarkers for risk of dementia using multiple novel EEG measures and deep learning techniques. Using the existing large datasets of >8,000 subjects from four longitudinal cohort studies, the project will also test whether the constructed biomarkers mediate or moderate the genetic influence on the risk for dementia and can facilitate a better prediction of future cognitive decline and other adverse health outcomes. This project will potentially provide a non-invasive, cost-efficient tool that can be used to identify people at risk for developing dementia and to evaluate the treatments/interventions of the disease.

application_id: 11231214
activity_code: F30
title: Investigating the Mechanism of Hair Follicle Placode Induction : A novel approach to understanding establishment of the periodic pattern
org_name: PRINCETON UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
While growing fully functional skin in culture would greatly benefit a diverse array of patients,
including those with severe burns, slowly healing wounds, and advanced forms of scarring
alopecia, developing skin in culture with hair follicles that are correctly arranged in a spatial
pattern has been particularly difficult to accomplish. Turing’s Reaction-Diffusion model has long
been employed to theoretically explain periodic pattern formation; however, data from our lab
has unveiled striking plasticity in hair follicle placode formation, uncovering a robust program for
developing a periodic pattern. In this project, we will test the hypothesis that long-distance cell
migration, rather than local signaling, serves as an underlying mechanism for hair placode
induction.

application_id: 11235940
activity_code: F30
title: Investigating the mechanism of infection and therapeutic potential of Pseudomonas nucleus-forming phages
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO
phr: Project Narrative
 Multidrug-resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa infections are a critical public health threat and a desired
target for bacteriophage therapy. Nucleus-forming bacteriophages are a recently discovered class of
bacteriophage with high therapeutic potential due to their lytic nature and intrinsic resistance to multiple
bacteriophage defense systems. The research in this proposal will determine the genetic, biochemical, and
structural basis for protein import into the phage nucleus, as well as how these bacteriophages affect
pathogenicity and antibiotic sensitivity in P. aeruginosa.

application_id: 11238935
activity_code: F30
title: Determining the effect of DNMT3A loss on the competitive fitness of mutant cells in somatic mosaicism
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The human body is a mixture of cells that carry different gene mutations, which may confer tissue-context-
dependent advantages or disadvantages. This phenomenon of somatic mosaicism has public health relevance
because the expansion of cell populations with pathological mutations, such as those in the leukemia-driver gene
DNMT3A, can contribute to cancer and developmental disorders. We plan to model somatic mosaicism using
both cell lines and mice that we have developed with the long-term goal of identifying tissues in which DNMT3A
loss confers a selective advantage or disadvantage, as well as the mechanism of this differential tissue
composition.

activity_code: R25
title: Research Education Program to Promote Diversity in Immunologic and Allergic Diseases
org_name: WESTERN UNIVERSITY OF HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: Project Narrative  The R25 research education program provides mentored summer research opportunities to health professional students from diverse background who are qualified and highly motivated in biomedical research relevant to NIAID mission. The goal is to effectively train and inspire them through mentored-research experience with instruction in the responsible conduct of research, instruction in methods for enhancing reproducibility, grant and scientific writing, and research methods. The long-term purpose of this research program is to motivate the next generation of underrepresented and disadvantaged health professionals to continue their quest for knowledge and become a successful and effective researcher and integrate their knowledge in better management of patients.

application_id: 11241105
activity_code: F30
title: Outer sphere tuning of a Eu(II)-containing contrast agent for imaging hypoxia via MRI
org_name: WAYNE STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
The ability to image hypoxia has vital diagnostic, prognostic, and therapeutic implications. The proposed
research is relevant to public health because an innovative approach to mapping hypoxia using magnetic
resonance imaging will be made possible through advancing the persistence time of an oxygen-sensitive contrast
agent. Consequently, this project is particularly relevant to the NIH’s mission to (1) protect and improve health
and (2) ensure the Nation’s capability to prevent disease because the creative chemical approaches described
in this project will prove to be a major step in enabling europium-based contrast agents to image hypoxia in a
wide range of diseases both preclinically and clinically.

application_id: 11242044
activity_code: F30
title: Defining Inflammatory Triggers that Induce Diverse Subtypes of Gastric Metaplasia
org_name: SAINT LOUIS UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 Gastric cancer is a major cause of cancer related death worldwide and screening practices are not routine
in the US, leading to late diagnosis and poor outcomes. To improve accurate diagnoses and medical intervention,
different types of metaplasia need to be molecularly defined and the inflammatory signals that trigger
development of potentially high-risk metaplastic cells need to be established. The proposed research aims to
define types of metaplasia that 1. Arise in models of Helicobacter pylori (Hp) infection compared to models of
autoimmune gastritis (AIG), 2. Arise in human Hp compared to AIG patients, and 3. Are selectively
induced/expanded by inflammatory cytokines, to ultimately improve methods in gastric cancer early detection
and prevention.

application_id: 11015771
activity_code: F32
title: Structure-Property-Function Relationship in mRNA/CARTs for Targeted mRNA Delivery
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative The recently developed charge-altering releasable transporter (CART) gene delivery vectors exhibit remarkable efficiency for mRNA transfection and expression with site specific selectivity and low cytotoxicity. We propose to study the structure-property-function relationship of mRNA/CARTs complexes with advanced microscopy and scattering techniques to elucidate how physicochemical properties affect nanostructure. Distinct formulations will be further screened to relate biological function to structure allowing for the establishment of a set of design rules to aid the development of CARTs for efficient and selective immunotherapies.

application_id: 11235822
activity_code: F32
title: Mitochondrial therapy in conjunction with guideline directed medical therapy in cardiomyopathy
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The cornerstone of medical therapy in HF is guideline-directed medical therapy (GDMT), including beta-
blockers and renin-angiotensin-aldosterone antagonists, which address HF by decreasing energy demand, but
do not address energy supply, creating a need for novel therapeutics for HF. Cardiac tissue in patients with HF
exhibit mitochondrial structural and bioenergetic abnormalities, and mitochondria-rich extracellular vesicles (M-
EVs) from induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs) have emerged as a promising method to restore myocardial
bioenergetics and function. As there is potential synergistic valve in combined therapy, I propose a study
evaluating M-EV therapy in addition to select GDMT in HF.

application_id: 10795933
activity_code: K08
title: The Role of Lung Megakaryocytes in Airway Disease after Neonatal Hyperoxia
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ROCHESTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The incidence of premature birth (delivery more than three weeks early) is increasing worldwide and many infants born extremely preterm (less than 28 weeks' gestation) suffer long-term lung disease resulting from injury due to oxygen therapy and mechanical ventilation. These former preterm infants experience lifelong breathing problems, including wheezing and increased respiratory infection severity which puts a high burden on the health care system because of the need for continued care and rehospitalization. This project will contribute to our understanding of reasons why preterm infants and their oxygen exposures at birth leave them vulnerable to worse respiratory infections and wheezing later in life, in attempts to alleviate this burden.

title: Genetic and Molecular Mechanisms of hypermobile Ehlers Danlos Syndrome
org_name: MEDICAL UNIVERSITY OF SOUTH CAROLINA
phr: Project Narrative Hypermobile Ehlers Danlos Syndrome (hEDS) is a genetic disorder that causes symptoms affecting multiple body systems including the joints, skin, gastrointestinal tract, and heart. Despite its prevalence of 1 in 500 individuals, there are currently no reliable tests or treatments available for hEDS. The completion of this proposal will provide new understanding of the disease, opening new avenues for future studies and the potential for therapeutic approaches that will make a significant impact on the lives of those affected by hEDS and their families.

title: Clinical, Histological, and Transcriptional Associations Between the Diabetic Placenta and Fetal Congenital Heart Defects
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF FLORIDA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Congenital heart defects (CHDs) are the most common class of birth defect worldwide, affecting 1% of all
pregnancies and 4-5% of pregnancies affected by pregestational maternal diabetes mellitus (DM). Placental
dysfunction is at the core of many adverse obstetric outcomes seen in pregnancies affected by either, and is
thought to have a pivotal role in the development of fetal CHDs, particularly in the vasculopathic context of the
DM state. The scientific aims of this proposed training plan could inform future biomarker-based diagnosis, in-
utero therapy, and clinical risk management of pregnancies with maternal DM and/or fetal CHDs by comparing
clinical risk associated with these pregnancies and determining the molecular correlates of placental dysfunction
in each disease...

application_id: 11230108
activity_code: K99
title: Affective States and Large-scale Network Activity
org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: Project Narrative
Negative affective states are associated with several psychiatric illnesses including depression and substance
use disorders. The goal of this proposal is to use a systems neuroscience, whole-brain approach to study the
effects of negative affect associated with an innately aversive substance and with cocaine in large-scale
brain networks. Collectively, these studies will provide crucial information on how brain networks and key nodes
within them are altered by distinct forms of learned aversive states that can set the foundation for the
development of targeted treatment strategies to recover normal hedonic processing in psychiatric illnesses.

application_id: 11145076
activity_code: K23
title: The effects of cumulative concussion, repetitive head impacts, and comorbid cardiovascular risk factors on MRI metrics of pathological aging and neurobehavioral functioning across the lifespan
org_name: MEDICAL COLLEGE OF WISCONSIN
phr: Project Narrative (2-3 sentence re: public health relevance)
With an estimated 2.87 million TBI-related emergency department visits each year in the United
States, and approximately 69.1% of children ages 6-12 participating in sports annually, there is an
urgent public health need to identify the process by which mTBI and RHI can lead to neurological
health problems later in life. Once this process and modifying factors are identified, a comprehensive
approach can be employed to mitigate adverse long-term outcomes, such as preventative health
behavior interventions and the development of therapeutic treatments. Given this, the proposed
research is relevant to the NIH's mission to enhance health, lengthen life, and reduce illness.

application_id: 11015774
activity_code: F30
title: Role of ELOVL2 in age-related visual decline
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA-IRVINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) is critical in preserving healthy vision, and degeneration of the RPE can
lead to irreversible blindness. This project will investigate the basic biology of the RPE, its role in maintaining
the health of the retina, and how essential processes are affected during aging. Understanding the molecular
underpinnings of age- and disease-related changes in the RPE will inform the development of therapeutics to
combat blinding diseases.

application_id: 11245702
activity_code: F30
title: Utilizing phylodynamic and causal artificial intelligence methods for predicting and characterizing antibiotic resistant bacterial transmission within and beyond hospital settings
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF FLORIDA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
This project aims to confront the global challenge of antimicrobial resistance by employing modern genomic and
computational approaches to trace and understand multi-drug resistant bacterial transmission patterns. The
integration of complex data from sources such as whole-genome sequencing and electronic health records, will
enable high-resolution characterization of bacterial evolution and flow both within and beyond hospital settings.
The findings from this project will contribute to the transformation of infection control teams by informing the
design of actionable interventions to control hospital-onset outbreaks due to these emerging bacterial pathogens.

application_id: 11131263
activity_code: F31
title: Molecular mechanisms regulating cranial sensory development
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: Project Narrative
The complex sensory and structural defects of craniofacial disorders can be traced to an early period in
embryogenesis, with the emergence of four multipotent cell lineages at the neural plate border. Of these four,
the neural crest and pre-placodal lineages are most closely-related, respectively forming all facial bones and
cartilage, as well as cranial sensory organs and nerves, in parallel. The goal of this project is to expand our
knowledge of the distinct molecular mechanisms that govern placodal and neural crest progenitor fates by
examining novel roles for the Foxi3 transcription factor and extracellular BMP signaling during border cell
specification.

application_id: 11015782
activity_code: F31
title: Investigating the role of DNA structure in centromere specification
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Ectopic centromere formation on chromosomes is associated with developmental disorders and genome instability in cancer. The goal of these proposed studies is to determine how DNA structure plays a role in centromere formation at specific loci that lack conserved DNA sequence motifs to facilitate chromosome segregation. Characterizing the mechanisms of centromere localization will contribute to our understanding of how ectopic centromeres form and how they impact human health.

application_id: 11137185
activity_code: K99
title: Fingerprinting Spatiotemporal Patterns of Brain Maturation with Combined Diffusion-Relaxometry Microstructure Imaging
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Charting the continuous cellular changes of the brain is critical for studying neurodevelopment, yet it remains
challenging due to the limited sensitivity and specificity of in vivo imaging methods. This project will capitalize on
the latest advancements in imaging systems and computational techniques to develop practical tools that can
disentangle cellular microstructural and macromolecular development across the brain. This will enable a deeper
understanding of typical brain maturation and, ultimately, the origins and mechanisms of atypical development.

application_id: 11245780
activity_code: F30
title: Investigation of Chromatin Modifiers to Elucidate the Phenotypic Variability of Congenital Heart Disease in Patients with 22q11.2 Deletion Syndrome
org_name: ALBERT EINSTEIN COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE:
In 22q11.2 deletion syndrome, around 60% of patients present with cardiac defects, largely due to a deletion
that includes TBX1; this provides a unique population in which to investigate possible genetic modifiers of
heart development. Our lab performed whole genome sequence analysis on human subjects and discovered a
subset of chromatin regulatory genes associated with heart defects in 22q11.2 deletion syndrome. This study
aims to determine the mechanism by which Tbx1 interacts with these chromatin regulatory genes,
particularly Kmt2c and Kmt2d, in mouse heart development.

activity_code: F30
title: Molecular Mechanisms of TOX-mediated Programming of CD8 T Cell Exhaustion
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Chronic viral infections and cancer are leading causes of morbidity and mortality, but are difficult to treat because
responding virus- or tumor-specific T cells become characteristically dysfunctional, or “exhausted.” Since these
T cell responses are key determinants of clinical outcomes in these diseases, “immunotherapies” targeting these
exhausted T cells have been developed and reduce disease severity for some patients. To improve current
limitations in immunotherapy efficacy and develop new therapy strategies, this proposal seeks to resolve key
mechanistic details of how exhausted T cell function is restrained in these diseases.

title: Neuronal encoding of rapid categorical decisions across primate oculomotor networks
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CHICAGO
phr: Project Narrative
Our ability to rapidly and effortlessly categorize incoming stimuli is an essential cognitive process that allows us
to interpret the meaning of incoming stimuli in our environment and plan appropriate responses—abilities that
are disrupted or impaired by a number of brain diseases and conditions including Alzheimer’s disease, autism,
schizophrenia, stroke, and attention deficit disorder. Here, we propose to study how neuronal dynamics across
oculomotor brain areas are coordinated to achieve categorization and rapid decision making. We argue that
developing a detailed understanding of the neuronal mechanisms underlying visual categorization and
category-based decision making will enable a new generation of treatments for the cognitive and perceptual
impairments that accom...

activity_code: F30
title: Utilizing Plasma Proteomics to Identify Novel Biomarkers of Cognitive Aging
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Alzheimer’s disease (AD), a leading cause of morbidity and mortality in older adults, causes significant cognitive impairment, but current therapeutic efforts have not provided clinically meaningful improvements in cognition. The proposed research will use plasma proteomic methodologies to identify novel plasma biomarkers predictive of early cognitive decline in older adults without clinical dementia or stroke. Findings from this proposal will aid in identification of novel therapeutic targets for prevention and treatment of cognitive decline and AD.

activity_code: F30
title: Leveraging Clinical Data for Phenotyping and Predictive Modelling of Alzheimer’s Disease
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Alzheimer’s Disease is difficult to study due to its heterogeneity and lack of integration between molecular and clinical datasets. This research proposal aims to enable precision medicine approaches for Alzheimer’s Disease by utilizing integrative networks on a large set of clinical data to identify potential clinical and molecular associations. Ultimately, this project will facilitate the identification of hypotheses for disease mechanisms underlying heterogeneous clinical manifestations, providing a step towards improved patient stratification and personalized treatment approaches.

title: Predoctoral and Postdoctoral Training in Alzheimer's Pathophysiology
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF TEXAS MED BR GALVESTON
phr: Alzheimer's disease (AD) is the leading cause of dementia and imposes tremendous burdens on families, communities, and the health care system. Tragically, the population of Americans suffering from AD is expected to grow to 16 million by 2050. There is no cure, but insights into the pathophysiology of AD promises more effective treatments to ease symptoms, slow progression, and prevent disease. This is an application for the training of pre-doctoral and post-doctoral fellows in the area of Alzheimer's Disease (AD) related to neurodegenerative disorders pathophysiology.

application_id: 10876901
activity_code: F30
title: The role of ion channels and transporters in B cell function
org_name: NEW YORK UNIVERSITY SCHOOL OF MEDICINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE B cells are a heterogeneous population of immune cells necessary for the humoral response against infections such as influenza or SARS-CoV2, the long-term immune protection afforded by vaccines, and the pathogenesis of many autoimmune diseases such as multiple sclerosis. However, the predominant therapy targeting B cell biology is rituximab, which virtually depletes all peripheral B cells and leads to many adverse effects. A better understanding of how different ion channels and transporters contribute to various B cell functions may reveal a novel repertoire of druggable targets with which B cells can be immuno-modulated.

title: Child Intervention, Prevention and Services Research Mentoring Network
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: Improved prevention and treatment of major psychiatric disorders in youth is a national priority. This R25 targets the critical transition of postdoctoral fellows and junior faculty without research grant funding to successfully funded researcher through a one-week Research Institute and subsequent year-long mentoring process. Our career development approach is based on the model of other successful Summer Research Institutes that target researchers studying adults and geriatric populations but modified to give greater emphasis on grant design and writing and greater time to small-group and one-on-one mentoring with many of the most senior researchers in this field.

activity_code: F30
title: Harnessing polymicrobial interactions in the catheterized urinary tract to identify novel inhibitors of Proteus mirabilis urease activity
org_name: STATE UNIVERSITY OF NEW YORK AT BUFFALO
phr: Project Narrative Urease-positive organisms cause detrimental consequences among long-term catheterized individuals, including painful catheter encrustation, blockages, stone formation, all of which increase morbidity and mortality. This project aims to determine the mechanism of action of novel compounds that decrease bacterial urease activity and to conduct pre-clinical assessment of their therapeutic potential. This project will provide new insight into urease enzyme regulatory mechanisms and provide new targets for therapeutics that prevent catheter-associated urinary tract infection associated morbidity and mortality, without the use of antimicrobials.

org_name: DREXEL UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
 Effective diagnosis and treatment of substance use disorders in people living with HIV is a critical public
health issue as these disorders are disproportionately prevalent in this vulnerable population, enhancing HIV
neuropathogenesis and promoting behaviors that spread HIV. The cause of the change in neuropathogenesis is
not clear, but we hypothesize that addictive substances enhance HIV neuropathogenesis by increasing
dopamine, which drives inflammation in macrophages. In this F30, we will address this hypothesis by defining
the mechanisms driving dopamine mediated inflammation in macrophages, which will give the candidate, Ms.
Breana Channer, a comprehensive set of technical and professional skills that provide a robust foundation on

application_id: 11249113
activity_code: F30
title: Dissecting the effect of diet on gut microbiome metabolism
org_name: RUTGERS BIOMEDICAL AND HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: Project Narrative
 Dysregulation of indole and phenol metabolite production by the gut microbiome is linked with many
diseases. Increasing circulating concentrations of indole or phenol metabolites has shown promise in
ameliorating inflammatory bowel disease or obesity in mouse models, respectively, but there are not currently
methods to increase gut microbiome production of these important metabolite. This proposal aims to define the
relationship between diet and production of indole and phenol metabolites, with the eventual goal to create
dietary formulations to help treat microbiome-related diseases such as inflammatory bowel disease and obesity.

title: Investigating a potential cardio-placental signaling axis
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: Project Narrative
The majority of mammals rely on a placenta for in utero gas, nutrient and waste exchange. This organ must
grow and vascularize to nourish an embryo whose metabolic demands it cannot directly sense, but the
mechanisms underlying placental vascular development are poorly understood. The proposed studies will
investigate the role of a cardio-placental BMP10-ENG/ALK1 endocrine signaling axis in instructing the
formation of the placental labyrinth to improve our understanding of how this process is regulated.

application_id: 11250052
activity_code: F30
title: Oral pathogen - mediated pro-tumorigenic transformation through disruption of an Adherens Junction - associated RNAi machinery
org_name: MEDICAL UNIVERSITY OF SOUTH CAROLINA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Oral cancer 5-year survival is poor, due to late diagnosis and intervention. Periodontal disease, caused by certain
oral bacteria, compromises the oral tissue barrier and increases the risk of oral cancer, through still unclear
mechanisms. This study will investigate a novel mechanism tethering microbes, the oral tissue barrier, and
molecules called microRNAs (miRNAs), to explain this relationship with oral cancer risk and open the way to
novel preventative and therapeutic approaches.

title: Racial disparities in shared decision making for patients with acute respiratory failure
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative/Public Health Relevance
Patients with severe lung problems (“acute respiratory failure”) who require life support treatments like ventilators
often cannot speak for themselves, therefore their families and intensive care unit (ICU) clinicians should ideally
work together to make medical decisions on their behalf in a process known as shared decision making. We
know from outpatient clinic settings that shared decision making happens less frequently with Black compared
to White patients, but this has not been studied in the ICU among patients with acute respiratory failure, therefore
no tools currently exist to support ICU clinicians to engage Black families in shared decision making. In this
study, we will document the reasons for racial disparities in shared decision maki...

application_id: 10929999
activity_code: F30
title: SEMIColon: Somatic Exploration of Mosaicism in Colon
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF UTAH
phr: Project Narrative Diseases of the colon are widespread, with colorectal cancer the 2nd leading cause of cancer death in the US and inflammatory bowel disease highly prevalent and rising in incidence across the globe. These pathologies have distinct presentations and outcomes across the length of the colon, but the genetic etiologies of these differences are poorly understood and limit precision treatment. By understanding how somatic mosaicism differs throughout the colon and disease development, we will be better equipped to treat these pathologies, better prepared to identify at-risk individuals, and better able to study tissue-specific disease progression in other organ systems.

exacerbate neurologic complications of HIV infection (neuroHIV). The mechanisms driving HIV

candidate, Mr. Yash Agarwal, a comprehensive and diverse set of technical and professional skills that prepare

application_id: 11257644
activity_code: F30
title: Combining OCE and Brillouin microscopy to evaluate in vivo lens biomechanics in Presbyopia
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MARYLAND BALTIMORE
phr: Project Narrative
Presbyopia corrections so far have not addressed the causative nature of loss of accommodation, and major
barriers to innovation result from the need to measure biomechanics in vivo. The goal of this project is to
characterize the spatially-varying biomechanical signature of the lens by developing an in vivo measurement of
mechanics. Results from this study will provide a method to monitor and plan novel presbyopia treatments that
target biomechanics.

title: Epidemiological Analysis and Causal Modeling of Non-AIDS-Defining Skin Cancers in People with HIV
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF FLORIDA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
People with HIV (PWH) face an increased likelihood of developing non-AIDS-defining skin cancers (NADSCs),
yet the mechanisms driving this increase remain poorly understood. This proposal aims to assess
epidemiological patterns, uncover the interplay of causal factors underlying NADSC incidence and severity in
PWH, and develop predictive models to guide early intervention and personalized care. These findings will
inform efforts for skin cancer prevention, detection, and treatment, addressing critical health disparities in this
vulnerable population.

title: Utilizing Plasma Proteomics to Identify Novel Biomarkers of Cognitive Aging
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Alzheimer’s disease (AD), a leading cause of morbidity and mortality in older adults, causes significant cognitive impairment, but current therapeutic efforts have not provided clinically meaningful improvements in cognition. application_id: 11260138
activity_code: F30
title: Spatial smooth muscle cell transition states in atherosclerosis
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease (ASCVD) is responsible for plaque formation in arteries and leads to
heart attacks and strokes when plaques rupture, causing clots to form and block blood flow to the heart or
brain. Vascular smooth muscle cells in the artery wall form a protective, fibrous cap on plaques and comprise
the majority of cells inside advanced plaques where they assume the behavior of other cell types.
Understanding the processes that determine why smooth muscle cells leave the protective, fibrous cap and
adopt features of other cell types will help develop strategies to improve plaque structure and halt progression
in ASCVD to potentially prevent heart attacks, strokes, and deaths in patients.

application_id: 11120838
activity_code: F30
title: Life Course Developmental and Reproductive Predictors of Increased Mammographic Breast Density in Black Women
org_name: TUFTS UNIVERSITY BOSTON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Despite advances in breast cancer prevention and therapies, Black women exhibit increasing incidence and continue to have the highest breast cancer mortality rate compared to any other racial population. Mammographic density is one of the strongest breast cancer risk factors, yet, while Black women are more likely to exhibit higher mammographic density, unique predictors of mammographic density are severely understudied in this vulnerable population. Using the Black Women’s Health Study, the largest cohort of Black women in the United States, the overall goal of this proposal is to identify life course developmental and maturation risk factors for increased mammographic density.

application_id: 11285175
activity_code: F30
title: The impact of the neuronal Kv7.2 current on cholinergic control of pancreas secretion
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MIAMI SCHOOL OF MEDICINE
phr: The vagus nerve of the parasympathetic nervous system targets intrapancreatic neurons to modulate the
secretion of both insulin and digestive enzymes from the pancreas. This project is aimed at elucidating the
mechanisms intrapancreatic neurons use to respond to vagal input and regulate pancreas function. We expect
our results to (a) demonstrate that intrapancreatic neurons not only relay, but transform vagal cholinergic input
and (b) produce mechanistic insight into how local intrapancreatic neurons functionally impact endocrine and
exocrine compartments of the pancreas.

application_id: 10897232
activity_code: F30
title: Right Ventricular Remodeling in Tetralogy of Fallot
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: Project Narrative Tetralogy of Fallot is a congenital heart disease affecting 1 in 3600 births that is typically repaired with surgery early in life. Although advances in care have greatly improved patient survival, there is limited understanding of which patients will experience adverse events related to right ventricular remodeling, resulting in reduced quality of life, increased healthcare burden, and increased mortality. The proposed research introduces a multi-faceted approach using machine learning and computational fluid dynamics to identify the structural and hemodynamic parameters of tetralogy of Fallot that are associated with right ventricular remodeling.

application_id: 11291779
activity_code: F30
title: INVESTIGATING THE CONTRIBUTION OF OSTEOBLAST DIFFERENTIATION IN THE PATHOGENESIS OF OSTEOGENESIS IMPERFECTA TYPE V
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
This project serves to determine how the c.-14C>T point mutation in the 5’ untranslated region of the osteoblast
gene, IFITM5, causes the brittle bone disease osteogenesis imperfecta type V (OI-V). Specifically, this project
will investigate the role of the TGF-β signaling pathway in OI-V development and determine the efficacy of
therapies that target either the TGF-β signaling pathway or the OI-V causative mutation to promote normal
osteoblast development. This research hopes to identify a novel mechanism of OI-V pathogenesis and validate
the efficacy of novel OI-V therapies using preclinical models.

application_id: 11292424
activity_code: F30
title: Investigation of the Mechanism(s) of Beta and Delta Cell Coordination Under High Glucose
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA AT DAVIS
phr: The proposed research is relevant to public health because there is currently no clear consensus of the
mechanism by which beta and delta cells coordinate their Ca2+ and hormone secretion in response to elevated
blood glucose. The proposed mechanisms complement existing observations and link delta cells with distinct
patterns of Ca2+ behaviors to specific mechanisms of coordination. The proposed research is relevant to NIDDK’s
mission because it directly addresses the mechanism of beta-delta cell coordination, which is a crucial aspect of
islet crosstalk to regulate insulin secretion that can be leveraged to improve the quality of life for millions of
Americans afflicted with diseases of impaired glucose homeostasis.

application_id: 11294200
activity_code: F30
title: Improving bacterial cancer therapeutics
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Bacteria programmed to make anti-cancer drugs will grow inside tumors but not normal organs,
which means they can be used to specifically deliver drugs to tumors and metastases and
reduce side effects. However, bacteria don’t colonize human tumors very well, and we don’t
really understand why. We will use many strains of bacteria with mutations in different genes to
see which genes affect colonization of tumors, which will let us make anti-cancer bacteria that
are even more specific to tumors.

application_id: 11230675
activity_code: K99
title: Role of BMP signaling in cardiac neural crest development
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA BERKELEY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
This project will unravel how intrinsic genetic and extrinsic signaling factors jointly regulate the formation of a
specialized embryonic stem cell population called the cardiac neural crest, and its contribution to heart
development. These advances will be accomplished by performing genetic perturbation studies on mouse and
chick embryos, as well as human stem cell models, complemented by high-throughput sequencing data. The
resulting findings may point towards new avenues for early detection and treatment of congenital heart defects.

application_id: 11299472
activity_code: F30
title: Targeting the lipid kinase PIKfyve in pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: NARRATIVE
Although there is strong evidence for targeting lysosomal processes such as autophagy in pancreatic ductal
adenocarcinoma, this strategy has been underutilized because of challenges in characterizing favorable
targets and developing inhibitors. This proposal will define the metabolic impact of inhibiting PIKfyve, a lipid
kinase crucial to lysosome function, specifically on lipid homeostasis in pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma.
With the availability of two PIKfyve inhibitors that have cleared phase 1 clinical trials, this proposal will establish
a novel, rapidly translatable and mechanistically-informed combination therapy for pancreatic cancer treatment.

application_id: 11304518
activity_code: F30
title: Intracellular signaling mechanisms underlying opioid modulation of pain
org_name: OREGON HEALTH & SCIENCE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Opioids are commonly prescribed analgesics with high addictive potential, but we do not fully understand
the signaling pathways that cause analgesia versus addiction. Here, I propose to (1) investigate the
spatiotemporal dynamics of one opioid-induced intracellular signaling pathway and (2) define this
pathway’s role in analgesia. By identifying key brain regions and cell types in which this signaling occurs
and the direct functional consequence of this signaling, this work will extend our understanding of opioid
modulation of pain circuitry.

title: Nitrogen Metabolism of Staphylococcus aureus
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF NEBRASKA MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Staphylococcus aureus is the leading cause of bacteremia, infective endocarditis, and skin and soft tissue
infections worldwide. Understanding the pathometabolism of S. aureus niche-specific infection is critical to
develop new therapeutic strategies. This application proposes to assess the importance and effect of nitrogen
metabolism during the establishment of S. aureus infection.

application_id: 11313684
activity_code: F30
title: Differentiation of uterine tissue-resident natural killer cells
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Uterine natural killer (uNK) cells are thought to be critical for reproductive success, as abnormalities in uNK
cells have been linked to adverse pregnancy outcomes, particularly in uterine transplant recipients. Despite
their critical role in pregnancy, the developmental origins and differentiation of uNK cells in the uterus remain
unclear. This proposal aims to elucidate the origins and differentiation of uterine tissue-resident NK cells, with
the goal of improving reproductive health outcomes by bridging knowledge gaps in uNK cell biology that hold
considerable potential for identifying key therapeutic targets for patients with uNK cell abnormalities, most
notably uterine transplant recipients.

activity_code: K23
title: Developing a modified brief alcohol-focused intervention tailored for patients with alcohol use disorder in opioid agonist treatment
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF NOTRE DAME
phr: 8. Project Narrative Alcohol use increases the risk for treatment dropout, relapse, and overdose in patients receiving opioid agonist treatment for opioid use. This project will develop and evaluate a modified alcohol-focused intervention tailored to address alcohol use in patients in opioid agonist treatment who are struggling with alcohol use disorder. This brief and scalable tailored intervention will have the potential to decrease alcohol use and improve opioid agonist treatment outcomes with minimal resources, adding in an innovative way to efforts to address the ongoing opioid overdose crisis in the United States.

Gonadal androgens produced by the fetal testis during perinatal development establish a critical period of

mating and aggression. This proposal aims to characterize a novel action of 9-Tetrahydrocannabinol (THC), an

at the androgen receptor will enhance our understanding of how gestational exposure to cannabis can disrupt

title: Therapeutic efficacy and mechanisms of psilocybin for traumatic stress-induced anxiety and pain hypersensitivity
org_name: PENNSYLVANIA STATE UNIV HERSHEY MED CTR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Individuals with post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) experience chronic pain at a greater rate than the general
population, and those with comorbid chronic pain experience greater symptom severity and rates of opioid use
disorder than those with just one of the conditions. Evidence suggests that this elevated comorbidity and
increased symptom severity is due to overlap in pathologic neuroplastic changes of the nucleus accumbens
(NAc) observed in both conditions. As psilocybin and other psychedelics have demonstrated clinical efficacy for
PTSD, and convergent pharmacological action at the NAc, this project aims to gather translational data on the
therapeutic mechanism of psilocybin for PTSD, and evaluate its efficacy for attenuating traumatic-stress induced
increases in ...

application_id: 11360580
activity_code: F30
title: Aberrant cortical entrainment in biomarker-confirmed Alzheimer's disease and mild cognitive impairment
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF NEBRASKA MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Project Narrative
In recent decades, the United States has been undergoing a dramatic demographic shift towards an older
population, and with this increase comes a higher prevalence of age-related neurodegenerative diseases. The
most common neurodegenerative disorder worldwide is Alzheimer’s disease (AD), although much remains
unknown regarding the brain physiology underlying the disease process. This proposal aims to help fill this gap
in knowledge by training a future physician-scientist whose first major project will utilize a cutting-edge brain
mapping method to characterize the aberrant oscillatory dynamics underlying visual processing and visuospatial
attention in patients with AD and its common precursor, mild cognitive impairment.

title: Endocrine-Disrupting Chemical (EDC) Mixtures in Relation to Metabolic Syndrome (MetS) and Metabolomic Mechanisms in a Prospective Cohort of Hispanic Women
org_name: ICAHN SCHOOL OF MEDICINE AT MOUNT SINAI
phr: There is a paucity of studies examining longitudinally the environmental determinants of the rising Metabolic Syndrome (MetS) epidemic and its mechanisms. The present proposal will address the current research gaps in the field by focusing on a mixture of endocrine disruptors with potential contributions to MetS development in a prospective cohort of pregnant women. Findings will help advance the understudied, environmentally-induced etiology of MetS and open new avenues of research by examining unknown metabolomic mediators potentially driving these associations.

application_id: 11163527
activity_code: K23
title: Adaptation and Pilot Testing a Behavioral Physical Activity Intervention with Peer Support for Women with HIV and Co-Occurring Hypertension
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ALABAMA AT BIRMINGHAM
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Hypertension is a modifiable risk factor for cardiovascular disease and the most common comorbidity among
women with HIV; yet few lifestyle behavior interventions have been adapted for this population. Using a
mixed methods approach informed by social cognitive theory, the current project aims to systematically adapt
a physical activity intervention supplemented by peer support for women with HIV and hypertension. The
adapted behavioral intervention will be pilot tested to assess its acceptability, feasibility, and preliminary
impact on blood pressure.

application_id: 11169928
activity_code: K23
title: An EYE toward healthy aging in HIV: building evidence for multimorbidity screening and prevention
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Multimorbidity in aging populations is a major public health threat, and the impact is magnified in persons with
HIV, who experience a higher burden and decade-earlier onset of aging-related comorbidities. Despite this,
HIV primary care guidance does not specifically address managing multimorbidity and therefore, this proposal
leverages a well-established HIV cohort to assess multimorbidity cascades, clusters, and progression markers
to guide the development of clinical tools for multimorbidity screening and prevention. Completion of the
proposed training and research will foster the development of an HIV translational clinician-scientist and will
build evidence to inform strategies to promote healthy aging in HIV.
