activity_code: F31
title: High-resolution functional mapping of non-coding sequences regulating fetal hemoglobin
org_name: HARVARD MEDICAL SCHOOL
phr: Project Narrative: The reinduction of fetal hemoglobin is a therapeutic strategy for β-hemoglobinopathies such as sickle-cell disease and β-thalassemia. Characterizing the cis-regulatory logic of non-coding sequences that regulate fetal hemoglobin is critical for understanding hemoglobin switching and developing more effective therapies. Therefore, I will establish a high-resolution CRISPR mutagenesis approach to dissect the major non-coding sequences that regulate fetal hemoglobin.

acceptability of: 1) the multinutrient supplement; and 2) refined collection methods designed to reduce research

application_id: 10796784
activity_code: K08
title: Harnessing the EHR for the Delivery of Supportive Care in an Oncology Setting
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA LOS ANGELES
phr: Project Narrative  With nearly 17 million cancer survivors living in the United States and 1.7 million new cancer diagnoses each year, effective delivery of supportive care to reduce symptom burden and promote quality of life in cancer patients is critical. There is strong evidence demonstrating the benefits of supportive care at all phases of cancer care, but unfortunately, many barriers exist to integration into routine cancer care, leaving many patients without adequate support for common physical and psychological symptoms. This project will develop and pilot test a novel tool within the electronic health record system to facilitate systematic symptom assessment and the tailored delivery of supportive care in a real-world multidisciplinary oncology setting.

title: The Role of Intraoperative Transesophageal Echocardiography During Isolated Coronary Artery Bypass Graft Surgery
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The lack of definitive evidence (prospective randomized or retrospective observational) to support (or refute)
the use of intraoperative transesophageal echocardiography (TEE) during isolated coronary artery bypass graft
(CABG) surgery has led to widespread practice pattern variability and a near 50/50 split in TEE (vs no TEE)
intraoperatively – an evidence gap with clinical equipoise. This study aims to leverage this existing practice
pattern variability and clinical equipoise to generate the required foundational data necessary to inform a
future, multicenter randomized controlled trial (RCT) that could resolve questions surrounding the efficacy,
effectiveness, and risk-benefit ratio of intraoperative TEE during isolated CABG surgery. These findings will be
critically i...

org_name: ARKANSAS CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL RES INST
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Lymphatic malformations are congenital anomalies that can cause significant or life-threatening morbidity due to infiltration and compression of critical structures. Targeting Angiopoietin-2, a vital lymphatic growth factor, may have the potential to reverse the abnormal phenotype of lymphatic malformation endothelial cells and would have a substantial impact on the field given current therapeutic limitations. The goal of this project is to identify novel mechanisms underlying lymphatic malformation endothelial cell dysfunction which may provide suitable targets for the development of deliverable, molecular therapeutics for the treatment of lymphatic malformations.

title: Subdermal, implantable monitoring platform for heart attack and heart failure
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: Cardiovascular disease carries a high risk of recurrent heart attacks, frequently leading to heart failure if there is
no timely intervention from medical professionals. We propose a minimally invasive cardiac health monitoring

title: POWER: Predicting Obesity with Enhanced EHR Resources
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Obesity, heart disease, and diabetes risks begin very early in life, and finding new ways to promote healthy
growth can be of large benefit to public health by reducing the number of children who are already obese and
at risk for poor health. This research explores factors related to obesity that are present in infancy, and how

predict obesity and designs tools for clinicians to assess a child’s obesity risk in real-time and provide guidance
on healthy growth.

application_id: 11193453
activity_code: K23
title: Integrating Maternal and Child Health Services: Development and Pilot Testing of a Pediatric Clinic-Based Postpartum Contraception Intervention
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The goals of the proposed research are to develop and pilot test a patient-centered, pediatric clinic-based
postpartum contraception intervention that will promote equitable access to care. The relevance of this
research to public health lies in its potential to transform perinatal maternal and child health services to better
meet women’s postpartum contraceptive needs, and to therefore improve related health and social outcomes
for women and families. This research aligns with NICHD research objectives, including setting a foundation
for healthy pregnancies, improving child health, and the cross-cutting topic of health disparities.

application_id: 10931328
activity_code: F30
title: Ovarian Function Among Samoan Women with Obesity
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Through this F30 training fellowship, I propose to examine the association between adiposity and ovulatory dysfunction among Samoan women. Project findings will shed light on the dual burden of cardiometabolic and reproductive risk among Pacific Islanders, who despite having among the highest obesity prevalence globally, are underrepresented in epidemiologic research. This training proposal integrates new learning experiences in longitudinal data analysis, epidemiological study design and implementation, and reproductive endocrinology which will prepare me for a career as a physician scientist that conducts longitudinal epidemiological research to characterize how obesity affects maternal and child health outcomes in low-resource settings.

application_id: 10825894
activity_code: F31
title: Regulation of the retinoblastoma (Rb) tumor suppressor by the ubiquitin-proteasome system
org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: Project Narrative The retinoblastoma (Rb) tumor suppressor is a negative regulator of cellular proliferation that is inactivated through phosphorylation by the cyclin-dependent kinases 4 and 6 (Cdk4/6). Very little is known about additional mechanisms that inactivate Rb and therefore dictate its functional properties. This project will investigate how the ubiquitin-mediated degradation of Rb controls normal cell cycle progression and therapeutic response to Cdk4/6 inhibitors in the context of breast cancer, and my findings will broaden our overall understanding of how Rb’s tumor suppressive functions are regulated.

application_id: 11112289
activity_code: F31
title: Examining the Role of a Pathogenic HTT Isoform, HTT1a, in Somatic Expansion and RNA Aggregation in Huntington's Disease
org_name: UNIV OF MASSACHUSETTS MED SCH WORCESTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Huntington’s Disease – a devastating neurodegenerative condition – is caused by a defect in the Huntingtin
gene, resulting in the production of alternative forms of Huntingtin mRNA and protein. This proposal will use
small RNA drugs to reduce alternative forms of Huntingtin in mouse models of Huntington’s disease and
determine the effect on disease features and outcomes. Findings from these studies will provide insight into the
mechanisms underlying Huntington’s Disease to inform the development of future therapeutics.

application_id: 11135873
activity_code: K08
title: Mechanisms of Metabolic Dysregulation in ZZ Alpha-1 Antitrypsin Deficiency Associated Liver Disease
org_name: BOSTON UNIVERSITY MEDICAL CAMPUS
phr: Alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency associated liver disease is a significant cause of morbidity and
mortality with no specific treatments beyond organ transplant. This proposal intends to test the
hypothesis that ATF6 branch signaling within a subset of hepatocytes preserves homeostatic
hepatocyte metabolic function and has an associated reduction in steatosis. Through an
advanced understanding of underlying disease mechanisms new potential druggable targets
could be identified and represent novel future treatment options for patients with alpha-1
antitrypsin deficiency associated liver disease.

application_id: 11164775
activity_code: F30
title: Multisensory Integration and Cortical Plasticity in Cochlear Implant Users
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY
phr: NARRATIVE
Hearing loss is one of the leading causes of disability in America. Many profoundly deaf adults significantly
benefit from cochlear implantation, but the hearing outcomes following this procedure are highly variable. The
proposed study aims to investigate audiovisual integration and cortical plasticity in cochlear implant users to
better understand and predict cochlear implant outcomes.

application_id: 11213336
activity_code: K23
title: Gastric dopaminergic signaling and mucosal injury in Parkinson's disease
org_name: BETH ISRAEL DEACONESS MEDICAL CENTER
phr: The proposed research is relevant to public health because it seeks to address an under-studied
pathway driving gastrointestinal dysfunction in Parkinson’s disease, a major source of morbidity
for which there are no targeted therapeutic strategies. If successful, this project has the potential
to facilitate development of gastrointestinal biomarkers for predicting sequelae of Parkinson’s
disease and personalizing care, as well as helping identify novel therapeutic targets. Thus, the
proposed research addresses a critical gap in the gut-brain communications field and is relevant
to the part of the NIH’s mission to enhance health and reduce illness and disability.

application_id: 11139527
activity_code: F30
title: Bottom-up and top-down computational modeling approaches to study CMV retinitis
org_name: MEDICAL COLLEGE OF WISCONSIN
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Human cytomegalovirus (HCMV) is the causative agent of CMV retinitis (CMVR), which is known to cause
blindness. In this grant, I propose to develop a computational model of HCMV lytic and persistent-like replication
kinetics. The findings from this research will advance the understanding of HCMV biology and the pathogenesis
of CMVR for both scientists and clinicians by providing (1) a dynamic computational model of the complex lytic
replication cycle, and (2) predictive quantitation of the response of a persistence-like HCMV infection in adult
retinal pigment epithelial cells to antivirals.

application_id: 11230343
activity_code: K23
title: Social Media Challenges and Adolescents: Who is at Risk and How Can We Prevent Injuries?
org_name: RESEARCH INST NATIONWIDE CHILDREN'S HOSP
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Social media challenges are often dangerous events where people share content of themselves engaging in
risky behaviors and challenge others to do the same. Adolescents are particularly at risk for participation in social
media challenges and harmful effects include injury and death, but few studies examine this population for
necessary public health interventions. This proposal will be the first to identify risk factors for U.S. adolescent
involvement in social media challenges, assess for concurrence of other high-risk behaviors, and identify
evidence-based components for an effective intervention.

title: Chronic Monitoring for a Chronic Condition: Leveraging Digital Assessment to Determine Lifestyle Impacts on Cognitive Aging in Epilepsy
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO
phr: Cognitive impairment and risk for accelerated cognitive aging in older adults with epilepsy is a major public health concern, yet we lack effective strategies for optimizing cognitive health in this vulnerable and fast-growing group. This proposal aims to determine the unique contribution of modifiable lifestyle factors such as physically, socially, and mentally stimulating activities to cognitive function in older adults with epilepsy using innovative digital health technology to longitudinally track outcomes. Understanding the impact of lifestyle behaviors on cognitive comorbidities and premature cognitive aging in epilepsy has implications for targeted interventions, clinical guidelines, and policy.

application_id: 10984967
activity_code: F30
title: The thalamic paraventricular nucleus mediates the influence of early-life adversity on reward-seeking behaviors
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA-IRVINE
phr: Project Narrative Many serious mental illnesses, including depression and addiction, are characterized by disruption of the ability to experience and seek out pleasure and rewards. A clear connection has been established between these mental health issues and adversity early in life, and an understanding of the mechanisms behind this connection is crucial to identifying those at risk and implementing preventative or mitigative strategies. The proposed research will examine the neurobiological mechanisms by which the paraventricular nucleus of the thalamus, an important component of the brain’s reward circuit, contributes of early life adversity-provoked disturbances in reward seeking and mental health.

application_id: 11357383
activity_code: K23
title: A behavioral intervention to optimize self-management of celiac disease in adolescents
org_name: CHILDREN'S RESEARCH INSTITUTE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The primary objective of this research proposal is to refine and test the first behavioral intervention for teens
with celiac disease and their parents. Celiac disease affects up to one in fifty individuals and increases risk of
costly and negative health consequences such as cancers, additional autoimmune diseases, and a two-fold
increase in risk of death unless a strict gluten-free diet can be achieved (the only treatment currently available).
The proposed study has the potential to reduce these complications by addressing psychosocial challenges
that may interfere with quality of life and gluten-free diet management during adolescence, a critical
developmental period for optimizing long-term mental and physical health across the lifespan.

title: The Building Research Independence by Developing Goals and Hands-on Experiences (BRIDGE) Program
org_name: TEMPLE UNIV OF THE COMMONWEALTH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Undergraduate research experience improves retention of underrepresented students in STEM disciplines, but most research programs target undergraduates in their sophomore year, leaving a group of students who do not want research experiences until later in their training with few options. The Building Research Independence by Developing Goals and Hands-on Experiences (BRIDGE) program addresses this barrier by providing paid summer research experience, professional development opportunities, and mentoring to underrepresented students with no prior research experience going into their senior year. Importantly, the BRIDGE program will increase diversity in biomedical fields.

fundamental principles of quantitative light microscopy and computational analysis of biological
images. The course offers lectures, discussion groups and extensive hands-on laboratory work
using a wide range of conventional, advanced and emerging imaging techniques, and is

computer scientists. The short intensive nature of the course enables scientists to immerse in

application_id: 11212717
activity_code: K99
title: The contribution of the mammalian visual cortex to stabilizing locomotion
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Motor functions as basic as walking straight depend on vision, yet it is unknown how the largest visual
area in the mammalian brain, the visual cortex, contributes to stabilizing locomotion. This project
aims to establish a cortical circuit that utilizes visual feedback to provide ﬂexible control over
locomotion. Uncovering an adaptable mechanism for stabilizing locomotion will provide novel
insights regarding the ability of the visual cortex to overcome sensory loss during aging, impacting
both physical health and quality of life.

title: Understanding how exocrine-derived signals promote beta cell growth
org_name: INDIANA UNIVERSITY INDIANAPOLIS
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE How the exocrine influences the growth and function of the endocrine is an understudied area of research. org_name: UNIVERSITY OF TEXAS HLTH SCI CTR HOUSTON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Gene activation depends on the communication between the enhancer and the gene promoter. Transcription
factors are known to mediate and regulate this process through binding to the genomic DNA. This proposal
aims to investigate the role of transcription factors and genomic sequences in oncogene regulation, and further
model oncogene activation in human cancer.

title: Functional characterization of CUX1 regulators for the treatment of myelodysplastic syndromes
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CHICAGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Non-toxic and tailored therapeutic options are severely limited for patients with high-risk myelodysplastic syndrome (MDS). application_id: 11137761
activity_code: F99
title: Neural Progenitor Environmental Sense, Mitotic Time, and Mitotic Location and their Effects on Corticogenesis
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative: F99 Phase
Cortical development is a complex and spatiotemporally sensitive process, that when gone awry, has been linked
to a wide array of neuropsychiatric and neurodevelopmental disorders. Strict regulation of when, where, and how
the cell division of neural progenitors occurs is crucial for typical cortical development. This research uses
molecular biology techniques, spatial transcriptomics, and single cell sequencing on temporally labeled cells
from mutant mouse models to better characterize how progenitor signaling, division time, and localization affect
fate determination and cortical structure.

application_id: 10929736
activity_code: K08
title: Regulation of portal hypertension through neutrophil-platelet interactions in liver sinusoids
org_name: MAYO CLINIC ROCHESTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Portal hypertension is a common final pathway of chronic liver disease and is responsible for significant morbidity and mortality among patients with liver disease. The goal of this research is to elucidate the role of neutrophil-platelet interactions in driving microvascular thrombosis which we previously identified as critical in the pathogenesis of portal hypertension. This proposal is expected to identify clinically-relevant mechanisms to inhibit the neutrophil-platelet interactions which drive portal hypertension.

application_id: 11031001
activity_code: K99
title: The role of oral microbiota in metastatic brain tumors
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF TX MD ANDERSON CAN CTR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Brain metastasis, the spread of tumor cells from the primary tumor to the brain, is the most common type of brain tumor in adults and associated with a significantly poor outcome. Different microbial communities reside within our bodies including the oral cavity and can impact tumor development through changing the behavior of the immune cells. This proposal aims to improve our understanding of the role of oral microbes in the development and progression of brain metastasis, with the ultimate goal of providing novel opportunities to develop diagnostic, preventive, and therapeutic strategies to improve the outcome of this disease.

title: Photo-experiencing and Reflective Listening (PEARL) to Promote Healing Engagement for Survivors of Interpersonal Violence: A Feasibility Study
org_name: TEMPLE UNIV OF THE COMMONWEALTH

application_id: 11141753
activity_code: K01
title: Integrative Data Science Approach to Advance Care Coordination of ADRD by Primary Care Providers
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE/LAY STATEMENT
Patients with Alzheimer’s disease and related dementias (ADRD) require effective coordination to manage
numerous longitudinal care needs including diagnosis, exacerbation of symptoms, and to treat existing
comorbidities. Supporting primary care providers to manage and coordinate healthcare delivery among specialist
teams is important to improve outcomes and better quality of life for patients with ADRD. The goal of this project
is to develop approaches that monitor trends in care coordination among primary care providers and specialist
teams and recommend actionable opportunities for improvement, which will ensure that patients receive high-
quality multidisciplinary care necessary to maintain long-term wellbeing.

application_id: 10901641
activity_code: F31
title: Chemoproteomic discovery of secreted microbial enzymes for engineered probiotics
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA-IRVINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  The ability of human gut microbes to produce bioactive metabolites may provide a conserved signaling pathway for host-microbial communication. Microbial carbohydrate-active enzymes (CAZymes) are an exciting target for altering host-accessible glycan metabolites, which have been shown to be translationally relevant in a number of immune-active states including inflammatory diseases and certain cancers. Deciphering host- microbial dynamics through the chemoproteomic discovery of highly functional CAZymes could lead to the development of novel, engineered probiotics to selectively control glycan metabolite output in human host systems.

title: Getting Excited about Cortical Malformations: Discovering the Mechanisms Leading to Seizure-prone Neurons in Malformations of Cortical Development
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: Project Narrative Childhood epilepsy is a common and often devastating disease, and chronic seizures lead to a significant increase in premature mortality and developmental delay. A substantial proportion of children with Malformations of Cortical Development (MCDs) experience medically and surgically refractory seizures. This project will determine whether elongated axons, caused by known genetic changes, directly contribute to the untreatable seizures in children with MCDs and are thus a potential therapeutic target.

title: Mayo Clinic Research Education Program in Computational Autonomic Neurobiology of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases
org_name: MAYO CLINIC ROCHESTER
phr: Project Narrative
The Research Educational Program in Computational Autonomic Neurobiology is being developed at
the Mayo Clinic in Rochester Minnesota to train a new generation of modern biomedical scientists
and physicians capable of performing, interpreting, and applying analyses of increasingly complex
and large datasets. By providing participants with foundational knowledge and practical skills to apply
computer-based analyses to data from impactful research experiences, the program is poised to
advance understanding of diabetes and digestive and kidney diseases.

application_id: 11051759
activity_code: F31
title: Self-Assembling Peptide Nanoparticles for in vivo Genome Editor Delivery to Hematopoietic Stem Cells
org_name: HARVARD MEDICAL SCHOOL
phr: Project Narrative Therapeutic genome editing has shown great promise for treating inherited hemoglobinopathies such as sickle cell disease, yet current approaches require ex vivo procedures that cannot generally be conducted in parts of the world where the prevalence and mortality of these diseases are greatest. The proposed project will engineer peptide-based nanoparticles for the delivery of therapeutic genome editing agents to targeted cell populations in vivo, eliminating the need for resource-intensive ex vivo processing. This research could serve as the basis for the development of hemoglobinopathy genome editing therapies that can be used globally, and establish an in vivo gene delivery platform for the treatment of other illnesses.

activity_code: DP2
title: Deep phenotyping of fusion oncoprotein-driven pediatric cancer metastasis with single-cell proteomics
org_name: RICE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative: When cancers lack immunocompetent mouse models, we have limited access to evaluate
the effect of tumor-immune cell interactions on metastatic capacity. To address this gap, we propose introduction
of integrated engineered tumor microenvironments with multiplexed single-cell electrophoretic analysis capable
of quantifying protein complex expression, cell structure and motility. We will test specific hypotheses on the
metastatic phenotypic heterogeneity of Ewing sarcoma cells in the presence of distinct immune cell populations
in the tumor microenvironment.

Cancer Center (MCC) PartnErship for CancEr Disparities Research and Training (SUCCEED) program is to lead

application_id: 10687517
activity_code: DP2
title: A Novel Class of Synthetic Receptors to Empower the Age of mRNA Therapies
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: Relevance to Public Health
This project aims to create receptors that can detect a wide variety of inputs that represent cell
states and external cues, and produce arbitrary proteins in response. Such receptors would
enhance the precision of therapies where engineered cells are used to seek and destroy disease-
causing target cells. In contrast to prior art, our receptors operate at the RNA level, and is
therefore uniquely suitable for leveraging all the efficiency and safety benefits of RNA-mediated
delivery.

title: Acetate as a Mediator of Hematopoietic Stem Cell Inflammatory Response and Clonal Hematopoiesis
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Systemic inflammation is necessary for effective disease response mechanisms but has significant harmful effects to human health in chronic or dysregulated settings, such as clonal hematopoiesis, the overrepresentation of mutant hematopoietic clones. Hematopoietic stem and progenitor cells govern the response to these diseases and are responsive to cues associated with inflammation, therefore it is necessary to identify new inflammatory signals that influence these cells’ behavior. application_id: 11137661
activity_code: K01
title: ADHD and Alcohol Use: A Longitudinal Examination of Dual Processes
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF RHODE ISLAND
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Young adult drinkers with attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD), compared to their non-ADHD peers,
are at increased risk for experiencing alcohol-related problems and developing alcohol use disorder (AUD).
The proposed project assesses individuals both in the laboratory and in their own natural environment to study
associations between executive functioning, craving, and alcohol-related behaviors over a one-year period to
inform etiological models underlying ADHD and AUD. Findings will identify opportunities to tailor prevention
and intervention approaches aimed to reduce alcohol-related risks for young adults with ADHD.

application_id: 10796822
activity_code: F30
title: Determining the role of oxysterols in lymphocyte homing to lymph nodes in homeostasis and inflammation
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The mounting of an effective and timely adaptive immune response depends on the continuous entry of naïve lymphocytes into lymph nodes via specialized endothelium. The chemoattractants driving migration of lymphocytes across the endothelium into lymph nodes are poorly understood and elucidating the signals mediating this process is important for developing novel and improved immunotherapies that modulate lymphocyte recruitment. We will define the role of oxysterols, which are metabolites of cholesterol, as chemoattractants that promote efficient transendothelial migration of cells into lymph nodes, and thereby advance basic understanding of a fundamental immune cell recruitment process.

activity_code: K01
title: From synapse to function: cocaine-induced cell-type- and circuit-specific accumbal proteome changes
org_name: ICAHN SCHOOL OF MEDICINE AT MOUNT SINAI
phr: Project Narrative
Dysregulated signaling in the nucleus accumbens (NAc) is pivotal in promoting drug-seeking and relapse.
However, our understanding of synaptic proteomic adaptations, particularly in cell-type- and circuit-specific
contexts, lags behind our grasp of transcriptional responses to drugs of abuse. This proposal aims to explore
cocaine-induced synaptic proteome changes in NAc, focusing on their interaction with cocaine-induced
transcriptional and epigenetic regulation to elucidate their role in addictive drug-seeking and relapse behaviors.

application_id: 11003725
activity_code: K23
title: Network dynamics of sleep-wake states in epilepsy
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The relationship between sleep and epilepsy can provide key insights into how network dynamics influence epileptic activity; however, the mechanisms that underly this relationship are largely unknown. In this proposal, I will investigate this interplay by 1) probing physiologic sleep-wake states that are naturally associated with different epileptic potential in patients with epilepsy and 2) elucidating pathologic, state-dependent network features that are predictive of epileptic activity. This investigation will take steps toward a long-term goal of devising rational strategies for neuromodulation-based therapies that reconfigure the epileptogenic network toward having favorable dynamics with low epileptogenic potential in patients with drug-resistant epilepsy.

title: Novel Role of SEL1L-HRD1 ERAD in Neurodevelopment

application_id: 11137957
activity_code: K23
title: Cardiac Output and Renal Perfusion Alterations in the Development of Acute Kidney Injury in Cardiac Surgery
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Low cardiac output (CO) is known to impair renal perfusion and cause ischemic renal injury, but methods have
been unavailable to assess continuous CO and renal perfusion in cardiac surgery even though they may be
modifiable with available treatments to reduce acute kidney injury (AKI). This proposal will use novel methods
to measure and model continuous CO, renal perfusion, and AKI, to elucidate the timing of renal injury and the
parameters of CO and renal perfusion at which injury occurs during surgery. Importantly, this proposal will
develop Dr. Lee Goeddel as an independent investigator in the area of perioperative organ protection and
prepare him to lead future studies to substantially reduce AKI in cardiac surgery.

activity_code: F32
title: The effects of noradrenergic activity on age-related distractibility
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF SOUTHERN CALIFORNIA
phr: Project Narrative Age-related cognitive impairments co-occur with the disruption of molecular signaling within the brain, but the basic mechanisms of this relationship are unclear. In the proposed research project, we will investigate the mechanisms by which changes in the activity of the noradrenaline signaling molecule modulates the increased distractibility by task-irrelevant information seen in older adults and whether managing baseline levels can improve attentional control. The findings in both older and young adults will contribute critical knowledge on how the changes in molecular signaling activity in the brain modulates behavior, which will inform therapeutic approaches in how to control the activity of these signaling molecules to prevent cognitive decline.

application_id: 11015848
activity_code: F31
title: Investigating folliculin as a regulator of VLDL-TG secretion in non-alcoholic fatty liver disease and steatohepatitis
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: Project narrative Disease pathogenesis for non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD) and non-alcoholic steatohepatitis (NASH) remains poorly understood and attempts to modulate a singular hepatic lipid homeostatic pathway have been plagued by unexpected compensation and feedback loops. We have shown that loss of hepatic folliculin (FLCN) robustly protects against NAFLD/NASH via an FLCN-mTORC1-TFE3 signaling axis that controls de novo lipogenesis and fatty acid oxidation. We have evidence that this non-canonical mTORC1 signaling also regulates VLDL-TG secretion and we propose that upregulated VLDL-TG secretion is a major mechanism by which loss of FLCN protects against NAFLD/NASH.

application_id: 10744766
activity_code: K22
title: Augmenting cancer checkpoint immunotherapies via microbially-derived metabolites
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA-IRVINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Cancer immunotherapies including checkpoint inhibitors have revolutionized the treatment of multiple cancer types, yet these drugs are only effective in a small subset of patients. Although specific members of the gut microbiota have been positively correlated with patient outcomes, the mechanisms underlying these clinical observations remain largely unknown. Using a combination of chemical tools, biochemical characterization, and in vivo model systems, this proposal will examine enzymatic mobilization of microbial glycans from the gut as a common mechanism to prime host immune responses during checkpoint blockade, with the goal of augmenting the efficacy of current and next-generation checkpoint immunotherapies.

application_id: 11311990
activity_code: K23
title: Graft harvest as a model of tendon injury; Trajectory of recovery and clinical outcomes
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE There is a poor understanding of tendon healing in humans, which has led to a disconnect between the way treatments are designed for overuse tendon injuries and their use in clinical practice, placing patients at risk for long-lasting impairments, injury recurrence, impaired function, and decreased quality of life. Graft harvest (removing a portion of the tendon to use in another area of the body) provides an opportunity to improve our understanding of tendon healing and narrow the gap between research and clinical practice. Long-term, this will allow us to design better treatments and measures of treatment effectiveness, which will accelerate and enhance recovery from overuse tendon injuries.

application_id: 10766124
activity_code: F30
title: Integrating common and rare genetic variation in autism spectrum disorder
org_name: HARVARD MEDICAL SCHOOL
phr: Project Narrative Autism spectrum disorder (ASD) is common, often disabling, and lacks pharmacologic treatment for its core symptoms: impairment in social communication and restricted/repetitive behavior. We propose to apply three novel statistical and functional approaches to interpret ASD's common polygenic influences, with an emphasis on assessing the convergence of (i) polygenic risk and (ii) the more than 70 genes that have been associated with ASD through exome sequencing studies. These activities will improve our understanding of the genetic underpinnings of ASD, strengthen biological and therapeutic hypotheses, and generate methods applicable to the study of other neuropsychiatric conditions.

application_id: 11004260
activity_code: K08
title: Interstitial macrophages in cigarette smoke-induced small airway remodeling
org_name: NATIONAL JEWISH HEALTH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Cigarette smoking-related lung diseases like chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) remain a leading cause of death worldwide. We believe Lyve-1 positive peribronchial interstitial macrophages play a key role in promoting the pathologic thickening of the small airway walls in response to cigarette smoke and may be a potential therapeutic target to intervene in COPD pathogenesis. We will test the hypothesis that matrix metalloprotein 9 production in response to Lyve-1 on the IM surface binding to hyaluronic acid promotes airway wall remodeling and fibrosis after prolonged cigarette smoke exposure.

application_id: 11214404
activity_code: K99
title: Decoding therapeutic, cross-presented tumor antigens in pancreatic cancer
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA BERKELEY
phr: Project Narrative
Identifying therapeutically efficacious tumor antigens remains a significant challenge in cancer vaccine
development for pancreatic cancer. The essential role of antigen cross-presentation by dendritic cells to elicit
anti-tumor T cell immunity highlights the clinical value of antigen discovery strategies to identify efficiently cross-
presented tumor antigens. Here, I proposed a multipronged approach to evaluate how the tumor antigenic
landscapes on dendritic cells are modulated by cancer-intrinsic and extrinsic mechanisms, and how this underly
the heterogeneity of T cell infiltration in both human and mouse pancreatic cancer.

application_id: 11123095
activity_code: F31
title: A translational approach to interrogate the gut microbiome in sepsis-associated acute kidney injury
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Acute kidney injury in sepsis is common, costly, and responsible for high mortality and long-term morbidity. In
preliminary studies of experimental sepsis and hospitalized patients, we have discovered that the gut
microbiome plays an important but poorly understood role in sepsis-associated acute kidney injury. This project
aims to understand how the gut microbiome contributes to the pathogenesis of kidney injury in sepsis, which
will help provide insights that may lead to advances in its prevention and treatment.

title: Circuit dynamics underlying perceptual learning in the functionally organized visual cortex
org_name: MAX PLANCK FLORIDA CORPORATION
phr: Project Narrative We will employ cutting-edge imaging techniques to investigate the dynamic interplay that takes place in the adult brain between cortical elements over the course of learning. application_id: 10798126
activity_code: F31
title: Neural Basis of Math and Aphasia
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: Project Narrative Despite the importance of math skills in daily life tasks and the role of mathematics as a symbolic communication system, relatively little is known about the neural correlates of math or its interaction with language processing in the brain. The proposed research will explore this relationship by utilizing the lesion method and converging with a functional dataset, which will inform cognitive models of how numeracy abilities are organized in the brain and linked to language areas during both functional and disordered performance. title: Targeting HMGA1-Mediated Progression and Immune Evasion in Myeloproliferative Diseases
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Myeloproliferative neoplasms (MPN) are clonal blood disorders characterized by overproduction of normal blood cells and an increased risk for progression to bone marrow scarring (fibrosis) or leukemia. Importantly, fibrosis or leukemia in MPN patients is highly lethal. I propose to focus on a key regulator, the High Mobility Group A1 (HMGA1) protein, because our preliminary data strongly suggest that HMGA1 is required for disease progression; my long-term goal is to identify pathways that could be blocked in therapy to treat, or even prevent, progression in patients with MPN.

org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: Project Narrative During division, cells must accurately segregate their genome into two equal daughter cells, as errors can impact cell health and lead to diseases such as cancer. application_id: 10822347
activity_code: F31
title: Endothelial NOX1-Mediated AMPKa1 Propagates Hypoxia-Induced Pulmonary Hypertension via the CIP/KIP family of CDK inhibitors
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: Project Narrative Right heart failure (RHF) is the leading cause of death among patients diagnosed with pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH). Consequently, RHF is caused by increased lung arterial resistance and, hence, thickening and blockage of pulmonary arteries. Despite approved therapies to treat PAH, RHF contributes to greater than 50% of patient deaths in this disease. This project aims to open a new field of inquiry of PAH by identifying a novel pathway in arterial cell proliferation in PAH. By extension, new druggable inhibitors are expected to target mediators in this pathway with the goal of mitigating PAH.

application_id: 11138294
activity_code: K08
title: Molecular characterization of ascites fluid to identify drivers of infection in patients with decompensated cirrhosis
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: Project Narrative
In patients with liver cirrhosis, infection is directly responsible for 30-50% of all deaths, the most common
infection being spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP) – a life-threatening, difficult to detect infection that
develops in excess abdominal fluid, known as ascites fluid. An improved understanding of the microbial and
host features in cirrhotic ascites that drive the susceptibility to SBP is central to improving outcomes. This work
applies novel methods with the potential to uncover unidentified host and microbial attributes with the potential
to predict outcomes in cirrhosis, and inform new treatments and interventions.

activity_code: F31
title: The role of BET proteins in pathological cardiac remodeling
org_name: OHIO STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: NARRATIVE Fibrosis in the heart is bad and contributes to poor survival for patients with heart failure, which afflicts millions of Americans, yet no effective treatments for fibrosis in the heart are available. Using special (genetically modified) mice, the proposed study will determine if targeting a specific protein, BRD4, in fibroblasts might work as a treatment for fibrosis in the heart and will determine if other proteins in the same family are important for turning on and off specific genes in the heart. Together, this work will help us understand how cells in the heart become disease activated and help us progress toward finding better treatments for heart failure.

title:  nbInnate Immunity Responses In Monocytes: Contribution To Neurodegeneration

phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  Alzheimer’s disease (AD) is the leading cause of dementia worldwide in older individuals, while HIV associated neurocognitive disorder (HAND) is prevalent in 20-50% of people with HIV, who live longer due to successful antiviral therapy, being the leading cause of dementia in younger individuals. title: A Multilevel Social Capital Approach to Address Cervical Cancer Screening and Follow Up Delays among Latinas in Safety-Net Settings
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF SOUTHERN CALIFORNIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Higher social capital (resources rooted in social networks) is positively associated with cervical cancer screening among Latinas, but how this association operates by nativity status and how clinic-based social capital interventions can be implemented in safety-net settings to address cervical cancer disparities among this population has not been explored. activity_code: K99
title: Subtyping tinnitus based on fMRI dynamic functional connectivity
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: Subjective tinnitus, defined as perceiving sound in the absence of actual sound affects 10% to 30% of the world population and at least half of tinnitus patients experience comorbidities such as cognitive dysfunction, or depression. This study aims to identify subtypes of tinnitus using state-of-the art neuroimaging, brain connectivity and machine learning methods. Subtyping tinnitus based on dynamic functional connectivity of brain networks would allow more objective, accurate and specific diagnosis, thus being an important step towards personalized tinnitus treatment.

application_id: 11211155
activity_code: F30
title: Interferon-Stimulated Gene 15 as a Novel Regulator of Cell Death DuringAcute Kidney Injury
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Acute kidney injury (AKI) is a common clinical condition with high mortality rates, long-term impact on
patients’ health and quality of life, and no definitive treatments that mitigate the injury or accelerate recovery.
This study aims to advance our understanding of the fundamental pathogenesis of ischemia-reperfusion
AKI, as well as elucidate the novel role of Interferon-Stimulated Gene 15 as a host protective factor
regulating cell death and inflammation during AKI. This knowledge could then be used to develop novel
therapeutic targets and innovative strategies to treat AKI, with the potential to impact millions of lives.

application_id: 11241245
activity_code: F31
title: Investigating the developmental programming of hematopoietic stem and progenitor cells
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WISCONSIN-MADISON
phr: Project Narrative
Hematopoietic stem and progenitor cells (HSPCs) are regulated by their microenvironment, however the
reprogramming signals they receive from the niche during development are not well understood. My research
will use an integrin alpha 4 mutant zebrafish model that prevents HSPC-niche interactions during development,
allowing dissection of the gene regulatory networks that control the transition of HSPCs to quiescence. Our
findings will link cell extrinsic signals to intrinsic changes in HSPCs that regulate the switch between proliferation
and quiescence, potentially transforming our approaches for in vitro stem cell expansion and treatment of
hematologic malignancies.

activity_code: F31
title: Quantifying the contribution of supracellular actomyosin networks during mouse neural tube closure
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  Supracellular actomyosin networks are used to fold a diverse range of tissues during development, like neural tube closure. Errors during neural tube closure cause congenital malformations in humans, but we lack a complete understanding on how actomyosin networks contribute to neural tube closure. We propose test a mechanistic model of mammalian neural tube closure which will provide insight on the generalizability of actomyosin-mediated folding mechanisms and create a conceptual foundation from which to understand, prevent, and treat congenital malformations.

title: Tumor-TAMs crosstalk enables bypass of oncogenic KRAS dependency in pancreatic cancer

phr: Project Narrative Tumor associated macrophages (TAMs) play an important role in tumor progression, metastasis, immune suppression and therapy resistance, but their function in oncogenic KRAS inhibition resistance is largely unknown. title: Sex difference in conjunctival epithelial cell response and their profile of extracellular bioactive lipids duringallergic inflammation
org_name: SCHEPENS EYE RESEARCH INSTITUTE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The sex difference in the profile of extracellular bioactive lipids, which mediate the progression and resolution of inflammation, is linked to the sex-dependent differential cellular response, and incidence and severity of various diseases. application_id: 11016980
activity_code: F31
title: Characterizing subsets of HIV-infected and uninfected CD14+CD16+ monocytes that contribute to neuropathogenesis
org_name: ALBERT EINSTEIN COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: NARRATIVE 38 million people worldwide are living with HIV. Despite successful antiretroviral therapy (ART), 15-40% of these people with HIV develop HIV-associated neurocognitive impairments (HIV-NCI) that gravely impact morbidity and mortality, for which there is no effective therapy in the ART era. Since HIV-infected intermediate monocytes are major contributors to neuropathogenesis and the development of HIV-NCI, defining and characterizing novel subsets is paramount to identifying new targets for potential therapies.

title: Myeloid TLR4 epigenetic regulation and signaling in accelerating venous thrombus resolution
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE When blood clots form in the large veins, a common condition known as deep vein thrombosis (DVT), patients are treated with blood thinning medications, which carry the risk of bleeding and are ineffective at dissolving the clot, ultimately leading to the risk of recurrent DVT and chronic leg swelling, pain and ulcers (post thrombotic syndrome). activity_code: F31
title: High-throughput thermodynamic and kinetic measurements for variant effects prediction in a major protein superfamily
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Missense mutations directly cause or exacerbate human diseases by introducing changes to the amino acid sequence of a protein that disrupt its native biophysical properties. Attempts to predict these functional effects from sequence variation have leveraged recent advancements in sequence-based machine learning, but have suffered from a lack of large, biochemical datasets connecting sequence variance to descriptive measurements of in vitro effects. Here, I will develop a cutting-edge experimental strategy to make quantitative biophysical measurements for thousands of mutations in a major class of human proteins and establish a framework for leveraging this data to enhance sequence-based predictive models for variant effects prediction.

application_id: 11101105
activity_code: F32
title: Targeting the cancer neo-genome for destruction with CRISPR-Cas enzymes
org_name: DANA-FARBER CANCER INST
phr: Project Narrative
Despite the presence of thousands of mutations in the cancer genome, including over one hundred structural
variants per genome, we lack a targeted therapy to treat many cancers. This proposal is designed to take
advantage of the multiple structural variants in the cancer genome and use them as unique genomic sites for
CRISPR nuclease targeting and cleavage, which in turn could lead to cancer cell cytotoxicity. This proposal will
design, validate and characterize a multi-cut CRISPR approach to cause cytotoxicity in targeted cancer cells,
while leaving normal cells intact, and is the first step in developing this approach to becoming a broad-
spectrum cancer therapeutic in the future.

title: Cholesterol modulation of BK currents and cerebral artery diameter via channel-forming slo1 subunits
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF TENNESSEE HEALTH SCI CTR
phr: The goal of this proposal is to determine the molecular mechanisms that govern cholesterol regulation of the protein termed calcium- and voltage-gated potassium channel of large conductance (BK), and the impact of this regulation on blood vessel diameter in the brain. title: Impact of ambient PM2.5 concentrations on fear extinction recall, frontolimbic circuitry, and anxiety in adolescents
org_name: WAYNE STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Despite the emerging link between air pollution exposure – specifically PM2.5 – and heighted risk of adolescent anxiety, the neurobehavioral mechanisms underlying this association are unknown. This project will be an important first step towards identifying mechanisms underlying environmental risk of psychopathology and will lay a critical foundation for early interventions to stem the etiology of anxiety in at-risk pollution-exposed youth.

title: Elucidating Thiol Reductive Stress in Lung Cancer Growth and Metastasis
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE NRF2 is hyperactivated by oncogenic mutation in ~30% of non-small cell lung cancers (NSCLCs), a destructive cancer with high rates of metastasis. NRF2 coordinates cellular antioxidant responses and promotes an environment that supports cell growth and metastasis, but its overactivation leads to reductive stress in a subset of NSCLCs. activity_code: F31
title: Probing Molecular Mechanisms of Allosteric Inhibition of HIV-1 Reverse Transcriptase
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Non-nucleoside inhibitors of HIV reverse transcriptase (RT) remain successful components of combined antiretroviral therapy regimens and have newly discovered HIV-specific cell killing activity, which makes them potential components of future HIV cure strategies. title: Characterizing the genetic etiology of delayed puberty with integrative genomic techniques
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: Narrative This project will evaluate the genetic etiology of both rare and common forms of infertility and delayed puberty. application_id: 11242024
activity_code: F32
title: Mutational scanning of seizure-related gene para in fruit flies
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The clinical relevance of most missense variants observed in human genes is currently unknown, because so
many possible variants exist and most have not been observed in the clinic enough times for their effect to be
known. The proposed work will enable the rapid phenotyping of thousands of gene variants in parallel in a
multicellular eukaryotic organism for the first time and will provide evidence for clinical classification of over 1,000
variants of the D. melanogaster ortholog of the very closely conserved human SCN1A gene. This technology will
improve disease screening, diagnosis, and prognosis by rapidly characterizing and annotating which variants
seen in the human genome contribute to pathogenicity.

title: Pediatric Training Program Hematology and Oncology
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE This program is designed to train the next generation of leaders in pediatric hematology-oncology biomedical science. title: The relationship between HIV exposure, contact networks, and TB in children under 5 years old
org_name: BOSTON MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Tuberculosis (TB) is a top-ten killer of children worldwide and is viciously synergistic with the global HIV pandemic. The projects proposed here will use unique databases and novel social network and ethnographic approaches to quantify and describe the relationship between HIV, TB, and children’s social environments. The results will inform child TB case detection strategies and policy to close the pediatric TB detection and treatment gaps in high HIV/TB-burden settings.

application_id: 11023088
activity_code: K08
title: Understanding and improving responses to adaptive NK cell therapy for leukemia and multiple myeloma
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MINNESOTA
phr: Project Narrative
Natural killer (NK) cell therapy for acute leukemia and multiple myeloma is limited by lack of NK cell
persistence and loss of function. A subset of NK cells, called adaptive NK cells, is longer-lived and exhibit
enhanced functional responses against tumor cells. This proposal will investigate the use of adaptive NK cells
to treat leukemia and myeloma and test methods to retain their function after exposure to tumor cells.

title: Momentary Geospatial, Psychological, and Behavioral Risk for Substance Use in Young Adults Under Criminal Justice Supervision
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ALABAMA AT BIRMINGHAM
phr: Project Narrative Young adults under criminal justice supervision are at an increased risk for development of substance use disorders; however, there is limited understanding of the real-time contextual, psychological, and behavioral antecedents of substance use in vulnerable young adults. This project will test the momentary psychological, behavioral, and geospatial risk for substance use in young adults enrolled in drug treatment court (DTC) by integrating geographically-explicit ecological momentary assessment (GEMA) data with risk terrain modeling (RTM). application_id: 11031940
activity_code: K99
title: Encoding of Bodily State and Motor Action in a Minimal Circuit for Food Consumption
org_name: ROCKEFELLER UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATION It seems obvious that when our body is low in energy we will eat, however, it is not known how the sensation of the bodily energy state transforms into the movements of chewing and biting. This interplay of signals reflecting the state of the body and control of muscles and movement that make up behavior is a tightly regulated process, and any decoupling will result in either obesity or eating disorders. Thus, this proposal aims to determine the computations that a simple brain circuit performs to control biting and chewing of food with respect to changes in energy state of the body.

title: Dissecting the role of the PICALM/EED locus on myeloid cells in Alzheimer's disease.
org_name: ICAHN SCHOOL OF MEDICINE AT MOUNT SINAI
phr: Project Narrative
While genetic studies have identified more than 75 genetic loci implicated in Alzheimer’s disease risk, the
underlying causal genes and biological mechanisms involved in disease development remain unclear. This
proposal combines patient-derived induced pluripotent stem cells, genome editing, and innovative
transplantation techniques to experimentally investigate the impact of prioritized genes and enhancers on the
cellular function of disease-relevant microglia both in vitro and in vivo. This approach will translate findings of
genomic studies from statistical associations to biological function and enhance our understanding of the
contribution of PICALM/EED locus to disease development.

application_id: 11243469
activity_code: F32
title: Developmental mechanisms specifying vagal innervation of organ targets
org_name: FRED HUTCHINSON CANCER CENTER
phr: Project Narrative:
Communication between the brain and body is important for organ function, and is facilitated by a group of
neurons in the brainstem which form the vagus nerve, responsible for sending signals back and forth between
the organs and the central nervous system. Despite this crucial function, the molecular mechanisms guiding
subsets of vagus neurons to form synaptic connections with the appropriate target organs during development
remain unknown. Through the following Aims, I propose to use genetics, live imaging and transcriptional analysis
to identify the developmental mechanisms directing vagal motor (Aim 1) and sensory (Aim 2) neurons to two
discreet organ targets in the zebrafish embryo: the heart and the gut.

application_id: 11080249
activity_code: K01
title: Leveraging multi-omics for endotyping to identify subtypes and mechanisms of cardiometabolic diseases
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Project Narrative
Individuals who develop cardiometabolic diseases may not have elevations in risk factors suggesting that there
are other risk mediators underlying diseases that are not yet identified. This project will use cutting-edge and
innovative multi-omics approaches which examine many factors measured in blood to identify groups of
individuals who are biologically similar and will determine whether the groups have different functional and/or
pathophysiological risk mechanisms for disease or different response to treatments. These groups can be
further studied to determine their risk mechanisms, and this will ultimately lead to new personalized ways of
managing and preventing cardiometabolic diseases.

application_id: 11218685
activity_code: F32
title: Daily and circadian regulation of respiratory plasticity in freely behaving rats
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF FLORIDA
phr: Acute intermittent hypoxia, or repetitive periods of low oxygen, has shown great promise as a therapeutic in
ongoing clinical trials to improve breathing (and non-respiratory) function in individuals with spinal cord injury
and amyotrophic lateral sclerosis. The proposed project will investigate whether behavioral and physiological
factors stemming from an individual’s biological clock influence the effectiveness of acute intermittent hypoxia in
improving breathing function. These studies will provide important new biological insights for the use of acute
intermittent hypoxia as a therapeutic strategy in humans.

application_id: 11124032
activity_code: F32
title: A child's best friend: Behavioral, neural, and endocrinological mechanisms of longitudinal social bond formation and stress buffering effects in children and pet dogs
org_name: HARVARD UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Attachment bonds between children and family dogs can result in positive therapeutic outcomes in both typical
and clinical populations, including reduction of child stress and anxiety when the dog is present. These
outcomes are likely mediated by the strength of the child-dog bond, so identifying the biological mechanisms
that support the formation of strong, successful bonds is foundational to maximizing these therapeutic effects.
The current proposal longitudinally examines the development of behavioral, neural, and endocrine
mechanisms that underlie the stress- and anxiety-reducing effects of social bonds between children and their
pet dogs.

activity_code: K01
title: Sexual network structure and HIV testing and treatment in sub-Saharan Africa: understanding the implications for ending the HIV epidemic.
org_name: PENNSYLVANIA STATE UNIV HERSHEY MED CTR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Despite clear evidence at the couple-level that HIV test-and-treat strategies are successful at reducing HIV transmission, population-level trials have shown mixed effectiveness of test-and-treat strategies to reduce HIV incidence. Through network analyses and simulations, contextualized with community interviews, this career development award will assess whether sexual network clustering impacts the effectiveness of HIV treatment as prevention in Malawi, Uganda, and Nigeria, to help us better understand situations where the population- level impact of test-and-treat strategies are successful and where they are not. These results will inform population-level treatment targets and will allow tests of the utility of network-driven strategies to drive testing and treatment, ...

application_id: 11124714
activity_code: K08
title: Single cell analysis of mitotic regeneration in the mouse vestibular system
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Hearing loss and balance dysfunction affect millions of people world-wide with the most common underlying
reason being the irreversible loss of specialized sensory cells called hair cells that transmit information about
hearing and balance. In this proposal, we use a mouse model to understand the behavior of a limited number of
stem cells that are activated after injury to the inner ear, with the goal of deciphering their mechanism of
regeneration. Our overarching aim is to identify what molecular pathways can be manipulated to stimulate
regeneration following injury in humans to help restore hearing and balance.

phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE When people get infected with viral infections like SARS-CoV-2 and influenza, some get very sick while some barely notice their symptoms. activity_code: R44
title: Multi-Media Professional Development for Parenting Educators to Deliver Oral Hygiene Education for Parents of Young Children
org_name: OREGON RES BEHAVIORAL INTERVENTION STRAT
phr: Project Narrative
This application features the construction and RCT-based evaluation of a professional development program
and tool, BeReady2Smile_ProD, which will promote oral health behavioral parenting strategies among parents
by providing a scalable and sustainable approach for distal training of Parenting Educators using both Home-
based and Group-based delivery methodologies. Parenting Educators learn to lead parents through

targeted at parents of young children attending parenting education classes and families receiving group and
home visiting services through Head Start and other early childhood agencies.

application_id: 10884408
activity_code: F31
title: Determining the Involvement of PERK in TMAO Induced Atherosclerosis
org_name: HARVARD UNIVERSITY D/B/A HARVARD SCHOOL OF PUBLIC HEALTH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE:  Trimethylamine N-oxide (TMAO) is a common dietary derived metabolite that has emerged as a central driver of cardiovascular disease. But efforts to leverage this pathway therapeutically have been impeded by a lack of a clear mechanistic understanding of TMAO action. Therefore, we propose to define how TMAO causes atherosclerosis by defining TMAO’s direct effects on its only known receptor and elucidating the TMAO atherogenic stress response.

application_id: 11194952
activity_code: F32
title: Identifying Developmental and Degenerative Mechanisms of SMA Using Single Motor Neuron Nucleus RNA Sequencing
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Spinal muscular atrophy (SMA) is a genetic, neuromuscular disease that causes early onset, severe muscle
weakness. While SMA patient outcomes have significantly improved since the development of SMN-
increasing therapeutics, neuromuscular deficits are still present in most patients. The objective of this
project is to identify mechanisms of SMA motor neuron maldevelopment and degeneration in mouse
models with differing disease severity to guide future therapeutic development and improve patient
quality of life.

activity_code: K08
title: mHealth for suicide prevention: Design, development, and feasibility of a scalable SMS-based safety planning intervention
org_name: NORTHWESTERN UNIVERSITY AT CHICAGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Young adults in the U.S. (ages 18-24) have the highest and fastest growing rates of suicidal thoughts and behaviors, but the lowest rates of mental health care. This project aims to design and pilot an automated interactive safety planning service delivered via SMS that yields highly personalized safety plans and supports use over time. By accomplishing the proposed training goals and research project, this project will generate data to support an efficacy trial.

application_id: 11091454
activity_code: T32
title: Medical Scientist Training Program
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ILLINOIS AT CHICAGO
phr: Project Narrative
This program produces physician scientists who have both MD and PhD training, i.e., who receive both
degrees at the completion of the program. MD-PhD graduates have deeper perceptions of medical significance
in biology than do scientists without medical education, more comprehensive understanding of the limitations in
medical practice, and greater ability to transfer basic scientific advances toward patient care advances. Thus,
MD-PhD graduates are positioned to identify and pursue interdisciplinary approaches to important biomedical
problems, and to help bridge the basic and clinical sciences.

application_id: 10799556
activity_code: F30
title: Uncharted Territory: Mapping and Manipulating Cholinergic Basal Forebrain Activity in a Mouse Model of Alzheimer's Disease
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: Narrative While the degeneration of basal forebrain cholinergic neurons (BFCNs) is a well-known feature of Alzheimer’s Disease, the pattern of degeneration and the extent to which this phenomenon contributes to cognitive decline remain elusive. The research proposed here seeks to define how the neural circuitry associated with BFCNs is altered throughout Alzheimer’s Disease progression and aims to evaluate the effects of targeted, cell-type specific manipulations of BFCNs. Studies such as this are essential to further our understanding of the circuit pathology underlying Alzheimer’s Disease and identify potential therapeutic targets.

application_id: 11081689
activity_code: K01
title: Chronic Murine Cerebral Mycosis: Pathogenesis, Neuroimmune Response, and Relevance to Alzheimer's Disease
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: Alzheimer’s Disease is a significant threat to public health as it is the only one of the top 10 causes of death in
the United States that cannot be effectively prevented, treated, or cured. This proposal aims to determine if
infections due to fungi and bacteria can be implicated as causes of Alzheimer’s Disease and involves creating a
mouse model of gut yeast infection that then spreads to the brain to cause Alzheimer’s-like disease. Success
with creating this new model will allow us to further understand how Alzheimer’s Disease progresses and how
to diagnose and treat this devastating illness.

title: Characterization of Chronic Pain and its Biopsychosocial Mechanisms in Lupus using Electronic Health Records
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: Despite treatment advances in rheumatology, pain remains the most prominent, unaddressed patient
complaint in systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), a condition that disproportionately affects women and racial

application_id: 11136846
activity_code: F32
title: Elucidating the role of Akkermansia muciniphila in Group B Streptococcal vaginal colonization and ascending infection
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Group B Streptococcus (GBS), a frequent colonizer of the vaginal tract, is also a leading cause of neonatal
sepsis and meningitis and can be transmitted to a newborn during vaginal delivery or by ascension into the
uterus during pregnancy. We have found that GBS is associated with the Gram-negative mucin-degrading
obligate anaerobe Akkermansia muciniphila, a novel vaginal commensal, and in pregnant females the presence
of both GBS and A. muciniphila coincide with pre-term birth. This proposal aims to characterize the properties of
A. muciniphila that contribute to GBS persistence and how mucin-degradation alters the vaginal environment to
promote GBS ascension.

activity_code: K99
title: Novel GluN2BR Radioligand for PET Neuroimaging in Alcohol Use Disorder
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
This K99/R00 proposal will perform first-in-human characterization of the novel GluN2BR PET neuroimaging
radiotracer (S)-[18F]OF-NB1 (K99 phase), anticipating its application in people with AUD and healthy controls
(R00 phase). We will investigate the relationship between GluN2BR availability and alcohol-related behaviors,
particularly alcohol seeking and consumption, to uncover neurobiological drivers behind AUD. This
groundbreaking research promises to yield critical translational insights and foster the development of targeted
AUD therapeutic strategies.

org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ILLINOIS AT CHICAGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Adjusting medication strategies in response to therapeutic needs throughout lifetime is an essential part of
managing type 2 diabetes to prevent costly and clinically severe complications. However, the diverse treatment
patterns and timing of these adjustments cannot be solely explained by clinical indications but are often
influenced by socioeconomic backgrounds and health inequities. To address the gaps in achieving optimal
outcomes in healthcare delivery, we propose a comprehensive examination of the causal associations
between social health determinants, patient-level diabetes treatment modality, and their impact on glycemic
control and diabetic complications, aiming to generate evidence on the factors contributing to treatment
outcome variations and identify strategi...

application_id: 11123409
activity_code: K01
title: Rehabilitation of Reading Deficits in Subacute Stroke using fMRI Neurofeedback and Motor Imagery
org_name: KESSLER FOUNDATION, INC.
phr: The overall goal of this project is to advance a biologically-based approach to treatment of reading disorders
after stroke, which will expand the limits of cognitive rehabilitation. Using a novel brain imaging technique,
called real-time functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) neurofeedback combined with right hand motor
imagery, this project will re-instate brain activity in the left language-dominant hemisphere. Stroke patients will
practice modulating their own brain activity using fMRI neurofeedback signal and will select the most effective
mental strategies that help them maintain brain activation patterns associated with better reading recovery.

Child maltreatment and adolescent substance use are highly-prevalent and tightly-connected public health
concerns in the United States. The current study aims to identify the pathways from child maltreatment to
adolescent substance use by focusing on risky and protective attributes within youth activity spaces as critical
underlying mechanisms. Findings from the proposed research will inform the development of more effective
interventions to prevent substance use among high-risk youth by elucidating key contextual mechanisms and
protective factors for adolescent substance use following child maltreatment.

activity_code: K01
title: Digital Biomarkers of Movement, Emotional State, and Social Connectedness: clinically integrated tools to improve health disparities in older patients with chronic pain
org_name: BRIGHAM AND WOMEN'S HOSPITAL
phr: Functional status—a summary of a patient’s physical, emotional, and social wellbeing—is a key index that guides the treatment of older patients with chronic pain conditions. Clinical integration of smartphones promises to bridge the “digital divide” by offering new ways to measure functional status and expand access to real-time care in vulnerable populations who lack access to in-person clinical evaluations. Because 80% of Americans in their fifties and sixties own and use smartphones, we will define and implement a smartphone-based profile of functional status and pilot how such a digital biomarker profile might offer real-time clinical decision support in real-world patients.

application_id: 10825103
activity_code: F32
title: Dissecting neuroinflammatory mechanisms in a model of progressive TDP-43 pathology
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative 97% of ALS patients and 57% of Alzheimer’s Disease patients present with dysregulation of the protein TDP-43, causing disruption of a variety of critical genes, leading to neurodegenerative disease. Neuroinflammation, an understudied facet of ALS and Alzheimer’s Disease, occurs when overactive immune cells cause catastrophic damage to otherwise healthy neuronal tissue. Using neuron/immune cell culture and a mouse model of TDP-43 pathology that exhibits a progressive neurodegenerative phenotype, this project aims to understand the genetic and molecular regulators of neuroinflammation and characterize potential therapeutic targets for ALS and Alzheimer’s Disease.

application_id: 10832474
activity_code: F31
title: Non-canonical mechanisms of excitotoxicity
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF NEW MEXICO HEALTH SCIS CTR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Ischemia following acute brain injury has been identified as one of the leading causes of transitioning vulnerable penumbra to damaged tissue, but understanding specific mechanisms of injury has lagged far behind. Spreading depolarizations (SDs) have been identified as key contributors to injury progression through a many underexplored cellular mechanisms and thus pose as the best area for preclinical exploration. This proposal seeks to understand the relationship between excessive SD-mediated L-type Ca2+ channel activation, Zn2+ release and subsequent post-synaptic uptake, and initiation of downstream cellular injury.

application_id: 11111916
activity_code: K01
title: Characterizing Biological Aging for Heart and Lung Diseases Using Multi-omics Ageotypes
org_name: BRIGHAM AND WOMEN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
As the global population ages, chronic heart and lung diseases have become a major public health burden that
underscores the need for more effective approaches to treatment and prevention. However, the complexity of
the aging process and the variability among individuals make it difficult to identify the relationship between
different aging processes and the development of specific heart and lung diseases. This proposal aims to
develop a new framework for understanding aging mechanisms by generating "ageotypes" that group individuals
by similar multi-omic profiles and improve the ability to identify specific molecular mechanisms of aging and their
connection to chronic heart and lung diseases, thereby providing a scientific basis for personalized prevention
and treatment ...

title: Type I interferon Impacts Treatment Response in Rheumatoid Arthritis
org_name: HOSPITAL FOR SPECIAL SURGERY
phr: Study Title: Type I Interferon Impacts Treatment Response in Rheumatoid Arthritis
PROJECT NARRATIVE
Insufficient treatment in rheumatoid arthritis (RA) results in increased morbidity and mortality, and a heavy
economic burden associated with loss of function. This research will help to develop a more individualized
approach to therapy in RA and determine an immunologic basis of non-response to TNF inhibitors. The findings could
suggest a molecular rationale for the use of specific therapies in biologically defined subgroups of patients.

 The goal of this proposal is to determine laryngeal mechanisms underlying dysphonia in connected speech,

The University of Rhode Island proposes the development of a new program to cultivate STEM learning for high
school students. This program – HERBAL: Hands-on Education and Research for Biomedical and Analytical

game-based learning modules centered on molecules from medicinal plants. The academic year course will be

with their cultural and traditional importance to human health and well-being, and HERBAL will increase student

application_id: 11142439
activity_code: K08
title: Determining biological and behavioral determinants of hepatocellular carcinoma incidence and outcomes
org_name: UT SOUTHWESTERN MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) is a rapidly rising cause of cancer-related death in the United States, with increasing incidence among individuals with chronic liver disease. Significant variation in risk and survival exists, but the biologic and behavior factors driving these differences are not well understood. This project will analyze data from three large, multicenter cohorts of patients with cirrhosis and HCC to identify key contributors to liver cancer development and outcomes. The findings will inform strategies for prevention, early detection, and more effective care, with the goal of reducing the burden of liver cancer in the U.S. and improving survival.

title: Thalamocortical dynamics during nociception and endogenous analgesia
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: Project Narrative
Chronic pain is a staggering health crisis adversely impacting the lives of millions of people. Existing treatments
fall short in providing safe and effective relief from pain. For this reason, my proposal will investigate a specific
pain-responsive circuit from the thalamus to the cortex to enhance our understanding of cell types and neural
pathways engaged by endogenous analgesic mechanisms to promote pain relief, and inform circuit-based ther-
apies for chronic pain to circumvent the use of opioid drugs.

Glycans are important modulators of immunity and are key signals detected by antigen-presenting cells, such

tools to direct immune cell phenotypes. This work will enable the design of glycan-directed immunotherapies for
the treatment of cancer and infectious diseases.

application_id: 11247083
activity_code: F32
title: Genetic and Molecular Mechanisms of hypermobile Ehlers Danlos Syndrome
org_name: MEDICAL UNIVERSITY OF SOUTH CAROLINA
phr: Project Narrative
Hypermobile Ehlers Danlos Syndrome (hEDS) is a genetic disorder that causes symptoms affecting
multiple body systems including the joints, skin, gastrointestinal tract, and heart. Despite its prevalence
of 1 in 500 individuals, there are currently no reliable tests or treatments available for hEDS. The
completion of this proposal will provide new understanding of the disease, opening new avenues for
future studies and the potential for therapeutic approaches that will make a significant impact on the
lives of those affected by hEDS and their families.
