application_id: 10894655
activity_code: K08
title: Defining the role of tuft cells in allergic airway disease
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: Project Narrative During chronic allergic airway inflammation, rare chemosensory cells called tuft cells produce prostaglandin E2 (PGE2), which I hypothesize modifies mucus movement and airway inflammation, contributing to diseases such as asthma and nasal polyps. My proposal will use mouse modeling and cell culture to understand how tuft cell-derived PGE2 orchestrates immune and epithelial functions in the airway during allergic inflammation, and then investigate whether the same processes contribute to asthma in humans. These studies will advance fundamental knowledge of airway biology and may identify new therapeutic targets to treat millions of patients suffering with chronic allergic airway diseases.

activity_code: F32
title: The mechanism of CELF1 upregulation and its role in the pathogenesis of Myotonic Dystrophy Type 1
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: Project Narrative
Myotonic Dystrophy Type 1 (DM1) is the most common cause of adult-onset muscular dystrophy and is
characterized by progressive muscle weakness and wasting. How the DM1 mutation leads to muscle wasting at
the molecular level is still unknown. This proposal aims to unravel the complex combinatorial DM1 disease
mechanisms of MBNL loss-of-function and CELF1 gain-of-function and to determine the contribution of CELF1
upregulation in DM1 muscle pathogenesis.

application_id: 11247928
activity_code: F32
title: Transposon control as a checkpoint during regeneration
org_name: CORNELL UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Transposable elements are differentially regulated upon injury; however, a functional assessment of their
contribution or hinderance to tissue regeneration has yet to be established. Expanding our understanding of how
transposons respond to and are regulated within the regeneration environment will greatly expand our
understanding of how transposons affect the regeneration outcome. The work proposed herein will provide
foundational insights regarding the transposon response to injury and define transposon control as a critical
checkpoint for successful tissue regeneration.

application_id: 10806968
activity_code: F31
title: Optimizing Music-Based Interventions for Stroke Rehabilitation
org_name: NEW YORK UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative: This study will address a need for motivational strategies to achieve therapeutic levels of task repetition in upper extremity rehabilitation post-stroke. To do so, we will characterize the motor, affective, physiological and neural effects of music improvisation in stroke survivors. The findings from this study will contribute to a framework for optimizing music-based interventions for upper extremity rehabilitation post-stroke.

activity_code: F32
title: Genomic Mechanisms of BDNF Signaling in the Brain
org_name: HARVARD MEDICAL SCHOOL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Although neurotrophins, such as brain-derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF), play an essential role in neural circuit
development and function, little is known about how neurotrophin-regulated transcription influences experience-
dependent plasticity in the brain. This study seeks to characterize the genomic mechanisms by which BDNF
regulates the maturation of inhibition within the mouse visual cortex. By identifying specific transcription factors
and target genes that mediate the genomic response to BDNF in the brain, this proposal will provide novel
insights into the etiology of neurological disorders that display a loss of BDNF production, including Alzheimer’s
and Huntingtin’s disease.

title: Transformative Solutions for Reducing Frequent 911 Fall Calls in the Homes of Patients with Cognitive Impairments
org_name: OHIO STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Healthcare costs for falls have reached 55 billion dollars annually in the US with falls in older adults impacting nearly every family, in every sector, of every community. Leveraging 911 fall calls as activation events could transform our ability to identify high risk individuals and significantly impact fall prevention strategies. At the completion of this study, a scalable EMS fall prediction tool will be developed to elucidate the impact that frailty, cognitive impairment and the home environment have on emergency fall events in order to activate and transform effective fall prevention solutions in the homes of vulnerable older patients.

application_id: 10914054
activity_code: K08
title: Targeting Protein Trafficking in Arrhythmogenic Cardiomyopathy
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF UTAH
phr: NARRATIVE The proposed research is relevant to the NIH because it is focused on fundamental biology that will impact cardiovascular disease and potentially develop a new therapeutic paradigm to treat and prevent sudden cardiac death; many heart disease states result in abnormal trafficking of proteins in the heart muscle cells. Abnormal protein trafficking is related to sudden death, but there are no therapies that address this trafficking defect. We propose to use a protein called GJA1-20k that we have shown improved trafficking of certain proteins to recover this trafficking defect in a mouse heart disease model.

application_id: 11263603
activity_code: F32
title: Learning-induced synaptic plasticity in corticospinal output neurons of motor cortex
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Corticospinal neurons serve as the output of motor cortex and coordinate muscle activation during voluntary
movements. Using advanced imaging techniques in behaving animals, this proposal aims to address how
synaptic plasticity in these output neurons can modify spike output in order to drive learned behaviors, thus
revealing how the brain can be dynamically altered during motor learning. Findings generated from this proposal
will elucidate a specific function for synaptic plasticity at the corticospinal node in the motor pathway, which is
highly relevant in the context of spinal cord injury, amyotrophic lateral sclerosis, and other neurological diseases
that affect learning and/or movement.

phr: Project Narrative This project will study how virtual reality augmented bicycling using visual feedback and competition will increase exercise intensity, motivation, and enjoyment for persons with Parkinson's Disease. application_id: 11286614
activity_code: F32
title: Heme Homeostasis in the Malaria Parasite Plasmodium falciparum
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 Blood stage malaria parasites encounter enormous amounts of heme and are extremely vulnerable to
disruptions in heme homeostasis, yet it remains unknown how parasites regulate cytosolic heme concentrations.
Use a cutting-edge click-chemistry-based technique, we will identify previously unknown heme binding proteins
and then directly investigate the role of these candidate proteins as well as Heme Detoxification Protein in
plasmodial heme homeostasis. Our work will reveal novel antimalarial targets that can be exploited to overcome
current problems with antimalarial resistance.

phr: Project Narrative

application_id: 11289297
activity_code: F32
title: Neural substrates of extinction deficits in pathological fear
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) is a psychiatric disorder characterized by intrusive memories and
unrelenting fear that is commonly resistant to cognitive-behavioral interventions. Investigations using post-
mortem human brains suggests that molecular changes in neurons that express the cortistatin peptide may
under PTSD symptomology. This proposal seeks to test this hypothesis by investigating the functional role of
cortistatin-expressing neurons using preclinical animal models and integrating these findings with postmortem
human brain tissue to help us better understand if selectively targeting these neurons in the amygdala can reduce
pathological fear in PTSD and other neuropsychiatric disorders.

application_id: 11227827
activity_code: K08
title: Intestinal chondroitin sulfate glycosaminoglycans and their role in inflammatory bowel disease
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The worldwide prevalence of inflammatory bowel disease and its associated economic burden continue to rise.
Increasing evidence implicates impaired extracellular matrix function in the pathogenesis of inflammatory bowel
disease, but studies testing the role of extracellular matrix augmentation as therapy for inflammatory bowel
disease or as a biomarker are limited. The goal of this work is to investigate whether chondroitin sulfate
glycosaminoglycan elements in the intestinal extracellular matrix can be used as novel therapeutic agents in
inflammatory bowel disease and whether their composition at diagnosis can predict clinical disease outcomes.

activity_code: K08
title: Post-GWAS Functional Genomics Analysis to Define Pathogenic Mechanisms for Pulmonary ArterialHypertension
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO
phr: Project Narrative
In this proposal, I aim to use a new method to define a human DNA mutation and a protein named FUBP1 that
binds to this mutation as an explanation as to why some people are more likely to develop a deadly blood vessel
disease in lungs called pulmonary hypertension. If successful, my work could change our understanding of
pulmonary hypertension and drive development of new ways to detect and treat this deadly disease.

activity_code: F32
title: The Role of Host Genetics in Shaping Intestinal Lactobacillus Population
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CHICAGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 A host’s physiology is contingent on the composition of their microbiome. While a major player in
shaping the commensal population is the host’s genetics, due to insufficient models, the mechanisms by which
this occurs is unclear. Herein, we describe our murine model in which host-commensal relationships driven by
genetics, independent of environmental factors, can be discovered and the mechanism determined by a
multifaceted approach spanning immunology, microbiology, genetics, and computational analyses.

application_id: 10914874
activity_code: K08
title: Optimizing Smoking Cessation Treatment for Patients with Bladder Cancer
org_name: SLOAN-KETTERING INST CAN RESEARCH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Bladder cancer is caused by smoking and continued smoking after diagnosis leads to significant and preventable morbidity and mortality. Bladder cancer patients rely principally on their urologist for medical guidance but urology providers do not frequently use evidence-based practices (EBPs) that screen patients for smoking and provide them with the necessary counseling and medications to quit. In this study, we will characterize use of smoking cessation EBPs in the urology clinic then adapt and pilot a strategy to facilitate use of these EBPs in the urology clinic for patients with bladder cancer.

activity_code: K23
title: Multi-omic Studies of Local and Systemic Immune Dysregulation in Rosacea
org_name: BRIGHAM AND WOMEN'S HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative The goals of our research are to 1) elucidate the mechanistic link between skin microbiota and local immune dysregulation in rosacea skin and 2) elucidate potential systemic immune dysregulation in rosacea patients. Unlike prior studies, we aim to employ multi-omics data integration to 1) explain the complex relationship of the microbial environment and the host immune response in the skin, and 2) characterize the systemic immune dysregulation that can explain the systemic disease burden in rosacea patients. We anticipate that our findings will lead to the development of novel preventative and therapeutic targets and shift the way we study and manage rosacea toward early detection and prevention of systemic co-morbidities.

title: Leveraging Social Networks to Promote Sexual Assault Recovery and Reduce Drinking to Cope through a Web-Based Intervention
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE After a sexual assault, peers often express confusion about how to best support survivors and may encourage drinking to cope, which can exacerbate the wide range of physical and mental health problems survivors experience, contributing to persistent distress, long-term healthcare costs, and loss of productivity. The current project involves the design and feasibility testing of a novel web-based intervention to increase effective peer support of survivors by focusing on behaviors incompatible with heavy drinking, which will in turn reduce alcohol use and related consequences within high-risk social networks of sororities. title: Molecular insights into regulator-dependent transcription initiation pathways

My research will reveal the mechanisms by which important transcriptional regulators mediate their effects on

application_id: 10807067
activity_code: K01
title: Autonomic and Vascular Mechanisms of Cardiovascular Risk in Women with Post-traumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD)
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MINNESOTA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The prevalence of post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) is growing at an alarming rate in the U.S, and these patients are at significantly greater risk for developing hypertension and cardiovascular disease. Women are twice as likely as men to develop PTSD, a chronic stress disorder that may negate the cardiovascular protection conferred by female hormones before menopause. This study of the neural and vascular consequences of PTSD in premenopausal women will help inform the development of therapies to improve cardiovascular health in this growing and high-risk population of women.

activity_code: K12
title: Reproductive Scientist Development Program
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative The Reproductive Scientist Development Program plan for career development serves as a model for postgraduate medical education and assures that some of the most promising young physician-investigators in the country will remain in academic investigative obstetrics and gynecology. A great majority of the highly diverse Scholars who have finished Phase II of the Program have dedicated themselves to research in the reproductive sciences and have developed major independent funding support, with many becoming division heads, department chairmen and/or deans at their top tier academic institutions. To date the productivity of RSDP scholars past and present has been outstanding, has contributed greatly to the reproductive sciences, and is a credit for the Program.

application_id: 11317551
activity_code: F32
title: Investigating the impact of a diabetic urinary environment on group B Streptococcus virulence and host response.
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
People with diabetes are more likely to develop urinary tract infections (UTIs) caused by Group B
Streptococcus (GBS), which can lead to severe complications. This project aims to understand how the high
sugar levels in diabetic urine affect GBS, making it more virulent and increasing its resistance to immune
defenses. By identifying key bacterial genes and studying how sugar levels impact infection and immune
response, this research will help develop new strategies to prevent and treat GBS UTIs in diabetic patients.

activity_code: F31
title: Uncovering the Synergistic Effects of Chronic Alcohol Use and Pathological Tau Expression
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: Heavy alcohol use can lead to cognitive, emotional, and physical impairments, and eventually culminate in neurodegeneration. Considering that alcohol consumption, especially heavy consumption during middle age, is the strongest modifiable risk factor for Alzheimer’s Disease and related dementias; it is imperative to understand how alcohol use affects health outcomes. This proposal will extensively characterize the behavioral and biological changes that occur with chronic alcohol use across the lifespan in a transgenic mouse model of neurodegeneration.

application_id: 11323083
activity_code: F32
title: Discovery of the mechanisms of resistance for the anthelmintic emodepside
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: Parasitic nematodes are the most abundant, diverse, and destructive macroparasites on the planet, placing
heavy health burdens on billions of people in developing countries and causing billions of dollars lost annually
in crops and livestock. This proposal seeks to identify genes, transcripts, and small molecules involved in
emodepside resistance, a promising new anthelmintic with completely uncharacterized resistance
mechanisms. Completion of this proposal will identify emodepside resistance genes and establish biochemical
mechanisms behind the metabolism of emodepside, which are both needed to implement targeted and
effective anthelmintic treatment plans.

application_id: 10750937
activity_code: F30
title: The roles of intestinal epithelial AhR in alcohol-induced barrier dysfunction and liver injury
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ILLINOIS AT CHICAGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  Alcohol related hospitalizations have been on the rise throughout the COVID-19 pandemic and intestinal barrier function has been shown to play a key role in the development of alcohol induced liver injury. Our proposed studies will reveal a very new cell-type specific mechanism by which intestinal epithelial and immune cells cross-talk in terms of signaling of a very important protein called Aryl Hydrocarbon Receptor (AhR) to restore the damage caused by alcohol on the gut and the liver.

application_id: 11375789
activity_code: F32
title: Probing chemical dynamics and catalytic mechanisms in metalloenzymes with advanced X-ray spectroscopy techniques
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIF-LAWRENC BERKELEY LAB
phr: Project Narrative
Metalloenzymes are involved in catalyzing many reactions that are of critical importance to health, using
transition metals such as Fe or Mn in their active sites. The introduction of X-ray free-electron lasers has made
it possible to follow these enzymatic reactions at room temperature in real time as the reaction proceeds.
Development of X-ray absorption spectroscopy methods that make use of this novel transformative tool is
important for understanding the dynamics of the metal sites and the mechanisms of these reactions.

application_id: 11387456
activity_code: F32
title: RNA regulatory circuits in allergic airway inflammation
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Asthma is an obstructive airway disease frequently driven by unrestrained allergic, “type 2” (T2), inflammation.
The regulatory mechanisms that control the magnitude and duration of inflammatory responses in asthma are
not well characterized. This proposal seeks to determine how post-transcriptional circuitry involving mRNA and
RNA binding proteins tunes the intensity T2 inflammatory signaling in asthma, with the goal of revealing novel
pathways that may have potential for therapeutic targeting.

activity_code: K08
title: Defining a therapeutic platform for DIPG with mRNA CAR T cells and microglia inhibition
org_name: CHILDREN'S HOSP OF PHILADELPHIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Diffuse intrinsic pontine glioma is a devastating pediatric brain tumor that despite decades of clinical trials remains fatal with a median survival of less than a year. This proposal focuses on the development of safe and effective immune-based therapies for these tumors, using chimeric antigen receptor T cell therapy. Through these studies we hope to improve our understanding of the tumor growth within the immune microenvironment and translate effective immunotherapies to patients.

application_id: 10750956
activity_code: F31
title: How Molecular Chaperones Promote Pathogen Survival During Starvation
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE All living cells must synthesize and maintain proteins to carry out biological functions, but microorganisms frequently face environments that lack the nutrients to do so – both in external environments and during infection of hosts. How do microbes manage to continue producing and maintaining proteins when nutritional resources are scarce? Our research answers this question by examining the critical role of protein-helping molecules, known as chaperones, in enabling pathogenic bacteria to adjust key cellular processes and survive even while starving for essential molecules.

org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Succinate dehydrogenase is a mitochondrial complex with critical roles in metabolism and. Inherited loss-of- function variants in the succinate dehydrogenase genes are causative in several human cancers, and tumors have been linked to DNA damage. This proposal aims to accurately characterize the phenotypes and tumor biology associated with succinate dehydrogenase germline pathogenic variants, to develop improved risk estimates and evaluate potential targeted therapies for patients who progress to disease.

Lack of microbial exposure during early life leads to a spectrum of chronic inflammatory disease in adulthood.

application_id: 11135372
activity_code: T32
title: Neurobehavior, Neuropathology, and Risk Factors in Alzheimer's Disease
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: Alzheimer's disease (AD) and related dementias affect a growing number of people as the population ages.
Currently, nearly 6 million persons in the US have AD; it is estimated that by the year 2050, that number will be
14 million. With this increase in the number of AD patients, there is a corresponding growth in the number of
family members and other caregivers affected by this devastating disease, as well as dramatic social and
economic costs. The goal of our research training program is to prepare a new generation of investigators to
translate mechanistic scientific discoveries into effective tools for diagnosis, treatment, and prevention of AD
and related dementias.

application_id: 10893573
activity_code: K08
title: Implementation of Eplet Mismatch Analysis in Pediatric Kidney Transplantation
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: Donor specific HLA antibodies (DSA) develop against short amino acid sequences (eplets) within the HLA antigen and lead to early graft loss in kidney transplant recipients. Identifying which mismatched eplets are most high-risk for DSA formation can help to avoid these in upcoming transplants and improve long-term allograft survival. This research will inform the development of a novel and adaptive allocation model aimed at reducing immunological burden, while optimizing racial and geographic equity, and ensuring optimal organ utilization.

activity_code: K23
title: CLots and Oxygen in Va-ExtracorpoReal membrane oxygenation (CLOVER) study
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE … Adult patients with heart and lung failure are sometimes supported with temporary mechanical support called extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (ECMO) and its use has increased dramatically in the United States over the last 10 years. Although ECMO is a life-saving intervention for patients, a potential complication is acute brain injury. We will examine whether ECMO causes the production of blood clots that lodge in the brain and cause stroke and whether too much oxygen may contribute to injury in this proposal and understanding these risks may enable us to suggest changes to treatment that can be tested in future studies.

application_id: 10911824
activity_code: K23
title: Identification of Precision Sepsis Subphenotypes Using Vital Sign Trajectories
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Sepsis is a severe life-threatening response to infection that results in over 270,000 deaths in the U.S. annually. The one-size-fits-all approach to treating sepsis has not worked, and thus it is essential to identify distinct sepsis subgroups that may have differential responses to treatments. This proposal applies cutting-edge machine learning algorithms to bedside vital signs (e.g., temperature, heart rate, etc.) to identify sepsis subgroups with differential responses to treatments, thus opening the doors for the precision medicine approach to sepsis.

title: The role of Tex15 in shaping stochastic olfactory receptor gene choice.
org_name: RUTGERS, THE STATE UNIV OF N.J.
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The gene regulation processes by which an olfactory sensory neuronal progenitor goes from co-expressing multiple olfactory receptor genes to monogenic, monoallelic choice of olfactory receptor genes in mature olfactory sensory neurons needs to be further elucidated. Tex15 is transiently expressed during the critical gene regulation window in olfactory sensory neuronal progenitors, and we hypothesize that Tex15 is regulating monoallelic olfactory receptor gene choice through either methylation patterns or through its effect on chromatin state of developing olfactory sensory neurons. Through this study, I will be trained on cutting-edge molecular, cellular and computational neuroscience approaches to answer some of the broader questions of the field of olfactory sensory neu...

application_id: 11142514
activity_code: F32
title: Predicting post-kidney transplant dementia/Alzheimer's Disease risk in older patients
org_name: NEW YORK UNIVERSITY SCHOOL OF MEDICINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Older kidney transplant (KT) recipients are at increased risk of developing dementia/Alzheimer’s disease (AD),
which significantly increases the risk of mortality or graft loss. Despite these risks, predicting who will develop
post-KT dementia/AD is not part of the pre-KT evaluation. The goal of the proposed project is to identify
specific risk factors that are associated with developing post-KT dementia/AD, and to create a model that
identifies which patients undergoing KT evaluation are at increased risk of developing post-KT dementia/AD,
so that they can receive targeted pre- and post- transplant interventions and additional support.

application_id: 11139380
activity_code: F31
title: Development and characterization of self-amplifying RNA Lipid Nanoparticles for potent and multiplex gene expression in solid tumors
org_name: BOSTON UNIVERSITY (CHARLES RIVER CAMPUS)
phr: Project Narrative
This proposal describes the training and mentoring of Mr. Nambiar and his research project to further expand
and explore the applications of self-amplifying RNA (saRNA) for the treatment of solid tumors. He aims to
accomplish this both by optimizing nucleoside composition and lipid nanoparticle (LNP) vehicle properties, and
determining the maximum payload for saRNA (both in quantity of genes and total length of transgene). The
capability of these optimizations of the saRNA-LNP platform for allowing efficient and multiplex gene
expression in solid tumors will be assessed both in vitro and in vivo.

title: Elucidating the sterol-sensing mechanisms that regulate lipid metabolism.
org_name: UT SOUTHWESTERN MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 HMG CoA reductase (HMGCR) is the molecular target of cholesterol lowering statin drugs, and
a key regulatory enzyme of the cholesterol biosynthetic pathway which is subjected to feedback
regulation by sterol and nonsterol isoprenoid end products that act to induce its endoplasmic reticulum
(ER)–associated degradation (ERAD). I have shown that the 1,1-bisphosphonate ester SRP3042
mimics sterols in inducing HMGCR ERAD without disrupting transcriptional feedback mechanisms, and
a photoactivatable form specifically crosslinks HMGCR. This proposal aims to determine the sterol-
sensing mechanisms through which HMGCR ERAD is initiated to determine whether SRP3042 induced
HMGCR ERAD is a viable strategy for overcoming statin-resistance, will identify the molecular target
of LY29...

application_id: 10612447
activity_code: K43
title: Defining Campylobacter and immune response related determinants in the pathogenesis of Guillain-Barré syndrome  patients in low-income countries
org_name: INTERNATIONAL CTR/DIARRHOEAL DIS RES
phr: Project Narrative:
This research project will identify potential biomarkers, nutritional factors and microbial strain
differences (using high-throughput sequencing) associated with the response of intravenous
immunoglobulin (IVIg) treatment, as well as the clinical course and outcome of Guillain-Barré
syndrome (GBS) in low-income countries. The proposed study will improve the understanding
of IVIg treatment outcome and provide insight to guide the development of personalized
medicine for patients with GBS. The project will involve extensive training activities and
guidance from the mentors and training advisory group.

application_id: 10806241
activity_code: K01
title: Surfacing values in the economic evaluation of genomic sequencing for diagnosis of children with rare diseases
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  Though any single rare disease is by definition uncommon, together these diseases affect nearly 30 million individuals in the United States, two-thirds of whom are children, and contribute significantly to morbidity, mortality, and healthcare costs. Exome and whole genome sequencing have the potential to provide a diagnosis to an estimated 25 to 50 percent of those patients struggling with undiagnosed rare diseases, but the downstream costs and benefits of this testing beyond diagnosis remain largely unknown. This study will contribute essential and timely data, new methods, and ethical analyses to inform both how we can, and how we should, define and measure the value of genome sequencing for pediatric patients with rare diseases.

application_id: 10752636
activity_code: K23
title: Obesity as a Driver of Inflammation and Brain Volume Loss in Pediatric Multiple Sclerosis
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF VIRGINIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Multiple sclerosis (MS) is an immune-mediated, neurodegenerative disorder that can affect children and results in progressive brain volume loss over time. Obesity is highly associated with progressive brain atrophy in people without MS. While early-life obesity is known to be a risk factor for developing MS, obesity’s impact on MS disease severity and course is unknown; thus, this proposal seeks to define the relationship between obesity, inflammatory disease activity, and neurodegeneration in children with MS.

activity_code: F31
title: Investigating mechanisms of neuronal nuclear mechanotransduction in physiological conditions and tauopathy
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF TEXAS HLTH SCIENCE CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Alzheimer’s disease and related tauopathies currently have no cure or robust treatment plan. Understanding the
molecular mechanisms driving neurodegeneration are vital for increasing our ability to accurately diagnose and
treat patients suffering from neurodegenerative disorders. The proposed study will investigate basic mechanisms
regulating mechanotransduction in neurons and will test the hypothesis that pathogenic tau drives neuronal
death by disrupting neuronal nuclear mechanotransduction.

application_id: 11112621
activity_code: K99
title: Investigating CNR1+ Corticostriatal Activity in Co-Exposure Models of FASD
org_name: TEXAS A&M UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCE CTR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Gestational alcohol exposure is a well-established causal factor in Fetal Alcohol Spectrum Disorders (FASD).
However, in human populations, similar maternal exposures may result in variable FASD outcomes, suggesting
that other risk factors may contribute to the severity of FASD. This project will investigate the contributions of
two poorly understood but common risk factors: paternal alcohol consumption in the preconception period and
maternal polysubstance use, which will enhance the translational relevance of animal models to human FASD
and inform public health recommendations surrounding gestational substance exposure.

activity_code: DP2
title: Leveraging HCV Phylogenetic Networks to Prevent HIV and Other Blood Borne Infections Among People Who Inject Drugs
org_name: ALBERT EINSTEIN COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
UNAIDS and WHO have issued targets to end the HIV epidemic and eliminate viral hepatitis by 2030. People
who inject drugs (PWID) are essential to both strategies, yet harm reduction services such as needle and
syringe programs and medication-assisted treatment for opioid use disorder have been challenging to
implement in low- and middle- income countries due to significant cost, complex logistics, and social factors.
Therefore, efficient strategies to prevent HIV and other blood borne infections among PWID are needed as
these services are scaled up. This goal of this project is to characterize HCV phylogenetic networks to identify
the highest risk PWID and model the impact of targeted approaches to the prevention of HIV and other blood
borne infections in Kenya, East Afri...

application_id: 10911843
activity_code: K08
title: Investigating the contribution of cellular senescence to the efficacy of radiation therapy.
org_name: SLOAN-KETTERING INST CAN RESEARCH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Ionizing radiation is known to induce cellular senescence and activate a secretory program that can result in immune modulation. We have shown that senescence is a significant contributor to the anti-tumor response to ionizing radiation. We propose to use mouse and human models of rectal cancer to define the contribution of senescence and its secretory program to the antitumor effects of radiation, to characterize how radiation-induced senescence remodels the tumor immune environment, and to assess the contribution of radiation-induced senescence and its secretory program to render systemic anti-tumor responses that can be augmented with immune checkpoint blockade.

application_id: 11032045
activity_code: K23
title: Developing a modified brief alcohol-focused intervention tailored for patients with alcohol use disorder in opioid agonist treatment
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF NOTRE DAME
phr: 8. Project Narrative Alcohol use increases the risk for treatment dropout, relapse, and overdose in patients receiving opioid agonist treatment for opioid use. This project will develop and evaluate a modified alcohol-focused intervention tailored to address alcohol use in patients in opioid agonist treatment who are struggling with alcohol use disorder. This brief and scalable tailored intervention will have the potential to decrease alcohol use and improve opioid agonist treatment outcomes with minimal resources, adding in an innovative way to efforts to address the ongoing opioid overdose crisis in the United States.

title: Role of Anterior Cingulate Cortex Ensemble Regulation of Spinal Cord Excitability in Associative Opioid Analgesic Tolerance.
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Opioids are important pain medications, but chronic opioid use in a particular environment can produce tolerance
that is associated with that environment. However, when opioids are consumed in a different place, tolerance
can be reversed rapidly, which can lead to fatal overdose. This proposal will explore the contributions of brain
and spinal circuits to this environmental control of analgesic tolerance to help inform the development of new
treatments aimed at decreasing pain and opioid overdose.

title: Primary cilia as regulators of vascular stability during embryonic development
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The proposed research is relevant to public health because understanding the mechanisms behind vascular
stabilization during embryogenesis is vital to preventing vascular birth defects, which are too often fatal. The goal
of this research is to investigate the role of endothelial primary cilia as mechanosensitive signaling structures in
vascular development and stabilization, which will have an impact on how new approaches are developed to
manage and prevent vascular birth defects such as arteriovenous malformations, aneurysms, and congenital
defects of the heart and great vessels.

activity_code: F30
title: The Molecular Role of C1QL3 and its Binding Partners in Synaptic Regulation
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CONNECTICUT SCH OF MED/DNT
phr: Project Narrative Certain neuropsychiatric disorders may be associated with synaptic dysfunction resulting from impairment of synaptic cell adhesion molecules (SAM). SAMs promote synapse physiology and prevent pruning by tethering the presynaptic and post synaptic membranes, but the molecular web of SAMs that contribute to proper synapse physiology are not fully explored. This project will investigate the role of a novel hypothesized SAM, complement C1q like 3 (C1QL3), in regulating excitatory synapses by forming a trans-synaptic adhesion complex in primary neuron cultures; investigation of C1QL3’s, and its binding partners’, roles in organizing synaptic nano- architecture will elucidate key mechanisms that regulate synaptic plasticity.

title: Nociceptor regulation of intestinal inflammation and tissue protection
org_name: WEILL MEDICAL COLL OF CORNELL UNIV
phr: Project Narrative
Inflammatory bowel diseases (IBD) are a group of chronic inflammatory illnesses of the gastrointestinal tract that

phr: The University of Arizona Training Program to Advance Translational Research on Alzheimer's Disease and AD Related Dementias is designed to create a workforce capable of developing therapeutics to address the national and global crisis of Alzheimer’s disease.

application_id: 11125746
activity_code: F32
title: Corticolimbic circuit and neuroimmune mechanisms of comorbid HIV and cocaine use
org_name: DREXEL UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) and cocaine use disorders (CUDs) are persistent public health concerns
within the United States and worldwide. Cocaine use can facilitate the pathophysiology of HIV and preclinical
studies suggest that HIV may promote addiction-related behaviors, highlighting the unique biobehavioral
interaction underlying the comorbidity of HIV and CUDs. This proposal will employ a cutting-edge combination
of preclinical rodent modeling of HIV and CUDs along with quantitative cellular imaging and circuit-specific,
closed-loop optogenetics to reveal corticolimbic circuit and neuroimmune contributions to HIV-induced
dysregulation of reward-seeking behavior, which will further our understanding of the neurobehavioral
underpinnings of comorbid HIV and CUDs.

application_id: 10911863
activity_code: F30
title: Integrated Electrochemical Aptamer Based Platforms for the Point-of-Care and Continuous Monitoring of Clinically Relevant Analytes
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CINCINNATI
phr: Project Narrative Obstetric and pediatric patients often suffer from imprecise medical treatment due to vast interpatient variability and lack of individualized data amongst these populations. Electrochemical aptamer based sensors offer the potential for a generalizable approach to the on-demand, high- frequency monitoring of clinically relevant analytes, which could enable personalized treatment tailored to each individual patient. This proposal will usher in the first integrated EAB platforms deployable both in the clinic and in the home of the patient, serving as a catalyst for the clinical adoption of EAB sensors for a variety of applications in the treatment of disease in children, pregnant women, and beyond.

application_id: 11237358
activity_code: F31
title: Querying the Interactome of Alpha-Synuclein During Pathological Processes
org_name: MAYO CLINIC  JACKSONVILLE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Lewy body dementias (LBD) represent one of the most common forms of progressive dementia in adults, with
symptoms that often begin decades before a clinical diagnosis is made. The goal of this project is to identify
proteins, and associated cellular mechanisms, which may contribute to early stages of alpha-Synuclein
pathology in Lewy body dementias. Findings from this study have potential to facilitate biomarker development
for early LBD diagnosis and/or promote new candidates for successful disease-preventing therapies.

application_id: 10731536
activity_code: RF1
title: Development and Validation of a Genetically Encoded Method to Trace and Manipulate Neuronal Circuits in Zebrafish - DIVERSITY SUPPLEMENT
org_name: CALIFORNIA INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY
phr: Project Narrative
Many neuropsychiatric disorders are thought to be caused by abnormal connections between neurons in the
brain. However, the methods currently available to visualize how neurons are connected to each other have
limitations that make it difficult to identify such connections in a comprehensive manner. We propose to develop
a new method to visualize how neurons are connected to each other in zebrafish, a vertebrate animal model
ideally suited to investigate how genes control the assembly of neuronal connections in the brain, and how this
process is affected in disease.

application_id: 11173657
activity_code: K99
title: Understanding the functional role of fibroid subtype mutations for drug discovery
org_name: HENRY FORD HEALTH + MICHIGAN STATE UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 Uterine fibroids affect up to 80% of women in reproductive age and the economic impact in the United
State is estimated to be nearly $34 billion annually. Fibroids account for 50% of the hysterectomies performed
in the United States, and in order to develop alternatives to hysterectomy, a major gap in our understanding of
how different fibroid mutations lead to disease needs to be investigated. This proposal addresses three goals of
NICHD interest, improving reproductive health, health disparities and disease prevention, through studies
that will determine the functional role of two most common mutations found in uterine fibroids and discover novel,
personalized therapies by using the transcriptomic profile of each fibroid subtype.

application_id: 11177951
activity_code: K99
title: Generative AI for synthetic data: A framework to expand health data reach for research and ensure algorithmic fairness
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Growing evidence suggests we can create privacy-respecting health datasets that are larger, more widely available through the integration of synthetic data produced by generative AI, thereby supporting advances in public health. However, it is currently unknown how best to appraise synthetic health data generation for the factors on which researchers need to trust (e.g., reliability of the data generated) and refine such technology to support robust healthcare research and applications. This project will create a set of computational methods, realized in open-source software, to enable systematic evaluation of and multi-institutional development of synthetic health data, as well as the development of a fairness-aware medical AI pipeline through the integration of such data.

title: IMSD at the University of Massachusetts Chan Medical School
org_name: UNIV OF MASSACHUSETTS MED SCH WORCESTER
phr: Research teams including scientists from different backgrounds have a broader set of perspectives and talents which in turn increase the breadth and depth of research questions and the methodological approaches to these studies. application_id: 11189776
activity_code: K99
title: Defining Mechanisms Governing Myc Stability and its Modulation by Aurora Kinase A
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MINNESOTA
phr: Public Health Relevance.
In this proposal I will characterize mechanisms of regulation and induced stabilization of the oncogenic Myc
family transcription factors, specifically the stabilizing influence of Aurora Kinase A (AurA). These AurA-Myc in-
teractions and other regulatory protein interactions will be structurally characterized and probed in cancerous
cellular environments to determine their physiological relevance. These studies will serve as a basis for under-
standing the mechanisms governing Myc dysregulation, responsible for 30% of all cancers.

application_id: 11243944
activity_code: F32
title: Forebrain Representations and Control of Sniffing
org_name: HARVARD MEDICAL SCHOOL
phr: Project Narrative
Almost all terrestrial vertebrates sniff, actively passing air and odors through the nasal cavity, as a
primary means to navigate and explore the world. This work aims to understand how the
hippocampus, a forebrain region crucial to navigation and memory, is involved in sniffing as means to
sample information and guide behavior. Results from this study will contribute to our understanding of
a complex brain-wide circuit that controls a ubiquitous adaptive behavior.

application_id: 11135293
activity_code: F30
title: Investigating the role of BCAA metabolism in regulating adiposity during systemic hypoxia.
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: Project Narrative
Prolonged hypoxia in mice and humans dramatically transforms systemic metabolism, decreasing body weight,
fat mass, and blood glucose levels. Oxygen deprivation itself is not a realistic therapeutic option, but hypoxia-
induced metabolic improvements coincide with significant reprogramming of branched-chain amino acid
metabolism in adipose tissue, presenting a potential target for treating obesity. This project will investigate the
mechanism underlying this rewiring of BCAA metabolism in adipose tissue and the role of this rewiring in
controlling adiposity and glucose clearance during hypoxia.

title: Deciphering Preserved Autonomic Function after Spinal Cord Injury
org_name: MAYO CLINIC ROCHESTER
phr: Project Narrative: Dysfunction in the autonomic nervous system is poorly understood after spinal cord injury, leading clinicians to watch and wait for potentially devastating secondary complications to occur. The goal of this project is to characterize dysfunction in the cardiovascular component of the autonomic nervous system using a combination of lab, ambulatory, and imaging-based techniques. These results could give new insights into who will develop secondary complications and, by developing precision markers, lay the groundwork for accurately assessing future interventions to treat and prevent them.

application_id: 11245184
activity_code: F32
title: Role of Intestinal Epithelial Cells in Controlling Local and Systemic Yersinia Infection through IL-1 Signaling
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Systemic bacterial infections are a major cause of morbidity and mortality, and understanding how bacteria
spread beyond the initial site of infection is crucial for developing effective host-directed treatments. This proposal
will investigate how interleukin-1 (IL-1) signaling in intestinal epithelial cells regulates immune responses to limit
bacterial replication in the gut and prevent the systemic spread of pathogens. By examining the mechanisms by
which IL-1 signaling controls infection dynamics at mucosal sites, the findings from our studies will have broad
public health importance and could lead to novel therapeutic strategies targeting mucosal immunity to prevent
disseminated bacterial infections.

title: Determining the cellular and nanoscale localization of Synaptotagmin VII
org_name: THOMAS JEFFERSON UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Fast and efficient neurotransmission is essential for normal brain function, and a thorough understanding of how synapses are organized is essential to our understanding of neurological diseases. application_id: 11244729
activity_code: F32
title: Exploring leaderless mRNA translation, a potential Achilles heel of mycobacteria
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: Project Narrative
Tuberculosis (TB) remains a significant global health threat, causing approximately 1.5 million deaths annually,
and the increasing number of drug-resistant Mycobacterium tuberculosis (Mtb) strains makes reducing this
burden even more difficult. This project seeks to increase our understanding of a prevalent but poorly
understood form of translation in mycobacteria, the translation of leaderless mRNAs. Knowledge of the
mechanisms that allow lmRNA translation to occur and a deeper understanding of its contribution to the
mycobacterial life cycle will contribute to the development of novel therapeutics exploiting this form of
translation, increasing treatment options for drug resistant Mtb and enhancing public health.

title: Virginia Tech Initiative to Maximize Student Development
org_name: VIRGINIA POLYTECHNIC INST AND ST UNIV
phr: Narrative The proposed IMSD program will develop and train successful PhD students with an emphasis on underrepresented minorities, and impart the skills needed to succeed in PhD programs in Behavioral and Biomedical Sciences and Engineering at a leading R1 university. These activities continue to positively impact the number of minorities who go on to receive PhDs and subsequently pursue careers in the Biomedical or Behavioral Sciences and Engineering research, which is an important goal of the NIGMS.

application_id: 11212160
activity_code: K99
title: Behavioral Obesogenics: Environmental EDC effects on behavioral processes promoting obesity.
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF TEXAS AT AUSTIN
phr: Project Narrative
Humans are ubiquitously exposed to complex chemical mixtures, including endocrine-disrupting chemicals
(EDCs), which are linked to both neurobehavioral and metabolic disorders. The co-morbidity of behavioral and
metabolic disruptions is underexplored but crucial for understanding how EDCs contribute to complex,
overlapping health risks. In this proposal, a rat model of human-relevant EDC mixture exposure is used to
identify the molecular and epigenetic mechanisms driving this co-morbidity, providing insights to inform medical
interventions and public health policy.

title: Multimorbidity Phenotypes and Effects on Endothelium in Critically Ill Patients with Sepsis and Sepsis-induced ARDS
org_name: RHODE ISLAND HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative Acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS), often caused by sepsis (si-ARDS), is a severe and frequently fatal lung condition exacerbated by chronic comorbidities like metabolic syndrome. This project investigates how these comorbidities influence extracellular vesicle (EV) interactions with pulmonary endothelial cells, aiming to identify distinct patient subgroups and their clinical outcomes. Utilizing biospecimens from an ICU biorepository, we seek to develop personalized treatment strategies by understanding the role of EVs in si- ARDS, thereby improving patient care and pandemic preparedness.

title: Computational Modelling of Perceptual Information Accumulation in Blind Individuals
org_name: SMITH-KETTLEWELL EYE RESEARCH INSTITUTE

title: Intelligent Clinical Decision Support for Perioperative Blood Management
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: Project narrative 20 million patients have surgery in the United States every year, with approximately 1 million of those patients requiring life-saving blood transfusion. Presurgical preparation for transfusion is important to allow for safe and timely transfusion during surgery; however, excessive preparation is unfortunately common, costly, and contributes to blood waste. This study aims to develop an intelligent clinical decision support system to help clinicians prepare blood for patients who are likely to need it, while avoiding excessive preparation for patients who don’t, thus improving patient safety while reducing blood waste and healthcare costs.

activity_code: F31
title: Major Histocompatibility Complex Shapes Early Life Microbial Events to prevent Autoimmunity
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: Project Narrative: The incidence of type 1 diabetes (T1D), an autoimmune disease which affect millions of people worldwide, has been rising for the past half century. While both genetic and environmental factors contribute to the risk of developing T1D, recent studies indicate that early-life microbial interactions with the developing immune system offer a critical window of opportunity to influence the immune system development and prevent disease. Our study uses a new mouse model of the early-life microbiome, derived from a line of mice that are genetically protected from T1D, to identify immunomodulatory commensal bacteria and immune system pathways that can be used to develop effective preventative therapies for T1D.

activity_code: F31
title: Decoding the role of chromatin architecture in alveolar epithelial cell identity and disease
org_name: BOSTON UNIVERSITY MEDICAL CAMPUS
phr: Project Narrative 3D chromatin architecture is known to play an important role in gene expression and cell identity and disruption of normal chromatin architecture has been implicated in a variety of disease. The goal of this project is to define the chromatin landscape that controls pulmonary alveolar epithelial cell fates and to investigate how haploinsufficiency of the lung lineage transcription factor, NKX2-1, may perturb chromatin topology and lead to a pathologic alveolar-basal intermediate (ABI) cell state associated with lung disease. application_id: 11014651
activity_code: DP2
title: Gel-based Optical-isolation Single-Cell 3D Spatial Multiomics
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA-IRVINE
phr: NARRATIVE
The project is relevant to NIH’s mission because the 3D spatial organization of cells is intimately linked to human
health and disease. The proposed GO3D spatial multiomics will provide an ideal technology to map all major
biomolecules of whole tissues in 3D.
Applications of GO3D will transform our understanding of many critical

activity_code: K99
title: The role of valence encoding amygdala ensembles in avoidance behavior
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The basolateral amygdala (BLA) contains discrete ensembles of anatomically intermixed neurons that respond
to positive and negative valence stimuli, though how these ensembles interact and coordinate to facilitate
approach and avoidance is not known. Here I propose to use two-photon imaging and multiphoton activation
and suppression to elucidate the microcircuit governing valence encoding amygdalar ensemble formation,
establish the necessity of negative valence ensemble activity for avoidance behavior, and identify the
neuromodulatory input that regulates ensemble formation endogenously.

application_id: 11248856
activity_code: K99
title: Regulation of intestinal lipid metabolism by type-3 immune cytokine
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA LOS ANGELES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Malnutrition and metabolic imbalance are prevalent comorbidities of bowel inflammation, frequently associated
with poor clinical outcomes. However, managing these complications is challenging, and current therapies are
ineffective, due to critical gaps in understanding the connection between the inflammatory environment and the
absorptive enterocyte function. This proposal aims to bridge these gaps by elucidating how mucosal immune
effector impacts enterocyte nutrient uptake and systemic metabolism, potentially uncovering new ways to treat
nutrient imbalance associated with inflammation and digestive disorders.

activity_code: K99
title: Quality control and spatial regulation of bacterial condensates
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Dynamic condensates are generally beneficial, whereas most insoluble aggregates are toxic. Understanding
the distinct regulations of aggregates and condensates is essential for designing fundamentally different
therapeutics to control and target diseases associated with these structures. The proposed work aims to
elucidate mechanisms of bacterial condensate quality control and develop minimal positioning systems to
study and manipulate bacterial condensates, providing potential tools for the study of infectious diseases and
the development of therapeutics to improve human health.

application_id: 11229479
activity_code: K23
title: Defining the Mechanism of Complement Pathway Activation during Pregnancy and its Role in Adverse Pregnancy Outcomes in Sickle Cell Disease
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Women with sickle cell disease, a common inherited blood disorder that can affect nearly every organ system
in the body, have high rates of pregnancy complications like preeclampsia (high blood pressure and protein in
the urine), premature birth, and even death of the mother or baby. We will study the role of complement, a
group of proteins that are part of the immune system and closely linked to inflammation and clotting pathways,
in causing these pregnancy complications. We will use what we learn from this study to better identify how
pregnancy complications happen in women with sickle cell disease, so we can predict who may get these
complications and then study targeted treatments to improve outcomes for mothers and their babies.

application_id: 11249560
activity_code: K99
title: Probing the Rules of Protein Structure-Function Relationships through the De Novo Design of Functional Proteins
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: Project Narrative
The design of proteins with functions not yet seen in nature is a fundamental goal in protein science, with many
translational applications. This proposal aims to extend our emerging capabilities in functional protein design,
through the design of de novo protein drug delivery vehicles, tailored to the structural and physicochemical
characteristics of a masked bioactive peptide, with multiple layers of controlled and triggered release capabilities.
This function-directed approach will allow us to obtain atomic-level understanding of protein structure-function
relationships and protein-peptide interactions, and establish innovative design principles which could ultimately
contribute to the development of protein-based therapeutics.

application_id: 11249620
activity_code: K99
title: Structured quantification of inherited macular disease phenotypes as the basis for automated algorithms to determine causal genes
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The ability to obtain a molecular diagnosis in patients is essential for understanding the cellular etiology, clinical
prognosis, and treatment prospects of inherited macular dystrophy —a heterogeneous group of genetic disorders
characterized by progressive central vision loss. Genetic testing has recently become more accessible to
patients with IMD, however, testing outcomes are often inconclusive due to the difficult task of identifying a single
causal gene across the immense and complex landscape of the human genome. Using advanced diagnostic
retinal imaging modalities, this research proposal will decipher precise phenotypic differences between IMD
genes and create a prediction tool to help improve genetic testing outcomes for patients.

application_id: 11249663
activity_code: K99
title: High-Throughput Studies of MYH7 Variant Cardiomyocytes to Identify Pathogenic Variants and Factors Governing Variable Expressivity of Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM) is a devastating disease caused largely by pathogenic variants in MYH7,
a clinically actionable gene, yet 75% of reported variants are of unknown significance (VUS). In this proposal, I
will establish a new paradigm to accelerate MYH7 VUS reclassification, bringing hope to patients (and clinicians)
waiting for an answer. In addition, I will also determine the role of putative HCM-modifying factors in diverse
genetic backgrounds to provide clinicians with novel, independent metrics of risk that will enable better HCM risk
assessment and counselling for patients.

application_id: 11250033
activity_code: K99
title: Unraveling Immune Cell Interactions and Multicellular Dynamics in GNAQ-Driven Capillary Malformation
org_name: BOSTON CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative – Capillary malformations (CM) are congenital vascular irregularities that manifest as skin
lesions, while Sturge-Weber Syndrome (SWS) is a neurocutaneous disorder in which CM extends to cerebral
and ocular regions. In 90% of CM and SWS cases, a somatic activating GNAQ mutation (p.R183Q) is
predominantly present in endothelial cells in the skin and brain. The proposed study aims to comprehend the
impact of p.R183Q in endothelial cells and its interactions with macrophages and mural cells, utilizing live cell
imaging and multicellular spheroid models.

title: Structural and Functional Characterization of CELSR-subfamily adhesion G protein-coupled receptors
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CHICAGO
phr: Project Narrative
Cadherin EGF Laminin G seven-pass G-type receptors (CELSRs) are conserved aGPCRs with large,
multidomain extracellular regions. CELSRs are essential for animal development, and they are associated with
a range of developmental and neuronal pathologies; however, understanding of CELSR pathophysiology as well
as future therapeutic development is hindered by the lack of molecular-level detail about these key proteins.
Using a combination of cutting-edge techniques including cryogenic electron microscopy and cellular assays,
this proposal will develop a mechanistic understanding of how the CELSR ECR regulates receptor function; this
proposal also aims to discover novel interacting partners of the CELSR intracellular region, which will open new
pathways for studying aGPCR biol...

application_id: 11258009
activity_code: K99
title: Neuronal mechanisms of selective visual attention
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA LOS ANGELES
phr: Project Narrative
This research proposal is aimed at unveiling core neurophysiological mechanisms of visual attention at
behavioral, cellular, and molecular levels of organization in a tractable model organism. Circuit motifs, neuronal
properties, and molecules involved will highlight basic mechanisms of an every-day cognitive process that
remains enigmatic. A variety of brain dysfunctions affect the ability to select and attend a visual object, and will
benefit from a comprehensive understanding of a simple form of selective visual attention.

application_id: 11258024
activity_code: K99
title: Role of Protein N-Myristoylation in Driving DNA Damage Response
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
This study focuses on protein N-myristoylation, a critical protein modification linked to various diseases, including
cancer, epilepsy, Alzheimer's disease, and infections. This study seeks to identify novel molecular targets for
therapeutic intervention by delving into the unexplored frontier of N-myristoylation within the DNA damage
response (DDR) pathway. The findings could not only advance our understanding of genomic stability but also
provide critical insights into the development of precision therapies, potentially revolutionizing the treatment
landscape for diseases associated with DDR dysfunction.

application_id: 11264822
activity_code: K99
title: Genomic Conflict Resolution: Establishment of a new model for unconstrained germline selection
org_name: ROCKEFELLER UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Germline selection and somatic conflict describe how beneficial de novo mutations in reproductive cells can
“selfishly” result in spontaneous congenital disorders for offspring, often occurring in complex and ill-defined
ways that confound proper diagnosis. This proposal will explore these genomic conflicts using the germline
restricted chromosome of songbirds, which uniquely isolates germline selective pressures in gene duplications
through their programmed DNA elimination from somatic tissues. The establishment of a novel zebra finch model
to study this chromosome will provide a bird’s eye view of germline sequence flexibility that underlies heritable
mutations and spontaneous congenital disorders.

phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Atrial myopathy is a novel biomarker for cardiovascular disease (CVD) risk that can be detected by measuring left atrial function before left atrial enlargement occurs, offering a potential opportunity to identify and treat high risk individuals before clinically symptomatic CVD occurs. We propose to randomize 80 individuals without diabetes and at risk for heart failure to either empagliflozin or placebo and conduct assessments of left atrial and left ventricular function and change in plasma proteins after 9 months of intervention. activity_code: K00
title: Evaluating the role of protein self-assembly in the innate immune system
org_name: NORTHWESTERN UNIVERSITY
phr: Aging in humans leads to a chronic inflammatory state, known as inflammaging, which increases the risk for age-related diseases such as type 2 diabetes, atherosclerosis, and Alzheimer's disease. This proposal applies novel approaches to identify a potential molecular mechanism of inflammaging and the associated impairment in blood cell differentiation, while illuminating the physiological roles of protein self-assemblies in innate immune signaling. The insights that emerge from this research will advance our understanding of aging from a fundamental molecular perspective and potentially illuminate new opportunities to combat age- and disease-associated autoinflammation.

application_id: 11264859
activity_code: K99
title: Environmental mixtures, immune function, and vaccine antibody response in children
org_name: ICAHN SCHOOL OF MEDICINE AT MOUNT SINAI
phr: Project Narrative
The global pandemic highlighted the importance of understanding risk factors that predict vaccine antibody
response. Effective strategies to prevent vaccine failures require understanding the underlying biological
pathways that impede antibody response. In this proposal I will (1) construct a fine-scale air pollution model over
Mexico City, (2) assess the impact of ambient pollutants on vaccine antibody concentrations, and (3) investigate
specific cell types involved in vaccine responses and whether they mediate the relationship between ambient
pollutants and vaccine antibody concentrations.

application_id: 11266211
activity_code: K99
title: IL-27 functions as a novel signal 3 cytokine and promotes cytotoxic T cell responses to tumors
org_name: HARVARD MEDICAL SCHOOL
phr: Project Narrative
Despite the success of immune checkpoint blockade (ICB) in diverse cancer types, the majority of patients do
not respond to ICB and the determinants of successful ICB responses are not yet clear. This project will test the
hypothesis that the cytokine IL-27 functions as the primary signal 3 cytokine in tumor immunity and will elucidate
(1) how IL-27 mediates CD8+ T cell cytotoxicity in cancer and (2) how IL-27 production is regulated. These
findings will inform new strategies for improving ICB responses by modulating the IL-27 pathway.

title: PTND PATH FORWARD study: PTND Pathologies and Trajectories of Functional Outcomes With comparison to other ADRDs
org_name: ICAHN SCHOOL OF MEDICINE AT MOUNT SINAI
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
There is no biological or clinical disease definition for post-traumatic neurodegeneration (PTND) following
traumatic brain injury (TBI), presenting a significant barrier to accurate diagnosis, prognosis, and the
development of targeted treatments. This research will be among the first to leverage existing deeply
phenotyped cohorts to examine multi-domain clinical decline in cognitive, neurobehavioral, and motor
outcomes and use a proteomic approach in blood and brain tissue to identify and validate PTND pathologies.
The knowledge gained from this project will produce clinically meaningful outcomes of highest importance to
patients and families by 1) characterizing clinical signatures of PTND to facilitate clinical diagnosis and
prognosis, and 2) advancing pathological di...

application_id: 10754585
activity_code: K23
title: Improving medication adherence and disease control for patients with multimorbidity: the role of price transparency tools
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: Many middle-aged and older adults who live with multimorbidity report abandoning their medications or skipping doses because of cost. Patients are unable to account for medication costs when making medical decisions, because they rarely know what those costs will be before getting to the pharmacy. Under the guidance of her mentors, Dr. Sloan will evaluate the uptake and acceptability of a novel clinician-facing out-of-pocket price transparency tool, as well as its impact on clinical outcomes and adherence among middle-aged and older adults with multimorbidity.

application_id: 11281870
activity_code: K99
title: Loneliness and inflammatory markers as biopsychosocial pathways to AD/ADRD: Analysis of a rapidly aging segment of the US population
org_name: RUTGERS BIOMEDICAL AND HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: NARRATIVE
The rapidly growing population of older immigrants may be at greater risk of loneliness, a well-established risk
factor linked to Alzheimer’s disease and Alzheimer’s disease related dementias (AD/ADRD) due to migration-
related social network characteristics. However, the pathway from loneliness to cognition has not been studied
in this critical subpopulation, a research gap that prevents the development of targeted interventions to reduce
the AD/ADRD public health burden. The proposed project, including training and research, focuses on the largest
group of older immigrants in the US and will enable Dr. Sheftel to be a successful independent scholar advancing
research on AD/ADRD risk in a population that will make up 20 million older adults in the US by 2050.

application_id: 11012352
activity_code: K99
title: Cell surface protein dynamics in neural circuit assembly
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
During development, neural circuits self-assemble to form the connectivity patterns governing all brain functions.
This proposal seeks to elucidate conserved cellular strategies that underly neural circuit formation in the intact
brain. Thus, this work will provide key mechanistic insight into an important neurodevelopmental process, which
will be crucial for creating therapeutic strategies to regenerate circuits following ageing, disease, or injury.

application_id: 11283327
activity_code: K99
title: Exploiting RNA biogenesis to accelerate neuronal maturation and model age-related tauopathies
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
This proposal seeks to uncover mechanisms of neuronal maturation to refine in vitro models of
neurodegenerative disease. The adult RNA biogenesis program is induced in immature human neurons to
accelerate acquisition of mature identity and, the same principle is employed to develop a novel cell-based
system to study adult tau biology and pathology. These technologies, when applied to patient-derived stem cells
with and without predisposing mutations, will yield neurons of pertinent age for the study of late-onset disease.

application_id: 11283388
activity_code: K99
title: Enantioselective Hydrochlorination of Alkenes enabled by Cooperative Catalysis and Enantioconvergent Dynamic Kinetic Resolution of Quaternary Stereocenters
org_name: HARVARD UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
The proposed research will deliver innovative synthetic technologies to access important chiral alkyl chlorides
(K99) and quaternary-center-containing molecules (R00) in enantiopure form. By developing novel principles in
cooperative asymmetric catalysis, readily available chemical building blocks will be transformed into valuable
functional motifs. This will ultimately enable the biological interrogation of new chemical matter with the potential
to significantly improve human health through the development of new small molecule-based therapeutics.

application_id: 11283445
activity_code: K99
title: Integrating AI and Co-production to Analyze Communications in Social Media Substance Use Recovery Groups
org_name: RUTGERS BIOMEDICAL AND HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: Substance use disorder (SUD) is a leading public health challenge tied to long-term adverse consequences for
physical and mental health. Peer support is well-established as beneficial for SUD recovery, yet little is known
about how engagement in online peer support groups is associated with recovery outcomes. This study will
generate insights that enhance understanding of digital recovery spaces while informing future mechanistic
studies and interventions that provide online peer support for individuals with SUD, ultimately reducing rates of
SUD in the US.

activity_code: K99
title: A Synaptic Basis for Dopamine-Driven Reinforcement Learning in Cortex
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: Project Narrative
 Reinforcement learning enables animals to adapt their behavior based on experience, a
process critically regulated by dopamine signaling throughout the brain. However, the precise
synaptic mechanisms through which dopamine shapes neural circuits to encode learned actions
remain poorly understood, limiting our ability to develop targeted interventions for disorders
associated with dysfunctional dopamine signaling and/or impaired reinforcement learning, such
as Parkinson’s disease, addiction, and schizophrenia. In this proposal, I will investigate how
dopamine directly sculpts synaptic connectivity and activity within cortical networks, using cutting-
edge in vivo imaging and an innovative brain-machine interface paradigm to establish a direct link
between dopamine reinfor...

application_id: 11032318
activity_code: K01
title: The mechanistic study on irisin-mediated immune response and its role in adipose tissue dynamics
org_name: DANA-FARBER CANCER INST
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Exercise benefits the body in multiple ways - and irisin, one of the exercise-induced hormones from muscle, recapitulates several aspects of these benefits, including the induction of heat generation from white fat tissue, strengthening bones, and improving brain functions such as memory and brain-directed movements. In obese and diabetic mice, irisin restores the metabolic functions of fat tissue, and reduces inflammation of fat tissue through upregulation of a cytokine IL33. The proposed project is focused on understanding the molecular interplay between irisin and IL33 in fat tissue and how their interaction affects different pathways in the fat cells responsible for heat generation.

application_id: 11283615
activity_code: K99
title: Mapping cerebellar influence in FTD: unraveling the role of the cerebellum in social cognition and clinical trajectory
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
This project will advance our understanding of how cerebellar degeneration influences social cognitive decline
across disease stages in behavioral variant frontotemporal dementia (bvFTD). By leveraging advanced
neuroimaging techniques and longitudinal analyses, this study aims to identify early cerebellar biomarkers that
predict later social, cognitive, and neuropsychiatric declines, and to elucidate the compensatory and
degenerative mechanisms underlying cerebellar-cerebral network dysfunction over disease progression.
Ultimately, these findings will improve early diagnosis, refine prognostic models, and lay the foundation for
targeted interventions that enhance patient outcomes and reduce caregiver burden.

application_id: 11283686
activity_code: K99
title: Investigating the Role of Tanycyte Barriers at the Circumventricular Organs in Health and Neuroinflammation.
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO
phr: Circumventricular organs (CVOs) are unique central nervous system interfaces with leaky vasculature, unlike
the tightly regulated blood-brain barrier. In the median eminence (ME), tanycytes form a barrier separating the
leaky vasculature, cerebrospinal fluid, and neural tissue, maintaining homeostasis. This proposal explores how
the ME barrier is established, differs from other CVO barriers, responds to neuroinflammation, and impacts
hypothalamic functions such as hunger, stress, and reproduction.

application_id: 11283809
activity_code: K99
title: Microglial control of thalamocortical circuits in Alzheimer's disease
org_name: J. DAVID GLADSTONE INSTITUTES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Alzheimer’s disease affects millions of Americans, yet current treatments provide limited relief, emphasizing the
urgent need for novel therapeutic strategies. The proposed research will investigate how TREM2R47H, one of the
top genetic risk factors for Alzheimer’s disease, disrupts microglial function, thalamocortical circuits, and synapse
remodeling, and examines whether manipulating microglia can alleviate Alzheimer’s disease symptoms in rodent
models. This proposal will yield the mechanisms by which TREM2R47H increases risk for disease and inspire the
development of novel therapeutic interventions to improve outcomes for individuals with Alzheimer’s disease.

application_id: 11283966
activity_code: K99
title: Targeting the evolving immunopeptidome of metastatic colorectal cancer
org_name: SLOAN-KETTERING INST CAN RESEARCH
phr: Metastatic colorectal cancer (mCRC) cells exhibit an essential and unique fetal WNT-signaling
signature for dissemination and renewal, consistently observed across multiple patients despite varying
genomic mutations. Immunopeptidomic analysis of patient-derived organoids (PDOs), which provide a
an effectively inexhaustive reservoir of clinically relevant cancer cells, reveals the presentation of
peptides associated with fetal WNT signaling on the cell surface, offering insights into novel cancer
targets. Through detailed and comprehensive studies of PDOs, we aim to uncover the evolving
immunopeptidomes in advanced disease and the role of NDK1 in the establishment of metastases,
paving the way for identifying T cell receptors for novel cellular therapeutic strategies.

application_id: 11289325
activity_code: K99
title: Role of gestational hypoxia in maternal brain health
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF NORTH TEXAS HLTH SCI CTR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Hypoxia exposure during pregnancy is associated with maternal cognitive impairments and adverse
psychosocial behavior. The proposed research will investigate the role of maternal proinflammatory responses
in gestational hypoxia-induced cognitive and psychosocial dysfunction during pregnancy and postpartum. The
findings of this study will be essential in the development of pharmacological strategies to mitigate the
devastating effects of hypoxia exposure on maternal brain health.

activity_code: K23
title: Cognitive mechanisms of delusion severity throughout recovery from an acute psychotic episode: a computational approach
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Delusions are impairing and distressing transdiagnostic psychotic symptoms that require better treatments. Longitudinal assessment of delusion severity and proposed cognitive alternations will advance mechanistic models, informing treatment development. This project will be the first to determine how proposed cognitive abnormalities in belief updating change over time during recovery from a psychotic episode, clarifying their role in delusion severity.

activity_code: T32
title: Training in Cardiovascular Imaging Research
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF VIRGINIA
phr: Program Director/Principal Investigator (Last, First, Middle): Kramer, Christopher M.
Nearly one in 3 deaths in the U.S. is due to cardiovascular disease, most due to coronary heart disease. The
need for improved noninvasive methods to detect early disease and to help manage chronic disease has never
been as critical. Training academic clinician investigators in the physics, engineering, and translational
research approaches to cardiovascular imaging is essential to future improvements.
