application_id: 11290325
activity_code: K99
title: Elucidating Thiol Reductive Stress in Lung Cancer Growth and Metastasis
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
NRF2 is hyperactivated by oncogenic mutation in ~30% of non-small cell lung cancers (NSCLCs), a destructive
cancer with high rates of metastasis. NRF2 coordinates cellular antioxidant responses and promotes an
environment that supports cell growth and metastasis, but its overactivation leads to reductive stress in a subset
of NSCLCs. The goal of this project is to use cutting-edge functional genomics and chemical proteomics,
innovatively uncover how NRF2 activity blocks tumor growth and metastasis through thiol reductive stress
leading to a blockage in mitochondrial respiration.

application_id: 11290791
activity_code: K99
title: Noninvasive assessment of renal fibrosis in kidney transplant recipients bymulti-contrast magnetic resonance imaging
org_name: HUGO W. MOSER RES INST KENNEDY KRIEGER
phr: Project Narrative
Chronic allograft dysfunction associated with progressive interstitial fibrosis and tubular atrophy is the major
cause of kidney transplant failure despite improvements in immunosuppression. The proposed research will
develop a novel multi-contrast magnetic resonance imaging method that can provide sensitive and specific
measures of renal allograft fibrosis. Such imaging biomarkers will enable non-invasive staging and longitudinal
treatment monitoring of renal fibrosis and therefore contribute to improved management of kidney transplant
patients.

application_id: 11291817
activity_code: K99
title: Metabolically Reprogramming Hepatic Macrophages to Conquer Immune Desert Tumors
org_name: SLOAN-KETTERING INST CAN RESEARCH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Antigen-presenting cells (APCs) play a crucial role in initiating and activating cytotoxic immune cells to target
and eliminate cancer cells. However, “immune-desert” tumors, which lack sufficient APC infiltration, often
exhibit reduced cytotoxic immune cell activity. This research project aims to address this challenge by
metabolically reprogramming hepatic macrophages, turning them into highly effective APCs to suppress tumor
progression via boosting cancer immunosurveillance. These investigations hold the potential to introduce
innovative strategies for turning “cold” tumors into “hot” tumors, offering new hope in the fight against cancer.

title: UKNeu-PREP: University of Kentucky Neuroscience Postbaccalaureate Research Education Program
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF KENTUCKY
phr: Project Narrative The goal of the University of Kentucky Neuroscience Postbaccalaureate Research Education Program (UKNeu-PREP) is to serve individuals from underrepresented and disadvantaged backgrounds from under- graduate institutions without substantive research opportunities. The realization of this mission is the develop- ment of a diverse pool of well-trained postbaccalaureate participants that transition into strong, neuroscience and research-focused higher degree doctoral programs. A diverse scientific community and workforce are vital for increasing the quality of higher education, medicine (including reducing health care disparities), scientific research, and technological innovation to solve health-related problems and challenges.

application_id: 11299158
activity_code: K99
title: Impact of Pathological Tau on Circuit Function in Phenotypic Mouse Models of Progressive Supranuclear Palsy
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: Project Narrative
Progressive Supranuclear Palsy (PSP) is associated with mutations in the Tau-encoding MAPT gene. Currently,
there are no cures or effective treatments for PSP. Understanding the circuit abnormalities underlying the
locomotor and occulomotor deficits present in PSP will illuminate potential disease-causing mechanisms and
may lead to new therapeutics.

application_id: 11300676
activity_code: K99
title: Investigation of Key Nicotinamide Adenine Dinucleotide Metabolism Pathway Roles in Support of Epstein-Barr Virus Replication
org_name: BRIGHAM AND WOMEN'S HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative (3 sentences)
As an orally transmitted pathogen, Epstein-Barr virus (EBV) crosses the tonsillar epithelium and
infects B cells, whereupon reaching the memory B-cell compartment it establishes lifelong
infection. In this proposal, we will investigate how EBV subverts cellular cell nicotinamide
adenine dinucleotide metabolism pathways to support efficient viral lytic replication, including in
nasopharyngeal epithelial cells. This research promises to lay a foundation for novel host
metabolism targeted therapeutic approaches to block EBV lytic gene expression.

application_id: 11301875
activity_code: K99
title: Regulating Skeletal Stem Cell and Neutrophil Dynamics for Improved Cleft Palate Bone Regeneration by Newly Designed Surrogate Bone Morphogenetic Protein-2
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: Project narrative
Bone Morphogenic Protein-2 (BMP-2) offers a less invasive alternative to the conventional autografting
treatment for cleft palate repair, yet it has not received FDA approval due to insufficient bone regenerative
efficiency and significant inflammatory reaction of immune cells. As BMP-2 acts on skeletal stem cells (SSCs)
to regenerate bone, research into the mechanism of bone repair from a SSC perspective will provide essential
information to improve palate bone regeneration efficiency. My research also aims to elucidate the influence of
neutrophils on inflammation and further develop strategies for safe and efficient BMP-2 based palate bone
repair, aligning well with the mission of the NIDCR.

title: Chromatin-based encoding of sex differentiation of neurons
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: Project Narrative Many animals exist in two different sexes which perform different behaviors. application_id: 10914233
activity_code: K01
title: Optimizing PET spatial extent measures to detect the earliest amyloid-beta and tau accumulation and associated cognitive decline in preclinical Alzheimer's disease
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative The proposed K01 research will provide a framework for future Alzheimer’s disease (AD) prevention trials to intervene at an earlier point in the disease process than is currently possible with standard amyloid-beta (A)- PET approaches, increasing the feasibility of AD prevention rather than treatment to slow a neurodegenerative process that is already underway. A novel A-PET spatial extent approach will be developed that enables detection of the earliest focal A deposits below standard PET detection thresholds, allowing future trials to target individuals in the earliest stage of amyloidosis. This novel spatial extent approach will be used to examine the earliest changes in A spread, tau proliferation, and subtle cognitive decline, and establish optimized primary and ...

application_id: 11212055
activity_code: K23
title: Machine learning approaches to personalized cigarette smoking risk prediction in adults trying to quit
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WISCONSIN-MADISON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Personalized treatment is a promising strategy that may enhance the effectiveness of smoking therapies and
deliver the right support for a particular person at a particular time in the quitting process. Machine learning
approaches afford the opportunity to develop sophisticated models that predict long-term smoking cessation
abstinence outcomes and daily smoking lapse risk. The machine learning approaches developed in this project
will inform hypotheses about when, how, and for whom to deliver personalized treatment planning and real-
time interventions to people trying to quit smoking in future digital and non-digital intervention approaches.

application_id: 10906164
activity_code: K01
title: An Intervention to Reduce Cancer Associated Thrombosis Through Improved Prophylaxis
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE: Recent studies show that if preventative dose anticoagulation is provided to outpatients with cancer at high risk for clotting, it reduces the risk of cancer associated thrombosis, which is the leading non-cancer cause of death among this population. While guidelines suggest offering this potentially lifesaving therapy to high-risk patients with an acceptable bleeding risk, it is not routinely done. This study will identify strategies to improve the appropriate use of preventative anticoagulation for patients with cancer in diverse settings and pilot test an intervention to improve the quality of care patients with cancer receive.

title: Defining pro-metastatic and endothelial-regulatory roles for LIN28B in hepatocellular carcinoma
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CHICAGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE LIN28B is a pleiotropic RNA-binding oncofetal protein that correlates with poor prognosis in hepatocellular  carcinoma (HCC). This proposal seeks to understand how LIN28B contributes directly to the metastatic  phenotype of HCC cells and how it contributes to the angiogenic and endothelial modulatory phenotype of HCC  cells using a combination of cell, animal, and human specimen studies. application_id: 11018504
activity_code: F31
title: NK Cell Cytotoxicity Against Cryptococcus neoformans in Persons with Advanced HIV and Cryptococcal Meningitis
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MINNESOTA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Despite advancements in antiretroviral therapy, cryptococcal meningitis continues to be the most common cause of meningitis in persons living with HIV and carries a high mortality rate in low-income countries. The proposed research focuses on quantifying human natural killer cell cytotoxicity against Cryptococcus neoformans and the identification of differentially expressed genes involved in exhaustion, cytokine production, and cytotoxicity. This research could lead to the identification of pathways that can be targeted by immunotherapies to enhance natural killer cell cytotoxicity with the overall goal to increase fungal clearance, reduce morbidity, and reduce mortality from cryptococcal meningitis.

application_id: 11139614
activity_code: K99
title: Quantitative characterization of tumor heterogeneity using habitat imaging for the prediction of patient outcome in triple negative breast cancer
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Despite recognized intertumoral diversity, current treatment strategies for triple negative breast
cancer remain generalized due to lack of reliable biomarkers. This proposal seeks to quantitatively
characterize TNBC heterogeneity using novel habitat signatures and elucidate interpatient
differences to aid in stratifying patients with increased probability of response to neoadjuvant
therapy. Successful whole-tumor habitat characterizations will yield greater understanding of an
individual’s tumor physiology and provide an avenue to further personalize breast cancer care.

application_id: 10888283
activity_code: K23
title: A Machine Learning Approach to Predicting Iatrogenic Withdrawal in Critically-ill Children
org_name: CHILDREN'S RESEARCH INSTITUTE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Iatrogenic withdrawal affects up to 57% of children who receive sedative and analgesic medications in the
pediatric intensive care unit (ICU), contributing to delayed recovery, patient and parental distress and
prolonged hospitalization. Due to limitations in sample size and variable sets, studies on iatrogenic withdrawal
in pediatric ICUs have primarily focused on the association of single risk factors, screening tools, and treatment
regimens, without attention to early identification of at-risk children. This proposal will leverage a national,
electronic health record derived database of over 200,000 pediatric ICU patients to investigate the full
spectrum of risk factors associated with iatrogenic withdrawal and using both traditional biostatistics and
machine learning ...

application_id: 10888299
activity_code: K23
title: The role of xanthine oxidoreductase activity and altered metabolism in scleroderma-associated pulmonary arterial hypertension
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
We hypothesize that the enzyme xanthine oxidoreductase (XOR) and oxidative stress from XOR-derived reactive
oxygen species influence development and progression of pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH) in systemic
sclerosis (SSc). This project examines XOR enzymatic activity and oxidative stress as potential contributors to
PAH in SSc, and investigates associations between XOR, altered downstream metabolic pathways, and disease
phenotypes and outcomes. Identifying XOR and oxidative stress as drivers of PAH pathogenesis would support
XOR as a novel potential therapeutic target, thereby enabling more precise treatment of SSc-PAH.

activity_code: F30
title: Functional evaluation of kinesin gene variants associated with female subfertility and egg aneuploidy.
org_name: RUTGERS, THE STATE UNIV OF N.J.
phr: Project Narrative Infertility is a disease that affects 1 in 10 women in the United States and a leading cause of female infertility is having an abnormal number of chromosomes (aneuploidy) in eggs and embryos. This proposal investigates genetic variants found in women who are predisposed to having more chromosomally abnormal eggs than would be typical for their age group. The outcome of this work, knowledge of the effect of these genetic variants on egg development, will lay the foundation for development of genetic tests that could help women and their physicians to non-invasively assess predisposition to having chromosomally abnormal eggs and thus infertility.

title: The role of extrachromosomal DNA in HER2-amplified breast cancer
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE HER2-amplified breast cancer is a major source of morbidity and mortality among American women. We find that approximately one third of HER2 amplifications in breast cancer occur on extrachromosomal DNAs (ecDNAs), but the significance of HER2 ecDNA in breast cancer pathogenesis and treatment response has yet to be explored. This project will discover the role HER2 ecDNAs in breast cancer pathogenesis and response to targeted therapy and will identify therapeutic vulnerabilities in HER2 ecDNA-containing breast cancers to pave the way for improved prognosis and treatment for patients with breast cancer.

title: Zikhethele - choose for you: evaluating choice-based antiretroviral therapy support for patients returning to HIV care after treatment interruption in South Africa
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CAPE TOWN
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The ultimate goal of this research is to evaluate the feasibility, acceptability and preliminary impact of incorporating choice in the delivery of ART support to patients who return to HIV care after an interruption, a growing population who are vulnerable to future interruptions. title: Mechanisms of clonal competition in age related clonal hematopoiesis
org_name: BOSTON CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL
phr: Age-related defects in the blood underlie many significant health conditions in older adults including higher risk
of infections, cardiovascular disease, and myeloproliferative disorders like leukemia. Our lab has recently
discovered a set of factors which appear to govern the development of these disorders. This study may
uncover mechanisms by which the cells that produce blood deteriorate and may lead to the development of
screening and treatment strategies for immune and cardiovascular defects, and blood cancers in older adults.

application_id: 11230663
activity_code: K99
title: The Impact of Pulmonary Influenza Infection on the Pathogenesis of Myocardial Infarction and Heart Failure
org_name: ICAHN SCHOOL OF MEDICINE AT MOUNT SINAI
phr: Narrative
The aim of this project is to expand our understanding of how pulmonary influenza A virus infection influences
the health of the cardiovascular system. Using innovative models of human disease, we will explore how
inflammation induced by influenza infection alters cardiovascular homeostasis; enhances myocardial infarction
risk; and affects the initiation and progression of heart failure over life. Results from this study will not only
uncover novel pulmonary-cardiac inflammatory cross-talk during infection, but also provide innovative means to
target immune axes following infection to protect the heart and mitigate cardiovascular risk.

application_id: 10933188
activity_code: K23
title: Reward responsivity and depression in autism spectrum disorder: A multimethod approach
org_name: CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL OF LOS ANGELES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Adolescents with Autism Spectrum Disorder experience depression at rates nearly twice that of their neurotypical peers, yet risk factors for depression in autism are not well understood and interventions are limited. Longitudinal, multimethod assessment of social and nonsocial reward responsivity and associations with depression symptoms in autism will advance mechanistic models, informing intervention development. This project will be the first to examine how relationships between reward responsivity and depression symptoms change over time in adolescents with autism, clarifying risk factors and potential intervention targets for this vulnerable population.

application_id: 10912498
activity_code: F32
title: Mechanisms of REST-mediated immunosuppression in cancer
org_name: BRIGHAM AND WOMEN'S HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative Current immunotherapeutic approaches largely fail to improve survival for patients with breast cancer, the second-leading cause of cancer related deaths of women in the United States. Completion of this research proposal will advance our understanding of how loss of tumor-intrinsic REST function, found in 15-20% of breast cancers, promotes an immune suppressive tumor microenvironment, which may, in part, promote resistance to immunotherapy. Information gained from these studies will provide targetable mechanisms to increase sensitivity to immunotherapies in REST-less breast cancer with the potential to inform treatment strategy for other REST- less tumor types.

application_id: 11125749
activity_code: F31
title: Lysosome and integrated stress response in aging and alcohol-associated liver disease
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF KANSAS MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Alcohol-associated liver disease (ALD) is a leading cause of liver failure with mortality rates projected to
continuously increase over the next few years. As the average lifespan increases, the prevalence of chronic
liver diseases like ALD increases especially in the older adult and elderly population. The proposed studies will
help to elucidate the underlying mechanisms in which aging and alcohol consumption collaboratively influence
and exacerbate the development of ALD by assessing the roles of TFEB-mediated autophagy and the
involvement of the integrated stress response (ISR) mediated by ATF3 activation.

org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: Project Narrative
Genetic variation underlies many human health conditions, and genomic data is an integral part of medicine,

application_id: 11230686
activity_code: K08
title: Elucidating the Mechanisms that Underlie Tau Aggregation and Neurotoxicity
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: Project Narrative
Alzheimer’s disease and other tauopathies are characterized by the toxic misfolding and accumulation of tau
protein aggregates in vulnerable neuron types. This project aims to identify the molecular pathways that lead to
the misfolding and accumulation of aggregated tau across these different neurodegenerative diseases. This
work will provide important insight into the underlying cause of these neurodegenerative disorders and may
identify novel therapeutic targets to slow or prevent this process.

title: Improving Thoracic Surgical Care using electronic Patient-Reported Outcomes (ePROs)
org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: Project Narrative Recovery from thoracic surgery (used to treat common chronic lung conditions) is associated with serious complications which may be prevented if more effective early detection and management strategies existed. Based on evidence from improved outcomes in oncology, electronic systems for monitoring patient-reported outcomes (ePROs), which are symptoms reported directly from the patient, are one potential solution. This application proposes to bridge the gap in knowledge on the optimal ePRO system design, how to use ePROs in surgery, and the benefits of ePROs in surgery using rigorous implementation science approaches.

application_id: 11063205
activity_code: K99
title: Epigenetic and genetic regulation of arsenic methylation and arsenic-relatedcardiovascular disease risk
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: NARRATIVE
Arsenic exposure is associated with increased risk of cardiovascular disease, and this relationship may be
modified by arsenic methylation, a biological pathway that decreases arsenic toxicity and increases excretion of
arsenic in urine. This study will identify pre- and post-transcriptional gene regulatory factors involved in arsenic
methylation efficiency and arsenic-related cardiovascular disease risk among Native American adults. Findings
will provide insight to the pathways that link arsenic to complex health outcomes, support the development of
biomarkers of arsenic-related cardiovascular disease, and help target public health interventions.

phr: Project Narrative The role of RB1 loss in bladder cancer progression, differentiation and cancer cell plasticity remains unknown. A major obstacle in translational bladder cancer research is the lack of genetically engineered mouse models that recapitulate aggressive muscle invasive bladder cancer in humans, that makes it difficult to rigorously study in vivo responses to systemic treatment. title: Improving the equity of RCTs among patients with acute and chronic respiratory failure
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: NARRATIVE Randomized clinical trials of interventions to prevent and treat acute and chronic respiratory failure are the bedrock of evidence-based practice. However, there are several threats to the equity of such trials, including failures to enroll participants with similar characteristics to persons to whom the interventions would be applied, and the possibility that commonly used scoring systems for risk adjustment within such trials would perform inequitably across groups defined by race, ethnicity, or sex. This mid-career investigator award in patient- oriented research will take essential steps toward surmounting these challenges in the context of a multifaceted training program that will make the Principal Investigator a more effective mentor and team leader.

application_id: 10933450
activity_code: F32
title: Investigation of Anterior Cingulate Cortex Contributions to Hippocampal Cognitive Control
org_name: NEW YORK UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Cognitive control is believed to primarily depend on prefrontal brain regions (such as the anterior cingulate cortex) which exert top-down executive function during sensory conflict to properly guide behavior. However, the best characterization of the neural representation of cognitive control implicates the hippocampus, a hub of memory and navigation. By evaluating the activity and influence of cingulate cortex on the hippocampus during a cognitive control task, we will generate insights into the neural basis of cognitive control to bridge an essential gap that persists between human and animal research in executive function, which will be essential for future investigations into mental health disorders that impact executive function and decision making.

phr: Project Narrative Mild traumatic brain injury, or concussion, gives rise to cognitive deficits, chiefly inattention, that overlap the phenotype of comorbid attention-deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) during adolescence. title: Targeting hyper-reactive cysteines within PPI interfaces
org_name: UT SOUTHWESTERN MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Project Narrative
Utilizing advances in human interactome structural characterization and covalent inhibitor development against
reactive cysteines, this project proposes Artificial Intelligence methods to identify hyperreactive cysteines on
protein-protein interaction (PPI) interfaces and explore covalent targeting of these cysteines with electrophilic
molecules. The insights gained will open new possibilities for developing covalent inhibitors to modulate PPIs
and potentially treat various human diseases.

title: Exa-scale tissue readout methods
org_name: ALLEN INSTITUTE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The field of spatial biology is transforming the landscape of scientific discovery, improving our ability to probe
the mechanisms that underly disease states and the response of diseased cells and tissues to treatments. It is
critical to extend these methods to bigger and bolder scientific questions, yet we currently lack imaging tools that
are fast and efficient enough to support the scale that these experiments will demand. To address this need, this
project will develop a pair of Exa-scale Tissue Readout Methods (ExTReMe).

title: Developing Selective P-catalysts for Dehydrative Transformations
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE This proposal will determine the various steric and electronic factors in designing stereoselective phosphorous catalysts for enantioselective acylations and tandem functionalizations. application_id: 11140273
activity_code: F31
title: Understanding the role of altered lipid metabolism in CD8 T cell exhaustion within the tumor microenvironment
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
A major barrier to the success of immune checkpoint inhibitor therapies is CD8 T cell exhaustion, which is a state
characterized by reduced tumor killing and metabolic insufficiency. Our lab and others have identified
mitochondrial stress as a key driver of CD8 T cell dysfunction, yet the impact of mitochondrial stress on
connected metabolic pathways remains understudied. This proposal aims to examine the role of altered lipid
metabolism in exhausted T cells, which will provide mechanistic insight into the metabolic underpinnings of T cell
exhaustion.

application_id: 11134151
activity_code: K99
title: Predicting craving and relapse using neurobehavioral markers of goals and habits in opioid use disorder
org_name: ICAHN SCHOOL OF MEDICINE AT MOUNT SINAI
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Opioid use related deaths continue to climb, imposing a devastating toll on individual and public health, and
necessitating the identification of neuroscience-based biomarkers of severity and recovery. This project will
apply naturalistic, longitudinal, and computational tools to study motivational control (i.e., balance between
goals and habits), which putatively underlies drug addiction, during inpatient opioid use disorder treatment to
predict prospective craving and relapse. Using these cutting-edge neuroscience-based tools, this project has
the potential to identify key biomarkers of craving and relapse in opioid addiction, ultimately to help curb the
impact of the opioid epidemic in our communities.

application_id: 10802105
activity_code: F31
title: REGULATION OF BONE MARROW MESENCHYMAL STEM CELLS BY VCAM1
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ILLINOIS AT CHICAGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Bone marrow (BM) mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) are rare non-hematopoietic perivascular stromal cells which regulate hematopoietic stem cell (HSC) function by secreting high levels of niche factors such as Vascular Cell Adhesion Molecule-1 (VCAM1). While the specific role of MSC-derived VCAM1 on HSC and MSC maintenance remains unknown, our supporting data indicates that MSCs are the BM’s main source of Vcam1 and suggests that VCAM1 is critical for the maintenance, survival, and function of MSCs. Here, we will use rigorous genetic models, and technically innovative experimental approaches to elucidate the mechanisms by which VCAM1 promotes MSC survival and regulates HSC and progenitor function.

application_id: 11136222
activity_code: F31
title: Quantifying the contribution of supracellular actomyosin networks during mouse neural tube closure
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 Supracellular actomyosin networks are used to fold a diverse range of tissues during development, like
neural tube closure. Errors during neural tube closure cause congenital malformations in humans, but we lack
a complete understanding on how actomyosin networks contribute to neural tube closure. We propose test a
mechanistic model of mammalian neural tube closure which will provide insight on the generalizability of
actomyosin-mediated folding mechanisms and create a conceptual foundation from which to understand,
prevent, and treat congenital malformations.

application_id: 11014985
activity_code: K23
title: Determining Mechanisms of Pain Reduction in Chronic Widespread Pain After Rapid Weight Loss in Non-Hispanic Black and Hispanic/Latino/a/x Adults
org_name: NEW YORK UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Chronic widespread pain (CWP) is an obesity-related disparity that disproportionately burdens non-Hispanic Black and Hispanic/Latino/a/x adults with obesity. Bariatric surgery is often prescribed for obesity and musculoskeletal pain comorbidities, but there is significant intra-group variability in pain improvement. The proposed work will identify key driver(s) of pain resolution or maintenance after bariatric surgery that could serve as targets for culturally informed, multimodal pain interventions for adults with obesity.

application_id: 11143114
activity_code: R25
title: Translational Research Activities in Neuropsychiatry (TRAIN) - Renewal - 1
org_name: UT SOUTHWESTERN MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Advances in the fields of neuroscience, genetics, and other biological fields are leading to profound
transformations in our understanding of mental health. In order to translate this understanding into new and
optimized treatment approaches, research training should encompass a solid foundation in translational
science. Consistent with the Research Domain Criteria (RDoC) initiative and emerging work on the use of
biomarkers to guide prediction of disease development and treatment response, our Translational Research
Activities In Neuropsychiatry (TRAIN) program is designed to train the next generation of physician-scientists in
psychiatry so that they can translate neuroscience and genetics research into better outcomes for individuals
with mental illness.

application_id: 10802109
activity_code: F31
title: Elucidating the role of nonessential amino acid metabolism in diabetic skin wounds
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA-IRVINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Diabetes can lead to complications like diabetic foot ulcers, which are nonhealing and may lead to morbidity and death. How small metabolites are utilized in normal wounds and altered in diabetic wounds remains understudied. By investigating metabolite utilization spatiotemporally and by host cells, this study aims to understand how alterations in metabolic usage contribute to delayed wound healing in diabetes.

application_id: 11140289
activity_code: F31
title: Epigenetic regulation of keratinocyte function in normal and pathologic skin repair
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 Non-healing diabetic wounds are the most common cause of amputation in the United States with a 5-
year mortality of nearly 50% and annual cost to the healthcare system of $19 billion. Our preliminary data
demonstrate that epigenetic changes in diabetic keratinocytes, driven by JAK/STAT/SETDB2, regulate the
activity of inflammatory cytokine production (i.e., TNFα). Increased production of TNFα by diabetic keratinocytes
then functions to impair keratinocyte migration and further promotes inflammation by promoting a pro-
inflammatory macrophage phenotype. The success of the human and animal studies outlined in this proposal
may lead to improved understanding of the pathophysiology of non-healing diabetic wounds and test novel
therapeutic targets and allow me to develop as ...

application_id: 10870084
activity_code: F30
title: The role of microglia as antigen presenting cells in Globoid Cell Leukodystrophy
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CONNECTICUT SCH OF MED/DNT
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Globoid cell leukodystrophy (GLD) is a genetic disease characterized by severe neuroinflammation and demyelination, leading to a very early death in most affected individuals. Excitingly, we have discovered that T-cells are present in diseased brain tissue of GLD and they appear to be engaged with a highly activated glial cell type called microglia which we think underlies the aggressive nature of this disease and its pathology. The goal of this project will be to define how these microglial cells function as professional immune cells to foster the pathological activity of T cells and drive brain injury in this untreatable disease.

application_id: 11095083
activity_code: T32
title: Michigan Predoctoral Training in Molecular Biophysics
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The Michigan Molecular Biophysics (MiMoB) training program leverages the depth and experience of highly motivated biophysicists to train students in the fundamental biophysical principles underlying biological processes, the use of cutting-edge techniques, and rigorous quantitative analysis of biomolecular systems directly related to human health and disease. Trainees will be equipped with professional skills that will help them function as highly effective members of interdisciplinary research teams and responsible members of the broader scientific community.

application_id: 11005722
activity_code: K08
title: Elucidating the role and mechanisms by which collagenase producing intestinal bacteria promote colorectal cancer recurrence and metastasis following surgery.
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CHICAGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE With little understanding as to its pathogenesis, colorectal cancer recurrence occurs in up to 40% of patients undergoing surgery, and when it does, it is almost universally fatal. This proposal is focused on studying a novel hypothesis in cancer recurrence and defining the role of intestinal microbes to influence cancer cells towards an invasive phenotype promoting tumor formation. These studies will provide transformative insights into the dynamic interaction between host-microbe-cancer that can be leveraged to provide novel interventions and therapeutics to prevent primary cancer and its recurrence.

application_id: 11139479
activity_code: K01
title: Sexual network structure and HIV testing and treatment in sub-Saharan Africa: understanding the implications for ending the HIV epidemic.
org_name: PENNSYLVANIA STATE UNIV HERSHEY MED CTR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Despite clear evidence at the couple-level that HIV test-and-treat strategies are successful at reducing HIV
transmission, population-level trials have shown mixed effectiveness of test-and-treat strategies to reduce HIV
incidence. Through network analyses and simulations, contextualized with community interviews, this career
development award will assess whether sexual network clustering impacts the effectiveness of HIV treatment
as prevention in Malawi, Uganda, and Nigeria, to help us better understand situations where the population-
level impact of test-and-treat strategies are successful and where they are not. These results will inform
population-level treatment targets and will allow tests of the utility of network-driven strategies to drive testing
and treatment, ...

application_id: 11031755
activity_code: K99
title: The effect of aging on cDC1 function and CD8 T cell immunity in tumors
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Aging increases the risk to cancer and weakens immune responses, including CD8+ T cell immunity and
immunotherapy efficacy in elderly cancer patients. Here, we will dissect the impact of aging on conventional type
1 dendritic cell (cDC1) function and CD8+ T cell immunity in tumors by employing single-cell multiomics
techniques, functional assays, innovative spatial imaging/transcriptomic approaches, and mouse melanoma
models with human melanoma biopsies. The study aims to advance our understanding of cDC1-CD8+ T cell
communication in aged tumors, providing insights to enhance cDC1 function and explore tailored approaches
for older melanoma patients.

application_id: 11015785
activity_code: F31
title: Molecular evolution of synthetic probes for orphan GPCRs
org_name: BOSTON UNIVERSITY (CHARLES RIVER CAMPUS)
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  GPCRs are the largest class of membrane proteins in humans and have been linked with many cardiovascular disorders. While GPCRs make up a third of all FDA-approved drugs, including for example widely prescribed anti-hypertensives, many potentially druggable GPCRs remain intractable because they lack known ligands to modulate their activity. Here, I propose a platform to systematically generate probes to modulate understudied GPCRs, which would serve as a steppingstone to study them and develop new therapeutic agents.

title: Determining the Role of Brain-Derived CCN3 in Bone and Marrow Fat Metabolism

application_id: 11244483
activity_code: F31
title: Investigating the role of CELSRs, a family of adhesion-type GPCRs, in hippocampal neural circuit assembly
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY
phr: Narrative
Synapses have the innate capability of assembling into neural circuits that mediate nearly all
behavior, yet their mechanism of action remains unclear. Emerging evidence has shown adhesion
class GPCRs to play a key role in these processes, and their mutations have been implicated in
neurological disorders such as autism and ADHD. Due to the cadherin adhesion GPCR, CELSR,
being shown to play a role in invertebrate neuronal migration, dendritic growth, and axon guidance
this project employs the use of cKO CELSR mice to uncover its unknown role in these
fundamental processes and its relation to neurological dysfunction in mammals.

activity_code: P20
title:  BCM/TSU P20  Collaborative Cancer Research Education Program (C-REP)
org_name: TEXAS SOUTHERN UNIVERSITY
phr: CURED C-REP Project Narrative 
The CURED C-REP will provide an 8-weeks mentored cancer research experience that integrates Baylor College of Medicine (BCM) and Texas Southern University (TSU) infrastructures every summer followed by year- long outreach education activities that will increase the awareness, knowledge, and understanding of research, concepts, processes, and careers in cancer research/cancer health outcomes in a cohort of 20 TSU students. application_id: 11244587
activity_code: F31
title: Investigating HLA-I as a driver of P. falciparum antigenic diversity
org_name: HARVARD UNIVERSITY D/B/A HARVARD SCHOOL OF PUBLIC HEALTH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Populations of the malaria parasite Plasmodium falciparum harbor remarkable diversity in antigens crucial for
vaccine-induced and natural immunity. This proposal investigates the hypothesis that some of this diversity is
selected to evade a heritable element of the adaptive immune response: HLA-I presentation. Our findings will
demonstrate the extent to which parasite variation may enable escape of sterilizing cell-mediated immunity to
key parasite antigens, providing crucial insights for vaccine development.

application_id: 11140334
activity_code: K23
title: Development and assessment of an HIV disclosure intervention for men
org_name: BRIGHAM AND WOMEN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Men consistently lag behind women along each step of the HIV care cascade and contribute to HIV transmission among women. HIV disclosure is key to HIV prevention; however, few evidence-based resources exist to support HIV disclosure among men with HIV. I propose to develop and pilot test an HIV disclosure intervention to support men with HIV with symptoms of sexually transmitted infections who are either not accessing antiretroviral therapy or are accessing antiretroviral therapy but without viral suppression, with the goal of engaging men in care and decreasing partner HIV transmission.

application_id: 11176333
activity_code: K99
title: Esophageal squamous cell cancer initiation and immune landscape remodeling
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF TX MD ANDERSON CAN CTR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Despite the importance of early diagnosis, genetic and molecular mechanisms of esophageal squamous cell
cancer (ESCC) initiation remain elusive. The proposed research will elucidate the dual role, both autonomous
and non-autonomous, of preneoplastic cells in driving ESCC development, which is expected to establish a
novel foundation to develop new types of ESCC detection and treatment. Therefore, this study is related to
public health and to the part of NIH’s mission that pertains to fostering fundamental creative discoveries and
innovative research strategies as a basis for ultimately protecting health.

application_id: 11171359
activity_code: K99
title: Unraveling the molecular link between heavy metal exposure and triple-negative breast cancer
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF LOUISVILLE
phr: Public Health Relevance Statement 
Disruption of DNA double-strand break (DSB) repair promotes triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC). The overarching goal of this study is to determine the molecular mechanisms responsible for disruption of DSB repair elicited by cadmium (Cd) exposure to promote TNBC. These studies will elucidate a fundamental mechanism not currently recognized that can explain TNBC arising in individuals without a genetic predisposition.

application_id: 11059887
activity_code: K23
title: Biobehavioral self-regulatory processes underlying real-time binge eating symptoms and obesity risk
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF SOUTHERN CALIFORNIA
phr: Project Narrative
Obesity is associated with increased risk for chronic diseases and mortality. Binge eating and binge eating
disorder are characterized by self-regulation problems and are strongly related to excess weight; thus, binge
eating represents an important modifiable behavior to target in obesity prevention and intervention efforts. The
proposed project will use a multimethod approach to examine affect, attention bias towards food, and
impulsivity in a sample of adults at risk for obesity, with the goal of elucidating how self-regulatory processes
predict momentary binge eating and short-term weight change.

application_id: 11033663
activity_code: K23
title: Methadone Loading Dose Initiation for Hospitalized Patients with Opioid Use Disorder Using Fentanyl
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
General medical and surgical hospitalizations for persons with opioid use disorder (OUD) and fentanyl use are
common, however patients often leave the hospital before completing treatment and without linkage to OUD
care. This proposal seeks to take advantage of methadone's long half-life to develop a “loading dose”
approach to starting methadone treatment for OUD and fentanyl use via a dose-finding study and a hospital-
based randomized pilot trial. Findings will yield crucial insights into our understanding of methadone treatment,
OUD with fentanyl use, and the design of hospital-based SUD trials, and could establish a new dosing
paradigm for methadone treatment.

Men in the United States have an exceptionally high prevalence of overweight and obesity, but are dramatically
underrepresented in weight loss programs because they find them unappealing. This project evaluates
engagement in and efficacy of a novel, game-based mobile weight loss program designed to be appealing to

basic brain capacities needed to resist impulses to eat tempting foods.

title: The role of Huntingtin in endolysosomal trafficking and Huntington's disease pathogenesis
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF TEXAS HLTH SCI CTR HOUSTON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Endolysosomal trafficking is a major cellular process that keeps aging neurons healthy by controlling whether cellular contents are properly recycled or degraded. Defective endolysosomal trafficking is a converging mechanism in neurodegenerative diseases including Huntington's disease, a fatal disorder caused by an autosomal-dominant mutation in the Huntingtin gene. The goal of this proposed study is to elucidate the precise mechanism and function of Huntingtin in endolysosomal trafficking in a physiologic organism to identify new molecular targets for treating Huntington’s disease.

title: Resistance Exercise in the Prevention and Treatment of Heart Failure with Preserved Ejection Fraction (HFpEF)
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MINNESOTA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Exercise-based prevention and treatment strategies for heart failure with preserved ejection fraction (HFpEF)
have historically focused on the promotion of aerobic exercise. However, given the coinciding epidemics of
physical inactivity and obesity in the U.S., and the compromised aerobic capacity and exercise intolerance in
patients with HFpEF, long-term adherence to aerobic exercise is generally poor and thus, the prevention and
treatment of HFpEF remains a major public health challenge. To help alleviate this public health burden, the
proposed project will be the first to examine the isolated role of resistance exercise—a more movement-varied,
non-weight-bearing exercise modality that may be better tolerated by obesity-burdened, aerobically-
compromised populations—in ...

title: Determining Tropism and Mechanisms of Ebola Virus Entry in Placental Tissues
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF IOWA
phr: Project Narrative During pregnancy, Ebola Virus Disease (EVD) caused by infection with Zaire Ebola virus (EBOV), leads to ~100% loss of the fetus or neonate with or without the additional loss of the mother. This project will begin the process of understanding the pathogenesis of EBOV during pregnancy by determining the route and mechanisms of infection in placental tissues. This data from this project will add to the knowledge regarding viral infections in placental tissues and the barrier role of the placenta as well as the development of potential therapeutics or treatment protocols to improve the maternal and fetal outcomes of EVD.

title: Cadherin complexes recruit PIWIL2 to suppress transposons and maintain the normal epithelial phenotype
org_name: MEDICAL UNIVERSITY OF SOUTH CAROLINA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Re-activation and integration of transposable elements (TEs) into a new genomic site can introduce mutations to regulatory or protein coding regions, which has been implicated in a variety of gastrointestinal (GI) diseases such as Crohn’s, colitis, Lynch syndrome, and cancer. However, the reasons for the increased activity of these elements in the GI epithelium still unclear. Our study has the potential to elucidate a previously underappreciated link between normal tissue architecture and transposon regulation, which may be critical for our understanding of the loss of epithelial integrity in GI diseases and could lead to the future development of novel therapeutics.

title: Elucidating Immune and Microglial Dynamics in Alzheimer's Disease with Advanced Organoid Models.
org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: Parkinson’s disease (PD) is a common neurodegenerative condition with major impacts on quality of life, no disease-modifying therapy, and mechanistic links to the toxic accumulation of a particular protein. Mesenchymal stromal cells (MSCs) release molecules that help combat disease states, with the specific molecules released influenced by their environment and surrounding pathology. By comparing what MSCs release when in conditions simulating PD with what they secrete in normal conditions, we aim to identify molecules that could be used to therapeutically target the abnormally accumulating protein in Parkinson’s disease..

title: Probing the Rules of Protein Structure-Function Relationships through the De Novo Design of Functional Proteins
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: Project Narrative The design of proteins with functions not yet seen in nature is a fundamental goal in protein science, with many translational applications. title: The Role of GCN2 in Ponatinib-Induced Cardiotoxicity
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ILLINOIS AT CHICAGO
phr: Ponatinib is the tyrosine kinase inhibitor with the most cardiotoxicity used in the treatment of chronic myeloid
leukemia, and the molecular mechanisms underlying its cardiac toxicity remain unclear. This research aims to
delineate novel molecular mechanisms through which the GCN2-mediated integrated stress response regulates
ponatinib-induced cardiotoxicity, and to contribute to the discovery of therapeutic interventions that could reduce
ponatinib’s cardiac toxicity while preserving its anti-cancer effectiveness. This research will provide new insights
into alleviating the cardiotoxicity induced by cancer treatment.

activity_code: K23
title: Targeting Network Dysfunction in Apathy of Late-life Depression Using Digital Therapeutics
org_name: ADVENTHEALTH ORLANDO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The proposed study will integrate a customized cognitive intervention with rigorous sample population phenotyping to target a distinct and prominent subgroup of apathetic depressed older adults at high risk of poor clinical and functional outcomes. In this K23 application, we will examine engagement of salience processing and executive control networks with digital cognitive training in older adults with apathy of late-life depression. This will provide preliminary data regarding brain network targets of a digital cognitive training program that may translate to mood and cognitive benefits, and may guide future development of non-pharmacologic treatments for apathy in older adulthood.

title: Understanding Glucocorticoid Toxicity: The Impact of Glucocorticoid Regimens on Toxicities, Clinical Outcomes, and Quality-of-Life in Vasculitis
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Glucocorticoids (e.g., prednisone) remain a cornerstone of therapy for many rheumatic diseases but can cause
numerous infectious, metabolic, neuropsychiatric, and other toxicities. Thus far, most toxicities (e.g., diabetes,
osteoporosis) have been studied individually, though the Glucocorticoid Toxicity Index (GTI) is a recently
developed, standardized and validated instrument that assesses and quantifies composite toxicity. This K23
proposal focuses on utilizing advanced epidemiologic methods and simulation modeling to assess the impact
of different glucocorticoid treatment regimens on glucocorticoid toxicity, quality-of-life associated with different
combinations of toxicity, and clinical outcomes and cost-effectiveness of different glucocorticoid treatment
regimens in ...

activity_code: T32
title: IMSD@UNTHSC
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF NORTH TEXAS HLTH SCI CTR
phr: Narrative As the Nation becomes increasingly diverse, we must include all individuals, particularly those historically underrepresented in STEM and biomedical sciences if we are to advance biomedicine for our citizenry. The overarching goal of the IMSD@UNTHSC is to support the timely completion of Ph.D. degrees by underrepresented groups and their transition into successful biomedical careers. The project has two main objectives: Objective 1) Recruit a competitive pool of underrepresented students that complete a Ph.D. and continue into biomedical research careers and Objective 2) To ensure that at least 80% of Ph.D. students will complete the Ph.D. degree at or in less time than 5.2 years within the median of 4.88 to 5.73 years for all biomedical sciences.

activity_code: K23
title: Reducing Polypharmacy and Fall Risk for Multi-Morbid Adults with Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project narrative Although the majority of patients with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) take fall-risk increasing drugs (FRIDs) and falls are a significant source of morbidity and mortality for patients, there are to date no interventions that tailor and target deprescribing efforts for this population. This K23 application will adapt and test a novel pharmacist-led deprescribing intervention for patients with COPD, using qualitative data from patients, family caregivers and prescribers to guide the adaptation, delivery, and evaluation of a pharmacist-led deprescribing intervention. This research will provide data on how to reduce medication-related harm to improve quality of life and reduce medication-associated morbidity and mortality for patients with COPD.

application_id: 10766706
activity_code: F31
title: The Experience and Regulation of Posttraumatic Shame among Women with Histories of Interpersonal Trauma
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF KENTUCKY
phr: The proposed project will address critical gaps in current knowledge regarding the experience and management of posttraumatic shame – an emotion consistently implicated in the development, maintenance, and/or worsening of PTSD symptoms among women with a history of sexual or physical assault or abuse (i.e., interpersonal trauma). Existing treatments for PTSD often fail to fully address posttraumatic shame, and untreated shame represents an ongoing risk factor for the development or worsening of PTSD symptoms. Findings from this study hold promise for improving treatment efforts for shame and PTSD by clarifying aspects of how women experience and manage shame after interpersonal trauma.

application_id: 11230534
activity_code: K08
title: Improving mutant KV11.1 trafficking to treat Long QT Syndrome
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Project Narrative
Loss-of-function variants in the voltage-gated potassium ion channel (KV11.1) are associated with Long QT
Syndrome, a risk for deadly cardiac arrhythmias, and around 90% of these variants disrupt intracellular protein
transport (trafficking) to the cell surface. I will test evacetrapib’s efficacy to improve KV11.1 variant trafficking
using human induced pluripotent stem cells and employ proteomics to identify KV11.1 protein interaction
networks. This research aims to uncover therapeutic strategies for managing Long QT Syndrome.

application_id: 10782516
activity_code: F31
title: Genetic, molecular, and neural mechanisms of alcohol-induced effects on sleep
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF UTAH
phr: –––– Project Narrative ––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––– Disrupted sleep is one of the most universally reported outcomes of active alcohol use and recovery from alcohol use disorder, and the harmful influence of alcohol on sleep is well-characterized but remains difficult to treat. To develop better therapies for alcohol-induced sleep effects, we must understand the genetic and molecular mechanisms underpinning the relationship between alcohol use and poor sleep. Utilizing Drosophila’s behavioral similarities with humans, many genetic tools, and high economy of scale, this work will identify genes and neural circuits regulating alcohol-induced sleep disturbances, thereby offering mechanistic targets for treating these disruptions.

application_id: 11228837
activity_code: K99
title: Contributions of itch-sensing neurons to skin anti-helminth immunity
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The goal of this project is relevant to public health because it will define the contributions of
functionally distinct itch-inducing sensory neurons to skin immunity against parasitic helminths
that affect one-fourth of the global population. The molecular pathways studied in this proposal
will inform therapeutic strategies against chronic itch and skin infection with the blood fluke
Schistosoma mansoni. The project is relevant to the NIAID’s mission of preventing infectious,
immunologic, and allergic diseases by identifying the contributions of discrete itch-inducing
neurons to cutaneous host-protective responses.

application_id: 11138768
activity_code: K99
title: Using Single-cell and Spatial Multi-omics to Improve Adoptive Cell Therapy against Pancreatic Cancer
org_name: FRED HUTCHINSON CANCER CENTER
phr: Project Narrative
Adoptive T cell therapy (ACT) has had limited success against pancreatic cancer, because of the
T-cell intrinsic epigenomic programs and the tumor microenvironment (TME) suppressive factors
that limit their anti-tumor functional status. Using single-cell and spatial omics, the proposed
research will identify strategies that improve the anti-tumor activities of ACT T cells in TME. The
proposed project will clarify the fundamental knowledge of the ACT for pancreatic cancer and will
ultimately lead to more effective therapy for pancreatic cancer patients.

application_id: 11214635
activity_code: K99
title: Uncovering the Impact of Air Pollution Sources on Autism Spectrum Disorder: A Metabolomic Approach to Identifying Critical Biological Pathways
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA LOS ANGELES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
This project investigates the biological pathways linking air pollution exposure, including traffic and wildfire
sources, to Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) using data from large population-based cohorts spanning 20
years and advanced metabolomics techniques from 2014 to 2019. By focusing on analyzing biomarkers in
neonatal blood spots, this study will identify disrupted metabolic pathways associated with key air pollution
sources. These findings will provide critical insights for developing targeted public health interventions to
reduce the rising ASD risk linked to environmental exposures.

title: UNC Integrated Translational Oncology Program (UNC-iTOP)
org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: Narrative The UNC Integrated Translational Oncology Program (UNC-iTOP) is a T32 training application from the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill (UNC) focused on training clinicians (MDs), basic scientists (PhDs), and MD-PhD candidates in clinical/translational disease based research at the interface of clinical oncology and cancer biology using patient samples. The UNC-iTOP is unique in its focus on developing clinician/scholars in translational oncology tissue-based research, integrating 1) physicians (residents and fellows) training in Medical, Surgical, and Gynecologic Oncology with post-doctoral fellows possessing PhD degrees and 2) MD, MD-PhD, PhD pre-doctoral candidates. UNC-iTOP will provide research training support for up to two years and will support two post-doctoral a...

application_id: 11019702
activity_code: F31
title: Investigating the Mucin Utilization Capabilities of Akkermansia muciniphila
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: Currently, much of the genetic systems of Akkermansia muciniphila remain unknown, despite the growing interest surrounding the use of this microbe in therapeutics. By utilizing newly developed genetic tools, combined with several in vitro assays, this proposal will elucidate the genetic mechanisms that underlie mucin degradation, the fundamental host-bacterial interaction that this commensal microbe undertakes. The knowledge gained from this study will enhance the knowledge of A. muciniphila and shed light onto the causes of its many purported effects.

application_id: 10924385
activity_code: K01
title: BIOLOGICAL AGING AND COGNITIVE IMPAIRMENT IN PERSONS WITH HIV
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE A substantial proportion of people with HIV experience cognitive impairment, despite effective multi-drug regimens targeting viral replication. This study will use DNA methylation profiling, magnetic resonance imaging, and machine learning methodology to investigate whether HIV infection and resulting cognitive impairment are associated with accelerated biological aging both in the epigenome and in the structure of the brain. This approach will help to clarify the mechanisms underlying cognitive impairment in people with HIV and may lead to better prognosis and clinical care for at-risk individuals.

title: Pathways to enrolling diverse Latinos in autopsy studies: Insights from a largelongitudinal study
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE In this day and age, brain autopsy is still regarded as the gold standard and only a definite diagnostic tool for ADRDs however, available brain tissue in the US largely originates from non-Latino Whites. phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Screening for hypertension in children is an important strategy for the prevention of future cardiovascular
disease. However, office blood pressure measurements are unreliable in children; thus, children with high blood
pressure at the primary care office are often referred to the subspecialist to perform ambulatory blood pressure
monitoring outside the clinic, resulting in delays in care, loss to follow-up, and clinical inertia. Given the rising
prevalence of pediatric hypertension and limited access to ambulatory blood pressure monitoring, the objective
of this proposal is to evaluate a multicomponent strategy to improve the guideline-recommended use of
ambulatory blood pressure monitoring in the pediatric primary care setting, which has the potential to empower

phr: Project Narrative Despite effective HIV viral control and longer life expectancies due to the success of combined Antiretroviral Therapy (ART), persons living with HIV continue to suffer from neurological complications including Post- traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) and HIV-associated Neurocognitive Disorders (HAND). application_id: 11032914
activity_code: K01
title: Circadian Regulation of Cardiomyocyte Size and Function
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF KENTUCKY
phr: Project Narrative The results of this study will establish human induced pluripotent stem cell–derived cardiomyocytes (hiPSC-CM) as a model to obtain critical new information on how the intrinsic cardiomyocyte clock impacts cell size and function. These data will also inform our knowledge of whether inherited genetic mutations that lead to hypertrophic cardiomyopathy resulting in increased cardiomyocyte size and contractile dysfunction can disrupt circadian rhythms. The impact of this study is that it will provide a mechanistic understanding of the interplay between the circadian clock and its regulation of cardiomyocyte size and function while also answering the question of whether the circadian clock in the heart is altered during hypertrophic states.

application_id: 10884285
activity_code: K08
title: Loss of the Exocrine Pancreas Improves Glucose Tolerance and Insulin Secretion
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: Diminished first-phase insulin secretion is the early and detrimental defect in type 2 diabetes. The preliminary data show an increase in first-phase insulin secretion and improved glucose tolerance to supranormal levels following exocrine pancreas ablation. This proposal aims to understand the dramatic increase in the first-phase insulin secretion following the exocrine tissue ablation, test the effect of exocrine pancreas loss in mice models of hyperglycemia, and identify the underlying causes of the improved insulin secretion that accompany loss of the exocrine tissue.

title: Intrinsic Ribosomal Decoding Center Methylation and the Bacterial Antibiotic Response
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: NARRATIVE
The increasing ability of bacteria to evade the effects of antibiotics is a significant global health crisis. This project
will investigate how RNA modifying enzymes place their chemical modifications on a key bacterial antibiotic
target (the ribosome) to alter antibiotic susceptibility and resistance. These studies will increase our
understanding of how such RNA modifications influence the bacterial antibiotic response and could support
future development of new therapeutic strategies to help counter the threat of bacterial antibiotic resistance.

title: Mechanisms of Renal Lipotoxicity
org_name: BROAD INSTITUTE, INC.
phr: The proposed research will provide insights into the molecular responses of kidney cells to saturated lipid induced injury, specifically, the mechanism by which deletion of Fas-associated factor 2 (FAF2) is protective against lipid induced cellular toxicity, known as lipotoxicity. Elucidating the molecular mechanisms of lipotoxicity in the kidney may contribute to the development of therapeutic strategies for diseases lacking treatment options.

activity_code: F32
title: Real-world Effectiveness and Utilization of Glucagon-Like Peptide-1 Receptor Agonists for Treatment of Type 2 Diabetes in Adolescents and Young Adults
org_name: CHILDREN'S HOSP OF PHILADELPHIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Type 2 diabetes (T2D) occurring in adolescents and young adults aged less than 40 years is associated with worse health outcomes. Glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonists (GLP-1 RAs) are a relatively new T2D treatment, but how well they work in adolescents and young adults outside of clinical trials is unknown. The purpose of this study is to use a large-scale database to test how GLP-1 RAs are prescribed and how well they work in adolescents and young adults in the real world.

activity_code: F31
title: Defining the Role of Aire in eTACs and its Contribution to Peripheral Immune Tolerance
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Autoimmune Regulator (Aire) has been demonstrated to be essential for central tolerance in the thymus by inducing tissue specific antigen expression in medullary thymic epithelial cells (mTECs), but little is known about the role of extrathymic Aire in peripheral immune tolerance. Since extrathymic Aire-expressing cells (eTACs) are transcriptionally and functionally similar to both mTECs and migratory dendritic cells, they could play a unique role in the maintenance of immune homeostasis as distinct populations of tolerogenic antigen presenting cells. Studying the biology of eTACs and peripheral Aire will help further elucidate the function of Aire and define basic peripheral tolerance mechanisms, which may have significance for a range of clinical applications from autoi...

application_id: 11021085
activity_code: K23
title: Prescription opioids, cognitive decline, and Alzheimer's disease in older adults
org_name: MAYO CLINIC ROCHESTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE This research evaluates the relationships between prescription opioid use, cognitive outcomes, and changes in brain morphometry in large population-based cohort of community-dwelling older adults, while also assessing their perceptions and experiences regarding the impact of opioids on pain and cognition. Opioid use is common in older adults (prevalence of approximately 10% in those >65 years of age) and may represent a modifiable risk factor for cognitive impairment and dementia. In this research, we will utilize a mixed methods approach, including prospectively-obtained epidemiological and neuroimaging data, and qualitative methodologies to assess the relationships between opioid use and cognition in older adults.

application_id: 10824719
activity_code: F31
title: Elucidating the Role of AIRE and Tumor Associated Aire-expressing Cells in Tumor Growth and Immune Evasion
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE AIRE is required for maintaining tolerance in the thymus and periphery to prevent autoimmune diseases but can also play a detrimental role by dampening immune responses against cancer. While deletion of thymic AIRE expression has been shown to improve response to immunotherapy in certain cancers by allowing the development of autoreactive T cells, whether extrathymic AIRE expression is involved in regulating anti-tumor immune responses remains unknown. This proposal investigates the role of peripheral AIRE expression and tumor associated Aire-expressing cells in tumor progression and immunity with the ultimate goal of improving immunotherapy approaches for cancer treatment.

application_id: 11138205
activity_code: K08
title: Artificial Intelligence for Thoracic Aortic Disease Screening and Gene Discovery
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF TEXAS HLTH SCI CTR HOUSTON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Thoracic aortic dissections, a leading cause of premature death particularly in those with heritable thoracic aortic
disease (HTAD), are entirely preventable if at-risk individuals are identified early and undergo surgical repair.
Artificial intelligence (AI), including machine learning and deep learning approaches, represents an ideal method
for identifying these individuals and gaining insights into the largely undefined genetic basis of aortic disease. In
this proposal, I aim to (1) develop an AI-based facial feature screening tool by leveraging images from the
Montalcino Aortic Consortium patient registry, and (2) integrate machine learning techniques and evolutionary
principles to identify novel genetic drivers of HTAD using genomic-scale data from diverse cohorts.

title: DEVELOPMENTAL REGULATION OF FIBROBLAST GROWTH FACTOR-19 IN NEONATES
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Preterm infants have immature gastrointestinal function, small bile acid pools, and adrenal insufficiency that limit their development and response to enteral nutrition. Fibroblast growth factor 19 (FGF19) is a potent fetal growth factor that is upregulated postnatally and functions to regulate bile acid homeostasis. In this proposal, we seek to determine the impact of perinatal glucocorticoids and FGF19 on gastrointestinal, hepatic and metabolic outcomes in preterm pigs.

application_id: 10806311
activity_code: K99
title: Revealing the Functional and Microstructural Response of Human Hippocampus to Spatial Learning with High-Gradient Strength Diffusion MRI
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Studies of brain neuroplasticity during learning and memory have been so far restricted to animal research. A significant reason has been the lack of non-invasive imaging technology to probe neuroplasticity with high biological specificity. This project aims to develop and validate a non-invasive marker of neuroplasticity during learning in the human hippocampus subfields of living humans with the latest ultra-high gradient strength diffusion Magnetic Resonance Imaging technology. The marker can be used to detect early signs of pathological remodeling of the hippocampus in Alzheimer’s disease or assess the efficacy of invasive tools for memory function repair.

application_id: 11240985
activity_code: F32
title: Investigating Neisseria gonorrhoeae chromosomal replication dynamics
org_name: NORTHWESTERN UNIVERSITY AT CHICAGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
We will define the minimal sequences required for Neisseria gonorrhoeae chromosomal
replication and determine how multiple chromosomal copies are maintained. Through genetic
approaches and mutagenesis experiments, I will define the origin of replication (oriC) and the role
of the replication initiator protein DnaA on chromosomal copy number. This study will advance
our understanding of gonococcal genome organization and replication control, potentially
revealing new targets for treating gonorrhea.

activity_code: F30
title: Nox4 and Epigenetics in chronically enhanced cardiac protein O-GlcNAcylation
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ALABAMA AT BIRMINGHAM
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Heart failure is a widespread and severe clinical outcome of poorly treated diabetes mellitus, and preliminary work has defined an association between diabetic heart disease, DNA methylation, and protein O- GlcNAcylation—(molecular marks implicated in the regulation of gene expression and protein function). However, while the possible downstream effector, Nox4, is upregulated upon long-term increased cardiac protein O-GlcNAc, the regulatory mechanism of Nox4 expression is understudied. This proposal aims to determine Nox4 regulation by DNA methylation, and Nox4’s necessity in the observed cardiac dysfunction during chronic enhanced O-GlcNAc.

title: Breadth and mechanism of Wolbachia-based inhibition of fungal infection in Drosophila

phr: Project Narrative: Wolbachia bacteria of arthropods are key to several global initiatives such as the World Mosquito Program in controlling pests and the transmission of arboviruses such as dengue. title: Robotic and handheld OCT imaging for diagnosis and treatment of diabetic retinopathy
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  This proposal seeks to advance the state of the art in automated image guidance methods with optical coherence tomography (OCT) to improve diagnosis and treatment of diabetic retinopathy. title: Identifying Determinants of Borrelia burgdorferi and Peromyscus leucopus symbiosis
org_name: TUFTS UNIVERSITY BOSTON
phr: Project Narrative
Lyme disease places a substantial health (affecting >450,000 US citizens per year) and financial (costing
Americans >$1 billion per year) burden on the United States. This project seeks to understand how the causal
agent of Lyme disease, Borrelia burgdorferi, is able to survive, propagate, and spread in nature. Understanding
the symbiotic relationship between B. burgdorferi and its natural rodent reservoir could reveal strategies to
substantially reduce or even eradicate the pathogen from the wild, which would prevent spread of the bacteria
to humans and the development of acute and/or chronic Lyme disease.

application_id: 10807354
activity_code: K99
title: Single cell multi-omics approaches in identifying driving cells and genes in pediatric MODS patients requiring ECMO support
org_name: HENRY FORD HEALTH + MICHIGAN STATE UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Multiple organ dysfunction syndromes (MODS) frequently occur in pediatric patients and severe MODS patients needed Veno-Arterial extracorporeal membrane oxygenation support (ECMO) (label as ECMO patients) as a palliating maneuver to recover their cardiopulmonary function.
